statement: These recommendations apply to asymptomatic primary prevention and not to people with chronic illness, known vitamin deficiencies, or malabsorption conditions such as celiac disease.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: High-dose vitamins can cause harm: high-dose vitamin D can be related to kidney stones, and large doses of vitamin A can cause headaches and liver problems.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: The 2022 recommendations align with the 2014 recommendations despite 52 new studies (84 total, over 700,000 people), reflecting variability rather than lack of evidence.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: Clinicians should ensure vitamin use does not distract from higher-value interventions; statins reduce cardiovascular outcomes by roughly 30%, far exceeding any possible small benefit from vitamins.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Confirm office hypertension with out-of-office readings; Dr. Cohen recommends averaging two readings in the morning and two in the evening over three days (at least six values) before making decisions.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Masked hypertension (normotensive in office but hypertensive at home) is common and not benign; it warrants aggressive risk factor modification. Classic patients are men who smoke tobacco, with alcohol use, impaired glucose tolerance, and central obesity as risk factors.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: ARBs match ACE inhibitors for nephroprotection and systolic heart failure but have fewer adverse effects (cough, angioedema, even GI bleed), so ARBs can be favored when initiating therapy.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Losartan does not last a full 24 hours and is ideally dosed twice daily; for suboptimal control, use 25 mg twice daily rather than increasing to 50 mg once daily.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Olmesartan is a long-acting ARB preferred by Dr. Cohen and available in combination with amlodipine; be aware it can cause olmesartan-induced enteropathy mimicking celiac disease.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Starting two antihypertensive agents at low dose (quarter or half maximum) gives good control with fewer side effects; most patients need two medications, and maxing out one agent yields diminishing returns and more side effects.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg is nearly a homeopathic dose for many patients; 25 mg is a more appropriate starting dose for the majority.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Chlorthalidone is roughly twice as potent as hydrochlorothiazide (25 mg are not equivalent) and carries a risk of hypokalemia requiring close electrolyte monitoring.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Contrary to older teaching, the CLICK trial (Agarwal et al., 2021) showed chlorthalidone effectively lowered blood pressure (~10 points) in patients with CKD, even those already on a loop diuretic.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Torsemide 20 mg is a preferred loop diuretic because it is longer acting and can be dosed once daily, unlike bumetanide or furosemide which are dosed twice daily.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are appropriate in CKD; expect a creatinine rise of up to 30%, which is acceptable, but a rise of ~50% should prompt consideration of bilateral renal artery stenosis.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: In CKD patients on ACE/ARB, a potassium up to 5.5 is tolerated; Dr. Cohen does not panic until potassium exceeds 5.5.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Cardiorenal syndrome is driven more by venous congestion (venous back pressure on the kidney) than poor forward flow, since cardiac index is often normal; this supports diuretic therapy as treatment.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: In acute decompensated heart failure, permissive hypercreatinemia is expected during diuresis and should not automatically stop therapy; the creatinine bump in the DOSE trial was associated with better outcomes.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: If a heart failure patient is not hypotensive, do not necessarily stop the ACE, ARB, or SGLT2 inhibitor for a slight creatinine bump; if stopped, resume them on discharge as these are important long-term.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Assess diuretic response promptly: IV loop diuretics should produce urine within ~30 minutes and oral within 1-2 hours if the threshold dose is met; if not making urine, increase the dose rather than just the frequency.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: If a patient does not respond to 40 mg furosemide, giving 40 mg twice daily will not work; you must reach the threshold dose to produce urine, then increase frequency.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: The benefit of a furosemide drip is achieving higher diuretic doses that are hard to reach with intermittent dosing, not anything magical about continuous delivery.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: For persistent diuretic resistance, use sequential nephron blockade by adding a thiazide, acetazolamide, or an MRA like spironolactone to block a different part of the nephron.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Bumetanide has high, consistent oral bioavailability whereas furosemide oral bioavailability ranges widely (~10-90%); bumetanide may be a better oral choice in patients with gut edema, and its levels are less GFR-dependent.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: An elevated troponin in CKD should not be dismissed; it is a poor prognostic marker even if not immediately actionable, useful for risk stratification.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: For BNP in CKD patients, where baseline levels may be chronically elevated, look at the relative change compared to prior values rather than the absolute number.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: When taking a palpitations history, ask the patient to tap out the strength and rhythm of what they feel on the back of their hand to better characterize the arrhythmia.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Tachyarrhythmias like SVT tend to have abrupt onset, whereas palpitations from an anxiety disorder tend to have a slow build.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Red flag palpitation symptoms include chest pain and lightheadedness, but syncope is most worrisome as it may represent a lethal arrhythmia and warrants urgent workup.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Women are probably underdiagnosed with SVTs and tachyarrhythmias, often incorrectly attributed to anxiety; endorsing anxiety does not complete the workup.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: In the palpitations workup, obtain an EKG (abnormalities may reveal rhythm disturbance or structural heart disease), plus a CBC, metabolic panel/electrolytes, and a TSH.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Ask about alcohol and other substances; a recent article found coffee was not associated with tachyarrhythmias whereas alcohol was, so blanket coffee avoidance is unnecessary unless it is a clear individual trigger.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: For ambulatory rhythm monitoring, about two weeks is enough for most patients; use an implantable loop recorder for more sporadic or worrisome symptoms such as syncope.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Match monitor duration to symptom frequency; monitoring for 48 hours when symptoms occur only once a month wastes time and money and misses the event.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: For SVTs (AVNRT/AVRT), first-line supportive care includes vagal maneuvers such as an ice pack on the face, which triggers the diving reflex.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: For persistent SVT symptoms, medication with beta blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers can help; Dr. Cooper favors the calcium channel blockers due to beta blocker side effects.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: "Pill-in-the-pocket" medications for SVT have an onset of about 20 minutes, so for short-lived episodes the arrhythmia may resolve before the drug takes effect.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Catheter ablation for SVT is both diagnostic and therapeutic, with an efficacy rate of about 95%.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Frequent PVCs (more than 10% of heartbeats) can cause cardiomyopathy and long-term damage, and should be managed with medication or catheter ablation and referral to electrophysiology.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: In erectile dysfunction, whether dysfunction occurs during partnered versus unpartnered sexual activity helps differentiate performance anxiety from an organic cause.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Per AUA guidelines, symptoms of ED may precede a cardiovascular event by up to five years, and in younger men ED predicts a marked (up to 50-fold) increase in future cardiac events, so these patients warrant CVD risk factor reduction.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Weight loss is effective for ED partly because adipose tissue contains aromatase that converts testosterone to estrogen; even 10% weight loss can improve sexual performance.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: PDE5 inhibitors do not cause priapism (that risk is with injectables), and they require sexual arousal to work rather than causing an immediate erection.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Daily tadalafil 5 mg reaches steady state and can treat both BPH and ED; for those without BPH, on-demand tadalafil 10-20 mg acts as a "weekend pill."

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: To improve affordability of PDE5 inhibitors, prescribe the largest tablet (e.g., 100 mg sildenafil) for patients to split, or use prescription discount websites to obtain many tabs for under $50/month.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Male enhancement supplements should generally be discouraged since some contain undisclosed sildenafil; L-arginine has at least some supporting evidence if a patient wants a nutraceutical.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Mirabegron, a beta-3 agonist, may have pro-erection effects and could be a choice for men who have both ED and overactive bladder.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Injectable ED medications such as papaverine avoid the systemic effects of PDE5 inhibitors but carry a higher risk of priapism, warranting proactive counseling.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: In patients with osteoarthritis of the knee, physical therapy resulted in better one-year WOMAC scores than glucocorticoid injection, with a significant difference of about 19 points.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: Benefit of both physical therapy and steroid injection for knee OA persisted at one year, contrasting with prior studies that showed only short-term effects.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: The 2019 ACR guidelines give a strong recommendation for intra-articular steroid injections based on short-term efficacy, and recommend against manual therapy for knee OA.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: In the LoDoCo2 trial, colchicine was associated with an increased incidence of non-cardiovascular death (hazard ratio 1.51), which the authors could not explain.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: The LoDoCo2 trial excluded patients with moderate to severe CKD, severe congestive heart failure, valvular heart disease, or known colchicine intolerance.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: In the gout flare trial, only about two thirds of patients were pain free at day seven and only 75% were pain free at four weeks, suggesting acute gout symptoms can linger.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: Updated ACR gout guidelines allow steroids, NSAIDs, or colchicine for acute flares, chosen based on what the patient can tolerate.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: A common colchicine flare regimen is 1.2 mg followed by 0.6 mg a couple hours later; steroids are an alternative if there is no contraindication.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: An older gout trial found low-dose colchicine (1.8 mg/day) worked as well as high-dose (4.8 mg/day) with fewer GI side effects.

episode_title: #255 Hotcakes: Colchicine for CAD, and Gout flares, PT versus Steroids for Knee OA
statement: In the colchicine gout trial, the colchicine group had more diarrhea and more headache than the naproxen group.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: When a patient presents with syncope, reframe it as the broader category of transient loss of consciousness to avoid prematurely pigeonholing the differential.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: Any patient with transient loss of consciousness should get a point-of-care glucose.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: Causes of syncope can be remembered by the mnemonic CORE: Cardiogenic, Orthostatic, and REflex (vasovagal).

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: Cardiogenic syncope, the most frightening cause, can be broken down into rhythm problems and pump (structural) problems.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: Sarcoidosis has a strong propensity to cause conduction system disease, making it a key concern in a syncope patient with a history of sarcoid.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: The main reason to obtain an echocardiogram in syncope is to assess for reduced EF, which can correlate with ventricular tachycardia and VF; other structural causes include aortic stenosis, PE, pulmonary hypertension, and HOCM.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: In a patient with syncope and a markedly prolonged QTc, empiric magnesium (2 g) is reasonable because the benefit for possible QT-induced torsades is high even before a diagnosis is confirmed.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: Ventricular tachycardia can be conceptualized like seizures: monomorphic VT (originating from one scar) versus polymorphic VT (whole heart quivering); this distinction is fundamental to prognosis and underlying cause.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: Long-standing ischemia creates a scar that serves as a nidus for monomorphic VT, whereas a long QTc affects every cell and produces polymorphic VT.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: A patient who walks into the hospital with documented VT from a loop recorder almost certainly has monomorphic VT, because polymorphic VT generates essentially no cardiac output and is incompatible with life.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: After excluding ischemia, sarcoidosis is the leading cause of VT in the United States, while Chagas disease is an increasingly common cause worldwide.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: On cardiac MRI with gadolinium, delayed enhancement in a coronary distribution suggests CAD/ischemia, whereas patchy delayed enhancement prioritizes an infiltrative process such as amyloid or sarcoid.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: The absence of epicardial coronary disease on catheterization does not rule out ischemia, especially in women (e.g., microvascular disease).

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: Sarcoidosis is a diagnosis of exclusion arrived at after ruling out other causes of granulomatous disease (fungal, mycobacterial infections, lymphoma, rheumatologic causes)—it is not simply a biopsy diagnosis.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: The four most important causes of an arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy to know in the US are hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (formerly ARVD), sarcoidosis, and amyloidosis.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: In cardiac sarcoidosis, patients can have serious arrhythmic events despite an EF that is not markedly reduced (e.g., 45%), a disconnect worth recognizing since guideline ICD indications are based on markedly low EF (<35%).

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: Discontinuing long-term suppressive prednisone may allow a previously controlled granulomatous process like sarcoid to resurface.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: When a patient goes truly from zero to 100 (a transient event with no residual scar on exam), the two possibilities are vasovagal syncope or a malignant arrhythmia.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: In evaluating syncope, clarify the time course of the illness—a decline in exercise tolerance may reveal a smoldering process, since going truly from zero to 100 is rare.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: When verifying a patient's methadone dose from an OTP, the two most important pieces of information are the milligram dose and when they last received a dose.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: A patient walking in to newly start methadone would generally receive 30 to 50 mg regardless, which supports giving methadone even when the prior dose cannot be confirmed.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: Absence of methadone on a state PMP does not exclude that a patient is on methadone, since many state PMPs do not include methadone records.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: Older guidance recommends dose-reducing methadone by ~50% after missing more than ~3 days and essentially restarting after more than 5 missed days, but this is being reconsidered.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: In patients who missed methadone doses but continue non-prescribed opioid use and lack risk factors (older age, concurrent benzodiazepine use), it is reasonable to be more aggressive in maintaining or only slightly reducing the methadone dose.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: In the hospital you can split and titrate methadone since you can give more but not take back a dose; assess response about 4 hours later at methadone's peak before giving more.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: For a QTC hovering around 500 in a patient in withdrawal, first repeat the EKG, correct potassium and magnesium, and remove non-essential QT-prolonging medications before altering methadone.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: For a low-500s QTc in a symptomatic withdrawing patient, give methadone with benefit of the doubt, consider splitting to twice daily, recheck EKGs, and fix reversible causes before changing therapy.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: Other full opioid agonists can be used to manage withdrawal in the hospital but cannot be prescribed outpatient for opioid use disorder, so they are temporizing measures.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: For medetomidine withdrawal, start oral clonidine early (often 0.1-0.2 mg every 6 hours standing) and uptitrate rapidly, with doses reaching as high as 0.6 mg every 6 hours, potentially adding scheduled guanfacine.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: For medetomidine withdrawal nausea, ondansetron works poorly; prochlorperazine or olanzapine are preferred, and promethazine (with more histaminergic activity) may also help.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: Guanfacine for medetomidine withdrawal is dosed starting at 1 mg three times daily up to 2 mg three times daily, and tizanidine can be added, especially when blood pressure is soft.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: Medetomidine withdrawal presentation is highly variable; many patients using an adulterated supply do fine with standard opioid-withdrawal doses of clonidine, so start lower and reassess rather than maximally dosing everyone.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: The worst of medetomidine acute withdrawal typically resolves within about 72 hours, and dexmedetomidine infusions typically run about 48-72 hours.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: Where medetomidine is pervasive in the fentanyl supply, medetomidine test strips are of limited value, but they may help in regions where its presence is more variable.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: The 2026 ACC/AHA lipid guideline pushes most patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease to an LDL goal of less than 55 mg/dL and a non-HDL cholesterol goal of less than 85 mg/dL.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Non-HDL cholesterol equals total cholesterol minus HDL, and the non-HDL goal is set approximately 30 points higher than the LDL goal.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: ASCVD is defined in the guidelines as acute coronary syndrome, MI, angina, arterial revascularization, stroke, TIA, or peripheral arterial disease.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: New LDL targets of less than 55 and non-HDL less than 85 are difficult to reach even on high-dose statin, typically requiring statin plus ezetimibe or statin plus PCSK9 inhibitor, with bempedoic acid or inclisiran as additional options.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: A coronary artery calcium score can reclassify a patient's risk; a score over 100 falls in the moderately high category and should prompt more aggressive lipid management.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Coronary artery calcium scoring typically costs between $75 and $300 out of pocket and is often not fully covered by insurance.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Ordering LP(a) with 'elevated LP little A' as the indication is usually covered by most insurances, and apoB can often be ordered alongside a routine lipid panel.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Daraxonrasib is a KRAS inhibitor that covers multiple KRAS mutations and also inhibits wild-type KRAS, and is taken as a once-daily oral pill.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: In a phase 3 trial of previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer, daraxonrasib nearly doubled overall survival compared to physician's choice chemotherapy, an approximately 6 to 6.5 month difference.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Skin toxicities are the most notable side effect of KRAS inhibitors like daraxonrasib; they are on-target effects and may correlate with response to therapy.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Standard-of-care chemotherapy for metastatic pancreatic cancer includes taxanes and FOLFIRINOX-type regimens.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: At high semaglutide doses (25 mg and up oral, or 2.4 mg and up injectable), dysesthesia occurs, and at the 7.2 mg dose almost one in five patients reported it.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: In the Oculus randomized trial, continuing GLP-1 or GLP-1/GIP agonists before upper endoscopy significantly increased residual gastric volume compared to skipping a dose, but produced no unplanned aspiration events, intubations, or hospitalizations.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Requiring a clear liquid diet the day before (as when endoscopy is combined with colonoscopy) appeared to abolish the difference in residual gastric contents between continuing and holding the GLP-1 agonist.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: In an open-label study of adults aged 60 or older on stable low-dose levothyroxine with TSH under 10, over a quarter successfully discontinued the medication at one year while maintaining TSH under 10 and free T4 within range and no worsening quality of life.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Levothyroxine de-prescribing success rates were higher at lower doses, with over 63% of patients taking 50 micrograms successfully discontinuing at one year.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: The levothyroxine de-prescribing study excluded patients on doses above 150 micrograms, those with prior thyroidectomy, radioactive iodine treatment, or congenital hypothyroidism, as these conditions require permanent replacement.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Excess thyroid hormone replacement, especially in older adults, carries real consequences including harm to bones and heart and increased arrhythmia risk, so there should be a high threshold to start levothyroxine for vague symptoms with a mildly elevated TSH.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: When attempting levothyroxine de-prescribing, closely follow the patient's symptoms and check TSH every 6 to 8 weeks.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The USPSTF recommends screening for only a small number of cancers: lung (in prior heavy smokers), colon (starting age 45), breast, and cervical cancer, with a guarded recommendation for prostate cancer.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The cancers with proven mortality benefit from screening are those the USPSTF has recommended.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Multi-cancer early detection tests should be viewed as an addition to standard-of-care screening, not a replacement.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The main concern with multi-cancer early detection tests is false positives, which lead to unnecessary imaging and procedures.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: In Grail's Pathfinder 2 study in the US, 30% of positive signals from the Galleri test were false positives.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The Grail (Galleri) test is based on methylated DNA, providing a methylation pattern and a tissue of origin that indicates where to look.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Pancreatic cancer is a poor shedder of DNA, and even in metastatic disease analyzable blood material is often not detectable, making it poorly suited to these blood-based assays.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The only blood-based cancer detection test reimbursed by insurance is Shield from Exact Sciences; the others are self-pay.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The positivity rate of multi-cancer early detection tests is about 1 to 2%, meaning 1 to 2 per 100 people will have a positive signal.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: A key limitation is overdiagnosis, since detecting indolent thyroid cancer, low-risk prostate cancer, or indolent lymphoma may not help the patient.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Whole body MRI is not recommended for average-risk asymptomatic patients, and there is no evidence it improves detection of important cancers or mortality outside hereditary cancer syndromes.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Whole body MRIs done at direct-to-consumer centers are often lower quality (thicker slices, lesser read quality), and academic centers typically repeat the MRI to obtain the best quality imaging.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: For a positive multi-cancer signal without a tissue of origin, the recommended approach is a whole body PET CT, though some tumors are not FDG avid or too small to register.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: A negative multi-cancer early detection test does not rule out cancer, and concerned patients should be advised to repeat testing (e.g., in a year).

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Most experts in the field consider a stage shift (fewer advanced-stage cancers) a sufficient endpoint, since mortality endpoints would take so long the technology may be obsolete before data mature.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Patients who elect non-traditional screening tests should be counseled that the tests aren't perfect, false positives can lead to unnecessary procedures, radiation exposure, and significant anxiety.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: In the CHAMPION AF trial, left atrial appendage occlusion was non-inferior to oral anticoagulants for major adverse cardiac events at 3 years and superior for bleeding, but there were actually more strokes in the device group.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: In non-inferiority trials, a large non-inferiority margin (here 4.8% absolute increase in stroke) and substantial crossover both bias toward concluding non-inferiority, so results should be interpreted cautiously.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: Patients with left atrial appendage occlusion devices are not free of antithrombotic therapy; they typically need dual antiplatelet or anticoagulant plus aspirin for the first 45 days and lifelong aspirin thereafter.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: For patients on oral anticoagulants without a contraindication asking about a Watchman device, the oral anticoagulant remains the better treatment for stroke prevention.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: In the COBRA trial, apixaban had lower clinically relevant bleeding than rivaroxaban when treating VTE for 3 months (3.3% vs 7.1%), an absolute risk reduction of about 4% and number needed to treat of about 25, with similar VTE recurrence (~1%).

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: Most of the excess bleeding with rivaroxaban in VTE treatment appears to accrue early during the 21-day high-intensity twice-daily loading period, whereas apixaban's higher-dose lead-in lasts only about 7 days.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: Current professional society guidelines recommend DOACs over vitamin K antagonists for VTE, but with no preference stated among the DOACs.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: In the EASY-PAVE trial, targeting LDL cholesterol of 55 mg/dL versus 70 mg/dL in patients with known atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease significantly lowered cardiovascular events at 3 years.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: The overall body of evidence, including IMPROVE-IT and PCSK9 trials, supports that lower LDL improves outcomes regardless of the agent used to achieve it.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: In a VA cohort study, GLP-1 receptor agonists compared with SGLT2 inhibitors were associated with lower risks of new substance use disorders (roughly 6-7 fewer per 1000 users over 3 years) and fewer serious adverse outcomes in those with existing SUD (about 12 fewer per 1000, including 2 fewer deaths).

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: An active comparator new user design (comparing GLP-1s to SGLT2 inhibitors in diabetics needing another agent) helps overcome selection bias by identifying patients with similar treatment indications.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: Prespecified falsification (negative control) endpoints help assess residual confounding: if outcomes not plausibly linked to the drug (e.g., BPH) show no spurious association, it increases confidence in the true associations found.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: Despite robust pharmacoepidemiologic methods, the GLP-1 and substance use disorder findings remain observational and require RCTs to confirm benefit beyond standard addiction therapy and in patients without diabetes.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: The paper linking e-cigarettes to oral and lung cancer was a narrative/qualitative risk assessment based on biomarkers and animal studies, not evidence that definitively demonstrates vaping causes cancer in humans.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: Current Cochrane evidence suggests a net benefit to replacing combustible tobacco with vapes, so patients should not abandon vaping and return to smoking based on the qualitative cancer review.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: A vaping history should include how many puffs and the nicotine content, since some cartridges hold thousands of puffs and users may consume more nicotine than with cigarettes.

episode_title: #523 Hotcakes: Left Atrial Appendage Closure vs AC for Afib, Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban for VTE, Intensive LDL Targeting, GLP1s and Substance Use Disorders, and more
statement: In a quasi-experimental hospital study, drawing morning labs at 6:00 a.m. instead of 4:00 a.m. improved subjective sleep quality and gave patients about one additional hour of sleep (7 vs 6 hours).

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: Retatrutide is a triple agonist (GLP-1, GIP, and glucagon) studied in the TRIUMPH phase three trials, with reported weight loss of nearly 30-35% in some patients.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: The SURPASS cardiovascular outcomes trial (NEJM, Dec 2025) compared tirzepatide versus dulaglutide in patients with type 2 diabetes and known cardiovascular disease and showed non-inferiority, likely leading to a MACE-reduction indication.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: Real-world discontinuation of GLP-1 agents is about 50% within 12 months, far higher than the ~20% dropout seen in trials over a 4-year median follow-up, highlighting a know-do gap.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: For coverage, straight-up obesity is the hardest indication; type 2 diabetes is easiest, while comorbidities like moderate-to-severe sleep apnea (tirzepatide) or cardiovascular risk reduction (semaglutide) can serve as bargaining chips.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: After stopping weight-loss medications, patients regain on average about 0.4 kg per month, and up to 0.8 kg per month for incretin mimetics like semaglutide and tirzepatide, returning to baseline weight around 1.7 years after discontinuation.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: Weight-loss medications should be framed as chronic therapies like antihypertensives; when stopped, patients regain weight and cardiometabolic parameters (blood pressure, A1C, cholesterol) worsen back toward baseline.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: The VALAD trial (JAMA) of valacyclovir 4 g/day versus placebo in early Alzheimer's or MCI with positive biomarkers was negative; the valacyclovir group had clinically meaningful worse cognitive worsening (between-group ADAS-Cog difference just under 4, where 3 points is clinically meaningful).

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: Valacyclovir does not clear latent virus and can cause neurotoxicity, especially at higher doses over longer periods in elderly patients, which may explain the observed cognitive worsening.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: Behavioral-science nudges in the EHR (precommitment and boostering) increased the likelihood of a deprescribing event for high-risk medications (benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, Z-drugs) by 40% and 26% respectively versus usual care.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: Deprescribing trials consistently show medications can be removed but have not clearly demonstrated that doing so reduces hospitalization, improves quality of life, or reduces harm from pill burden.

episode_title: #517 DIGEST: Weight regain after weight loss medications, GLP1/GIP comparisons, valacyclovir for Alzheimer’s, and nudges to encourage deprescribing
statement: Deprescribing nudges may result in substitution with other potentially inappropriate sedating medications, so removing a benzodiazepine alone may not represent a true win.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: Oral semaglutide is now approved for obesity treatment, with the trial using a 25 mg dose showing about 13.6% weight loss versus 2.2% with placebo.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: For weight loss, oral semaglutide is dosed as 1.5 mg daily for month one, 4 mg for month two, 9 mg for month three, and 25 mg maintenance from month four onward; diabetes dosing differs (3 mg, then 7 mg, then 14 mg).

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: GI side effects are the main limiting factor with oral semaglutide, with nausea in nearly half and vomiting in about a third of patients; injectable semaglutide reports about 25% vomiting.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: Dysesthesia is a dose-dependent side effect of GLP-1 agonists, occurring in about 5% of patients on the 2.4 mg injectable or 25 mg oral semaglutide dose, and up to 13-20% at very high doses.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: In the PISCES trial, hemodialysis patients given 4 g daily fish oil had about 40% lower cardiovascular event rates than placebo (hazard ratio 0.57).

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: Statin therapy is not even recommended to be initiated in end-stage renal disease patients on hemodialysis due to lack of efficacy, despite their very high cardiovascular mortality (about 20 times the general population).

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: The PISCES fish oil dose was 4 g daily providing 1.6 g EPA and 0.8 g DHA, far higher and higher-quality than typical over-the-counter fish oil supplements, so patients should not expect equivalent benefit from CVS-brand products.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: A retrospective study suggesting IV iron during acute infection improved survival is likely explained by immortal time bias, since patients had to survive long enough to receive IV iron to be included, so it should not change practice.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: Systematic reviews show a modestly increased risk of infection associated with IV iron administration, which underlies concern about giving IV iron during active infection.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: The updated US dietary guidelines recommend healthy adults target 1.2 to 1.6 g/kg/day of protein, higher than the prior 0.8 g/kg recommended daily allowance, though this is not supported by the accompanying scientific report.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: The new dietary guidelines controversially list butter and beef tallow as alternatives to olive oil, despite evidence that butter raises LDL and increases cardiovascular mortality compared with plant oils.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: Current AHA/ACC guidelines give a class one recommendation to use the CHA2DS2-VASc score to guide anticoagulation after AF ablation (anticoagulate if CHA2DS2-VASc 2+ in men or 3+ in women), but this is based on extrapolated data from non-ablated patients.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: In the ALONE-AF trial, discontinuing anticoagulation one year after successful AF ablation (no recurrence, mean CHA2DS2-VASc ~2.1) led to lower adverse event rates than continuing, driven mainly by reduced bleeding, with very low stroke rates.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: Patients randomized to discontinue anticoagulation in ALONE-AF had at least two Holter monitors (24-72 hours) between ablation and randomization to rule out silent or subclinical atrial fibrillation.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: The OCEAN trial (rivaroxaban vs aspirin ~1 year after successful AF ablation) was negative for the composite outcome, complementing ALONE-AF in showing very low stroke/embolism event rates in this population.

episode_title: #514 Hotcakes: Oral Semaglutide, Fish Oil in ESRD, IV Iron During Infection, New US Dietary Guidelines, & Anticoagulation after Ablation in AFib
statement: Observed stroke rates after successful AF ablation appear lower (about or under 1% per year) than the CHA2DS2-VASc score predicts (~2.2% for a score of 2), suggesting the score may not apply well to patients remaining in sinus rhythm post-ablation.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Functional impairment is what differentiates mild cognitive impairment (MCI) from mild/early stage dementia; objective cognitive impairment with preserved independence suggests MCI, while impairment affecting activities indicates dementia.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: In a typical amnestic presentation of Alzheimer's, focus on delayed recall (three words on MMSE, five words on MoCA) rather than isolated deficits like being one day off on the date.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Plasma biomarkers and FDA-approved blood tests for Alzheimer's are only indicated in people with objective evidence of cognitive impairment (MCI or dementia), not for the worried well or asymptomatic patients.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Plasma biomarkers are screening tests, not standalone diagnostics; they are the initial step and not the end of the diagnostic road.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: For amyloid beta 42 to 40 ratio, low levels in the blood (like in CSF) are indicative of amyloid plaques in the brain.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: P-tau 217 is currently the most favored phosphorylated tau form, being more sensitive and specific than p-tau 181, and elevated levels are highly indicative of Alzheimer's disease pathology including brain amyloid.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Neurofilament light chain is less specific for Alzheimer's, tends to be positive more in frontotemporal dementia, and can help distinguish FTD when p-tau/amyloid is normal but NfL is elevated.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: APOE4 is a risk gene, not an autosomal dominant gene; having one or two alleles increases risk but does not guarantee developing Alzheimer's, and the absolute risk still isn't that high.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: P-tau 217 has a sensitivity and specificity of about 88%, making it a good test that may not require additional beta amyloid ratios.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Interpret biomarkers cautiously in older patients, since about 30 to 40% of cognitively normal adults at age 80 will have a positive amyloid PET scan; ensure high clinical suspicion before testing.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Candidacy for anti-amyloid therapy requires confirmed Alzheimer's (amyloid PET or CSF) and disease stage of MCI or early stage Alzheimer's; moderate stage patients are not candidates.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: APOE4 genotyping is required before anti-amyloid therapy; the FDA label carries a black box warning for APOE4 homozygotes, and many institutions treat homozygosity as an absolute contraindication due to much higher ARIA risk.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: For lecanemab specifically, subgroup analysis of APOE4 homozygotes showed a greater risk of harm than benefit.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: MRI findings that exclude anti-amyloid therapy include more than four microhemorrhages, superficial siderosis, prior hemorrhage over one centimeter, or other cerebrovascular abnormalities; an updated MRI within 6-12 months is needed before treatment.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: ARIA (amyloid related imaging abnormality) includes ARIA-E (edema) and ARIA-H (hemorrhage); ARIA-E is the more concerning outcome, and most ARIA is asymptomatic with only about 3 to 6% of patients having symptoms.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Overall ARIA risk for lecanemab was about 22%, while for donanemab at original doses it was about 36%; a new lower modified donanemab dosing reduces ARIA-E risk while hemorrhage risk stays about the same.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: ARIA risk is much higher in APOE4 homozygotes (about 40%) and lower in non-carriers; most ARIA occurs in the first couple of months, so MRI monitoring is clustered up front with four or five MRIs on a schedule.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Patients on anti-amyloid therapy cannot receive TPA if they have a stroke; consider a medical ID bracelet, since deaths have occurred with ARIA in the context of TPA administration.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Anti-amyloid therapies effectively remove amyloid plaque but do not stop or reverse disease; they slow cognitive and functional decline roughly 25 to 30% over 18 months, translating to being about 5 to 7 months behind an untreated patient.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: ARIA may not be visible on a CT scan, so patients on anti-amyloid therapy with new neurologic symptoms (headache, dizziness, cognitive change, or even a fall) need an MRI performed promptly.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Infusion reactions occur in about 20% of patients on anti-amyloid therapy and can be managed with premedication such as low-dose dexamethasone, acetaminophen, and diphenhydramine.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: Cholinesterase inhibitors like donepezil can be used alongside anti-amyloid therapies in early stage dementia; many clinicians prefer to establish tolerance of the anti-amyloid agent first before adding donepezil.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: For lecanemab, current recommendations support maintenance treatment (monthly infusion or weekly subcutaneous dosing) until the patient reaches moderate stage, whereas donanemab trials stopped treatment once amyloid was cleared on repeat PET.

episode_title: #511 Alzheimer’s Disease: Updates in Diagnosis and Treatment
statement: One FDA-approved blood test is approved for ruling out Alzheimer's in primary care, but false positives can occur, leading to indeterminate results that require confirmatory amyloid PET.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: In the ERSPC prostate cancer screening trial with 23-year follow-up, prostate cancer mortality was 13% lower in the screening group, corresponding to an absolute risk reduction of about 0.22%.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: PSA screening in the ERSPC yielded a number needed to screen of about 456 to prevent one prostate cancer death, while diagnosing roughly 30% more men with prostate cancer, and saving one death for every 12 men diagnosed.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: Current guidelines recommend a shared decision-making model for PSA prostate cancer screening rather than universal screening or no screening.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: In a large Swedish study, a crude analysis found a small increased risk of autism with in-utero acetaminophen exposure (hazard ratio 1.05), but this association was completely attenuated in a sibling-controlled analysis designed to control for genetic and environmental confounders.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: In observational cohort studies, associations with a hazard or odds ratio less than two should generally be viewed skeptically given the amount of noise and confounding.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: Studies of acetaminophen and autism are confounded by indication, since untreated maternal fever itself is thought to increase risk of neurodevelopmental disorders, making it hard to separate the drug from the reason it was taken.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: Acetaminophen remains preferable to alternatives like NSAIDs and aspirin in pregnancy, which have clear harms acetaminophen does not, and maternal fever, especially prolonged and untreated, carries its own risks.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: A South Korean cohort study reporting increased cancer risk after COVID-19 vaccination is best explained by detection/healthy-user bias: the six cancers found more common (breast, gastric, prostate, colon, thyroid, lung) are all commonly screened for in Korea, so vaccinated people were more likely to see a doctor and get screened.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: Receipt of the COVID-19 vaccine should not change your decision to screen a patient for cancer; screen them as you otherwise would.

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: In the AQUATIC trial, adding aspirin 100 mg daily to oral anticoagulation in high-risk chronic coronary syndrome patients who had undergone PCI more than six months prior was stopped early for harm, with about a 50% relative increase in major adverse cardiovascular events and a tripling of bleeding risk.
topic: Aspirin plus anticoagulation in coronary disease

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: The AQUATIC trial randomized 872 chronic coronary syndrome patients on long-term oral anticoagulation to aspirin 100 mg daily versus placebo added to their anticoagulation.
topic: Aspirin plus anticoagulation in coronary disease

episode_title: #506 DIGEST-Hotcakes: PSA Screening, Acetaminophen and Autism, COVID19 Vaccines and Cancer, Aspirin and Anticoagulation in Coronary Artery Disease
statement: The takeaway from AQUATIC is that combining an oral anticoagulant with aspirin in these patients causes more harm than good, favoring DOAC monotherapy.
topic: Aspirin plus anticoagulation in coronary disease

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: A fall is defined simply as coming to rest unintentionally at a lower surface, and every fall deserves a workup regardless of whether syncope was involved.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Avoid the term 'mechanical fall'; it is used inconsistently and often leads clinicians to prematurely stop working up the fall.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: The 5Ms framework (multimorbidity, medications, mind, mobility, matters most) is a useful way to dissect geriatric syndromes like falls when a classic organ-based approach fails.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: About a quarter of older adults fall every year, and having one fall is the biggest risk factor to predict future falls.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Having had one fall is the strongest predictor of future falls.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: High-risk medications for falls include psychoactive drugs, antidepressants, antipsychotics, benzodiazepines, opioids, and loop diuretics; overtreating hypertension or diabetes can also cause falls.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Always ask specifically about over-the-counter diphenhydramine, since it is often taken without being on the medication list and few older patients should be on it.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: For the 'mind' component, consider the three Ds — dementia, depression, and delirium — as contributors to falls; an acute URI or UTI causing delirium can present as a fall.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: For a fall workup, targeted testing based on history and exam may include an EKG and telemetry for arrhythmia, a hemoglobin A1C to assess diabetes control, and a good exam of affected joints.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Screening tests like the timed up and go are less useful in the acute hospital setting because a patient hospitalized for a fall is already known to be at highest risk; these tests are more for outpatient risk stratification.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: There are three evidence-based arms of fall prevention: multifactorial risk factor assessment and management, exercise, and home modifications.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Multifactorial risk factor assessment and management yields about a 25% relative risk reduction in falls in meta-analyses and is recommended for the highest-risk patients (hospitalized for a fall, major injury, one or more falls in the past year, or unsteady on their feet).

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: About half of falls are not reported to physicians because patients assume falls are a normal part of aging or fear loss of independence.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: In a bradycardic patient on both metoprolol and donepezil, consider that donepezil may cause bradycardia and can be deprescribed if not clearly effective; back off metoprolol depending on the indication.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: For a patient with atrial fibrillation who falls, unless there is an immediate contraindication, continue anticoagulation in the hospital; the decision is nuanced and best made with the patient's PCP based on trajectory and goals rather than scores alone.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Exercise—specifically balance and strength training—reduces the risk of falls and is recommended for all patients, both low-risk and those who have had major falls.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Tai Chi is a specific evidence-based exercise program shown to be beneficial for fall prevention and can be adapted from a seated position up to standing.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Vitamin D is no longer thought to prevent falls in and of itself; supplement only for a low level, not routinely for fall prevention.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Home modifications reduce falls by about a 23% relative risk reduction in meta-analyses and can be guided by occupational therapist home safety evaluations or the CDC STEADI home safety checklist.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Getting an assisted device is not necessarily permanent; the safest gait is a normal step-through pattern, and many patients can progress from walker to cane to no device.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Physical therapy assistance levels: minimal assist is about 25% help from the therapist, moderate assist is about 50/50, and maximal assist is about 75% therapist and 25% patient.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: For a fallen patient, PT assessment starts with orthostatic blood pressures and functional testing of bed mobility, sitting balance, and leg strength against gravity before ambulation.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: GERD is a clinical diagnosis defined by troublesome symptoms occurring when gastric contents travel from the stomach into the esophagus, distinguishing the disease (GERD) from simple gastroesophageal reflux.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Alarm/red flag symptoms to screen for in reflux patients include dysphagia, bleeding, anemia, vomiting, and weight loss.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: The vast majority of reflux events are an upright, postprandial phenomenon occurring in the first 90 minutes after meals, and reflux typically decreases when lying recumbent at night.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: The primary mechanism of reflux is transient lower esophageal sphincter relaxations occurring with a mismatch of elevated intragastric pressure, with anatomical causes such as a hiatal hernia displacing the EG junction defense mechanism.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Common trigger foods (the 'five C's') include citrus, carbonated beverages, caffeine, and cocktails/alcohol, but there is no strong objective evidence they worsen acid exposure; patients should avoid foods that trigger their own symptoms.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: For classic heartburn without alarm symptoms, an 8-week trial of once-daily PPI at 40 mg omeprazole equivalent, taken 30 minutes before the first meal of the day, is the recommended initial treatment.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: PPIs are preferred over H2 receptor antagonists for reflux because H2RAs develop tachyphylaxis after about 4 weeks, and PPIs are superior at improving heartburn symptoms and healing erosive esophagitis.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Prescribing PPIs with an exit strategy—e.g., 8 weeks with no refills requiring a call-back—prevents patients from remaining on indefinite therapy inadvertently.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Potassium-competitive acid blockers (PCABs/'P-CABs') reversibly inhibit the H/K ATPase, act within hours (versus 2-3 days for PPIs), and are FDA-approved for non-erosive reflux disease but currently mainly used for severe erosive esophagitis and H. pylori.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Patients with high-risk complicated reflux disease—class C or D esophagitis, Barrett's esophagus, or peptic stricture—should be kept on long-term PPI therapy.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Patients with non-erosive reflux disease are the ones to consider periodically trialing off PPIs to see if their symptom burden allows discontinuation.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: When stopping PPIs, counsel patients about rebound acid hypersecretion and encourage them to push through the first 10 days, after which symptom trajectory often improves.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: A PPI trial is pragmatic but not diagnostically conclusive: sensitivity is about 71% and specificity only about 44%, with ~35% of patients with completely normal testing still improving on PPI.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: The 2022 ACG guidelines recommend objective testing (rather than simply resuming a PPI) when symptoms recur after a trial, performing endoscopy 2-4 weeks off PPI so erosive esophagitis has time to recur.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Non-erosive reflux disease cannot be diagnosed if the endoscopy is performed while the patient is on a PPI, since about 90% of endoscopies done on PPI appear normal.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Stopping PPI for 2-4 weeks before endoscopy also helps unmask eosinophilic esophagitis, a mimicker of persistent reflux disease that can be treated by PPIs, allowing biopsy at endoscopy.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Among reported PPI adverse effects, only increased risk of enteric (non-C. diff) infections had the most conclusive association; the others (CKD, osteoporosis, dementia) are association studies without established cause-and-effect.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Routine monitoring of bone density, magnesium, or B12 is not recommended for patients on PPIs without other risk factors; those with CKD can safely use PPIs but should have creatinine monitored at least yearly.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Reflux hypersensitivity is when patients have normal acid exposure on testing but remain symptomatic from weekly acidic/non-acidic reflux, whereas functional heartburn shows no pathologic reflux at all—neither responds well to further acid suppression.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: PPIs are not effective at treating regurgitation, which is often the reason regurgitation-predominant symptoms get grouped into 'refractory GERD'.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Alginates form a raft/matrix over the acid pocket in the stomach acting as a physical barrier against reflux, are taken after meals and at bedtime, and are available over the counter.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Surgical anti-reflux evaluation is most appropriate for patients with regurgitation-predominant symptoms and an anatomical cause (e.g., hiatal hernia); heartburn-predominant patients well-controlled on PPI generally do not need surgery.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: A hiatal hernia is not typically labeled as such until it exceeds 2 cm, and the vast majority of hernias do not require operation unless something else is going on.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Barrett's esophagus screening is a one-time endoscopy for patients with chronic GERD (typically 5+ years) plus three risk factors: age over 50, male sex, white race, tobacco smoking, obesity, or family history of Barrett's/esophageal cancer.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Barium esophagram is not diagnostic for reflux disease and should not be used to diagnose GERD; it is a provoked test useful for evaluating dysphagia and anatomy such as hiatal hernia.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: The overarching principle is to objectively define reflux disease before committing a patient to long-term PPI therapy, and to verify that PPIs were started for genuine reflux rather than nonspecific symptoms like cough or throat clearing.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: All individuals with a diagnosis of COPD should be screened for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, regardless of age or ethnicity, per GOLD 2025.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: A low alpha-1 antitrypsin level should be repeated, as it can come back spuriously low; if low on two tests, refer to pulmonary.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: Any patient presenting with a pulmonary complaint should get an AP and lateral chest X-ray early to avoid missing a mass or obstructing lesion.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: The GOLD spirometric criterion for obstruction is a post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC ratio less than 0.70.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: GOLD severity grades by post-bronchodilator FEV1: Gold 1 is >=80% predicted, Gold 2 is 50-79%, Gold 3 is 30-49%, and Gold 4 is less than 30%.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: GOLD 2025 classifies patients as A, B, or E: A and B are less versus more symptomatic (by MMRC or CAT scores), and E denotes exacerbators.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: A COPD hospitalization in the last year or two moderate exacerbations pushes a patient immediately to group E regardless of symptoms, because recurrent exacerbators are at higher risk for progressive disease.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: GOLD 2025 supports race-neutral GLI reference equations rather than race-specific equations, which could disadvantage certain groups.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: A low-dose CT for lung cancer screening or emphysema findings on imaging is not a diagnostic tool for COPD; COPD is a clinical/spirometric diagnosis based on symptoms and airflow obstruction.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: The mortality benefits in COPD come largely from smoking cessation, vaccinations, pulmonary rehabilitation, and oxygen therapy—these outweigh inhaler therapy for adding quality-adjusted life years.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: ATS favors starting COPD patients on a LABA/LAMA combination (e.g., tiotropium-olodaterol), though guidelines also permit LABA or LAMA monotherapy.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: LABA monotherapy, discouraged in asthma due to a harm signal, does not carry that same signal in COPD and can be used.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: Inhaled corticosteroids should not be first-line in COPD patients without an allergic signal or exacerbation history; eosinophils >=300 favor ICS, 100-300 is uncertain, and <100 shows little to no benefit.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: Triple therapy means adding an ICS; double therapy should be LABA/LAMA, not LAMA/ICS or LABA/ICS.
