episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: COPD patients should be screened for comorbidities including cardiovascular disease, pulmonary hypertension (via echocardiogram), diabetes/metabolic syndrome, osteoporosis (from steroid courses), and depression/anxiety.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: If an echocardiogram in a COPD patient shows elevated right-sided filling pressures, elevated pulmonary artery pressure, or right ventricular/atrial dilation, refer to pulmonary even if symptoms are well controlled.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: A cost-conscious 'open triple therapy' can be built with generic budesonide-formoterol plus generic tiotropium for roughly $150-200 per month, versus $500-600 for branded combination triple inhalers.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: Home non-invasive ventilation is considered in COPD patients with chronic hypercapnia (PaCO2 > 52 mmHg) and should be used 6-8 hours a night; the HOT-HMV trial showed adding home NIV plus oxygen reduced readmissions and improved survival.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: Dupilumab is currently the biologic available as an add-on for COPD, having gained an indication for asthma-COPD overlap.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: A bode index, calculable in primary care, estimates mortality using BMI, obstruction, MMRC dyspnea, and the six-minute walk test.

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!
statement: For patients starting non-invasive ventilation, counsel them to wear the device 1-2 hours before bed while awake to acclimate, and provide anticipatory guidance that they will likely dislike it initially but get used to it.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: In a double-blind RCT, intermediate-dose CBD (2.5 mg/kg twice daily, i.e., ~5 mg/kg/day) over 28 days caused elevated liver enzymes in more patients than placebo, even in healthy individuals.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: The intermediate CBD dose studied (2.5 mg/kg twice daily) is substantially lower than the epilepsy dosing of roughly 25 mg/kg daily, which was also associated with elevated liver enzymes in clinical trials.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: For adverse-event outcomes, a highly selected healthy population and a per-protocol analysis increase confidence in the causal effect, whereas pragmatic trials with intention-to-treat analysis tend to underestimate adverse effects.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: In the FAIR-HF2 trial, ferric carboxymaltose did not significantly reduce cardiovascular death or heart failure hospitalization versus placebo in HFrEF, even among patients with transferrin saturation less than 20%.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: IV iron is currently in the heart failure guidelines as a class 2A/2B recommendation, and experts continue to screen and treat iron deficiency in HFrEF despite this negative trial.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: Ferric carboxymaltose can cause hypophosphatemia, which may make patients feel unwell and blunt quality-of-life benefits in iron trials.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: In the BedMed trial, taking antihypertensives at bedtime versus in the morning showed no difference in death or hospitalization for stroke, MI, or CHF (2.3 vs 2.4 per 100 patient-years, hazard ratio near 1) over ~4.5 years.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: Bedtime dosing of antihypertensives appears safe, with no difference in falls, orthostasis, cognitive decline, or quality of life between morning and night dosing.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: Because adherence was higher in the morning-dosing group at 6 months, a reasonable default is to recommend morning dosing while allowing patients to take antihypertensives whenever they can most reliably adhere.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: Carpal tunnel syndrome is associated with rheumatoid arthritis, with a roughly twofold increased risk in the years before RA diagnosis and about a 1.8-fold increased risk after RA diagnosis.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: This RA-carpal tunnel association should not prompt serologic testing in every patient with carpal tunnel syndrome, but atypical or worsening wrist pain warrants pausing to consider alternative diagnoses.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: As of July 2025, the nonsteroidal MRA finerenone is FDA approved for heart failure with ejection fraction at or above 40%, based on the FINEARTS-HF trial.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: In FINEARTS-HF, finerenone reduced worsening heart failure events (first or recurrent unplanned hospitalization or urgent visit for heart failure) but did not reduce cardiovascular death.

episode_title: #496 Hotcakes: Cannabidiol (CBD)-induced liver injury, IV iron for HFrEF, bedtime BP meds, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome & RA, & Finerenone for HFpEF
statement: For HFpEF, if cost is not an issue finerenone is a reasonable evidence-based choice, but spironolactone is a perfectly good and lower-cost alternative supported by reappraisal of the TOPCAT trial.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: Diarrhea is defined as liquid stools more than three times a day; multiple formed stools do not qualify and this should be confirmed before working up 'diarrhea.'

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: PPIs like omeprazole are an established risk factor for C. difficile, thought to work by subtly modifying the microbiome.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: Nearly all beta-lactams (with augmentin sometimes implicated) and fluoroquinolones are the highest-risk antibiotics for C. diff, while macrolides, trimethoprim-sulfa, aminoglycosides, methenamine, fosfomycin, and tetracyclines are lower risk.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: Toxin B (encoded by TCDB) is the toxin tied to human C. diff disease; there are no reported cases of toxin A-only C. diff disease.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: A positive NAAT with a negative toxin B EIA should not lead you to dismiss C. diff; in the absence of other information a compatible clinical picture can still be defined as C. diff.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: GDH is present in all C. diff strains, so a positive GDH indicates colonization (toxigenic or non-toxigenic) and requires a second-tier test to determine if the strain is toxigenic.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: Metronidazole is a poor choice for C. diff: it is predominantly absorbed in the small bowel so it doesn't reach the colon, and the CDC has associated it with increased mortality.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: For microbiome support after C. diff, clinicians may counsel soluble fiber (psyllium/Metamucil, store brand is equivalent), kefir, and a healthy diet (DASH or Mediterranean), acknowledging this is soft science.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: Antimotility agents are generally not recommended in C. diff, and diphenoxylate (Lomotil) especially should be avoided, for fear of precipitating toxic megacolon.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: For home infection control after C. diff, advise handwashing, hygiene, and using 10% bleach in bathrooms, since the organism forms hardy spores that spread widely.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: The risk of recurrent C. diff escalates with each episode: about 25% after the first, 40% after the second, and 60% after the third or more.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: For recurrent C. diff, treatment involves tapering: fidaxomicin at full dose for 5 days then every other day, or vancomycin four times daily for 10-14 days followed by a long slow taper stretched out over weeks.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: Previous oral antibiotic exposure (even ~15 days total) more than 10 years before colon cancer onset has been linked to increased colon cancer incidence across multiple studies, supporting antimicrobial stewardship.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: Traditional fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) was cut off by the FDA as of 1/31/24 once quality-controlled fecal microbiota replacement products became available; FMT now requires investigational protocols or patient-specific INDs.

episode_title: #494 C. diff, Diarrhea, the Microbiome, and New Therapies with Dr. Cynthia Sears. Live from Johns Hopkins Grand Rounds
statement: C. diff accounts for about 20% of antibiotic-associated diarrhea; virtually all antibiotics can cause nonspecific GI distress and diarrhea.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: Home self-collection kits for cervical cancer screening have received FDA approval, using a wand-shaped tool with a cotton swab used like a tampon to collect a sample, aiming to lower barriers to screening.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: In the ESSENCE phase 3 trial, once-weekly semaglutide showed greater resolution of steatohepatitis without worsening fibrosis, plus reduction in liver fibrosis, compared to placebo in patients with MASH and moderate/advanced fibrosis.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: Resmetirom is a liver-specific drug approved for steatohepatitis that works without requiring weight loss, in contrast to semaglutide whose benefit appears tied to weight loss.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: In an open-label RCT, treating male partners with metronidazole 400 mg twice daily plus topical 2% clindamycin to the glans penis twice daily for 7 days significantly reduced BV recurrence in female partners (35% vs 63% in controls at 12 weeks).

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: BV recurrence at 12 weeks occurred in 35% of the partner-treatment group versus 63% of controls, an absolute risk difference of 2.6 recurrences per person-year, and the trial was stopped early for inferiority of the control arm.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: Bacterial vaginosis affects about a third of reproductive-aged women, with over 50% of patients experiencing recurrence within 3 months.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: In the SAVE-SAMS trial, moderate-intensity statin plus ezetimibe caused fewer statin-associated muscle symptoms (0.7% vs 5.7%) with similar LDL and total cholesterol lowering and less new-onset diabetes compared with high-intensity statin alone.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: The SAVE-SAMS statin/ezetimibe trial was limited by its open-label design (symptom-driven outcome), short follow-up, and lack of hard cardiovascular endpoints, so it demonstrates comparable LDL lowering but not equivalent clinical outcomes.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: In the CHALLENGE trial, a 3-year structured exercise program plus health education improved both disease-free survival and overall survival in high-risk stage 2 or stage 3 colon cancer patients who had completed adjuvant chemotherapy.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: In CHALLENGE, exercise produced an absolute overall survival benefit of about 7% at 8 years, a magnitude comparable to trastuzumab's ~5% absolute overall survival benefit in breast cancer.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: The CHALLENGE exercise trial is limited by very slow recruitment (2009 to 2023), low adherence that declined by years 2 and 3, and unclear biologic mechanism since exercise physiologic measures did not change significantly, raising questions about implementation and reverse causality.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: Cytisinicline is the first potentially new smoking cessation drug in about 20 years, dosed 3 mg three times daily, with a mechanism of action similar to varenicline.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: In an RCT, cytisinicline produced roughly a threefold increase in smoking abstinence at 6 weeks and a fourfold increase (odds ratio ~4) at 12 weeks, with significantly higher abstinence than placebo maintained at 24 weeks whether given for 6 or 12 weeks.

episode_title: #488 DIGEST: Cytisinicline for smoking cessation, partner treatment for BV, combination therapy with statins and ezetimibe, and exercise and survival in colon cancer
statement: Cytisinicline's three-times-daily dosing is more burdensome than bupropion or varenicline, and its side effects of insomnia and abnormal dreams occurred in over 10% of patients, more than placebo and at rates similar to varenicline.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: In a substudy of the VITAL trial, neither 2,000 units/day of vitamin D3 nor 1 gram/day of omega-3 fatty acids improved grip strength, walking speed, balance, chair stands, or the timed get-up-and-go test in generally healthy older adults over two years.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: On balance, current evidence makes it hard to prove that vitamin D prevents falls, and some higher-dose trials show a signal for increased falls.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: Guidelines recommend checking vitamin D only in people at risk for deficiency, such as those who are institutionalized or have osteoporosis, rather than screening the average patient.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: In a target trial emulation study of adults over 65 with diabetic neuropathy, postherpetic neuralgia, or fibromyalgia, incident gabapentin users had a lower hazard ratio for falls compared with duloxetine users.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: The gabapentin falls study is limited by a low median starting dose of 200 mg and short follow-up, so it may not reflect the higher doses (up to 3600 mg) and long-term use where falls risk is a concern.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: In the gabapentin falls study, patients were censored after 14 days of discontinuation, making the average treatment duration only about four to six weeks, which limits applicability to patients on gabapentin for years.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: Gepotidacin received FDA approval in March 2025 for treatment of women aged 12 and older with uncomplicated UTI, based on the EAGLE-2 and EAGLE-3 trials.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: Gepotidacin has a novel mechanism, inhibiting DNA gyrase and topoisomerase 4 at a site different from fluoroquinolones, which is hoped to limit cross-resistance with fluoroquinolones.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: Gepotidacin is dosed 1500 mg twice daily for 5 days for uncomplicated UTI.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: In the EAGLE trials, gepotidacin was non-inferior to nitrofurantoin (and superior in EAGLE-3), but had more side effects (diarrhea in 15-20%, nausea in ~10%) and likely higher cost, so it will probably be reserved for resistant cases.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: In the DRIFT trial, 4:3 intermittent fasting (80% calorie reduction on 3 non-consecutive days) produced greater weight loss at 12 months (7.6%) than daily caloric restriction (34% daily reduction) which produced 5% loss.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: In the intermittent fasting trial, both groups also received a high-intensity behavioral support program including portion control, calorie counting, a gym membership, and encouragement to exercise 300 minutes per week.

episode_title: #484 Hotcakes: Vitamin D3, Omega-3 Fatty Acids, and Physical Performance, Gabapentin and Falls, Gepotidacin for UTI, and Intermittent Fasting for Weight Loss
statement: Preserving muscle by exercising is important with any weight-loss modality, including GLP-1 agonists, because calorie restriction plus weight-loss drugs without exercise leads to loss of lean mass many patients cannot afford.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: For older adults, guideline-writing agencies recommend relaxing the A1C target to reduce the burden of polypharmacy and the risk of hypoglycemia.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: In older adults, recurrent hypoglycemia worsens cognition and increases the risk of falls, so both high and low blood sugars cause harm.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Glucose tablets are preferable for hypoglycemia treatment because they are pure glucose and unappealing to snack on; four white sugar packets are needed to reach 15 grams of glucose.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: The rule of 15 for hypoglycemia: give 15 grams of glucose every 15 minutes, rechecking blood sugar every 15 minutes until it rises; if it doesn't rise, go to the ER.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Sulfonylureas like glimepiride bypass glucose sensing and stimulate insulin release even when glucose is low, making them high risk for hypoglycemia in older adults.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: For older adults, target fasting glucose of about 100 to 150 and postprandial less than 180; readings below 100 or below 150 postmeal suggest treatment is too tight and should be backed off.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: A glucose of 150 corresponds to roughly an A1C of 7 and a glucose of 180 to roughly an A1C of 8; for older adults an A1C target of 7 to 8 is appropriate.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: The ADA guidelines recommend becoming thoughtful about the diabetes regimen for patients 70 years or older.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: For a patient with very limited lifespan (e.g., hospice), the goal is to avoid significant glycosuria; keeping glucose reasonable, roughly 150 to 250, is acceptable to prevent symptoms.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Medicare and Medicaid will cover a CGM for patients on insulin (even single daily injection) without requiring proof of fingerstick testing.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Meglitinides (nateglinide, repaglinide) are weaker cousins of sulfonylureas with a shorter duration of action and lower hypoglycemia risk, making them a reasonable secretagogue option for older patients.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: DPP-4 inhibitors provide a small A1C drop of about 0.4 to 0.5% with a minimal side effect profile; they are renally dosed but remain safe above the GFR cut point.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Pioglitazone was absolved of the concern for causing heart disease but causes fluid retention, so it should not be used in patients with heart failure or CHF.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Pioglitazone, dosed at 15, 30, or 45 mg once daily, can cause bone loss and osteoporosis, so caution is warranted particularly in patients with low bone density.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Pioglitazone was shown in the IRIS trial to reduce the risk of stroke, may delay cognitive loss, and mobilizes visceral fat, offering potential benefit for MASH patients with low hypoglycemia risk.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: The A1C target during pregnancy is less than 6% because increased red blood cell turnover and higher intravascular volume make the A1C read artifactually low.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: For gestational diabetes, treatment targets are fasting 70 to 95 and 2-hour postprandial 100 to 120, which are more stringent than for non-pregnant adults.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: GLP-1 agonists and SGLT2 inhibitors must be discontinued in pregnancy; GLP-1s should be stopped once pregnancy is discovered and SGLT2 inhibitors discontinued immediately.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Metformin and sulfonylureas are no longer first-line in pregnancy because they cross the placenta and are associated with higher rates of metabolic dysfunction in the child later in life; insulin is preferred.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Lifestyle changes (nutrition counseling and regular moderate exercise) are first-line for gestational diabetes, with insulin added if that is insufficient.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Folic acid supplementation at 400 to 800 micrograms per day is recommended to prevent neural tube defects in pregnancy.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Women with diabetes need yearly dilated eye exams, and during pregnancy every trimester if concerns exist, because retinopathy and macular edema can accelerate and cause blindness during pregnancy.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: In gestational diabetes, glucose spikes after meals often predominate over fasting elevations, so starting fast-acting insulin before basal may be appropriate.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Women who had gestational diabetes have about a 50% chance of developing type 2 diabetes within the next 5 years, warranting close attention to weight loss postpartum.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Topiramate is absolutely teratogenic and must not be used for weight loss in patients who may become pregnant; GLP-1 agonists are the safest effective weight loss option after breastfeeding concludes.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Corticosteroids raise glucose throughout the day but have their most dramatic effect on postprandial glucose due to impaired glucose disposal at insulin-sensitive GLUT receptors.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: NPH insulin has a pharmacokinetic profile similar to short and intermediate-acting steroids (hydrocortisone, prednisone, prednisolone, methylprednisolone), making it useful for steroid-induced hyperglycemia; onset 2-4 hours, peak 4-10 hours, duration up to 18 hours.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: For insulin-naive patients starting steroids, NPH can be dosed by prednisone equivalent: 0.1 units/kg for 10 mg, 0.2 for 20 mg, 0.3 for 30 mg, and 0.4 for 40 mg (capped at 0.4), given at the time of the steroid.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Dexamethasone has a 1-to-5 prednisone equivalency (much stronger) and a long, flat profile, so glargine may be a better choice than NPH for dexamethasone-induced hyperglycemia.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Insulin is renally cleared, so declining renal function can cause hypoglycemia from insulin lasting longer than expected and stacking.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: When adjusting insulin, start by changing doses 10 to 20% based on hypoglycemia (20% if a lot of hypoglycemia), then reassess response.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: When converting basal insulin to NPH, take about two-thirds of the dose in the morning with the steroid and one-third at dinner to cover the meal and nighttime.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: NPH is dosed twice a day, but patients on it should eat three meals a day because the midday peak can cause hypoglycemia if lunch is skipped.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: SGLT2 inhibitors become more effective at offloading glucose the higher the sugar is above the renal threshold of 180 mg/dL and may help prevent steroid-related fluid overload, but should not be started around an acute kidney injury.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: In patients starting high-dose steroids (e.g., for PMR), insulin is needed because glucose rises quickly and GLP-1 agonists take several weeks of dose titration to work.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Long-standing type 2 diabetes often requires insulin because beta cell function is progressively lost over time, not just because of insulin resistance.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: The ADA older adult section introduces a 4-M framework: mentation, medications, mobility, and what matters most.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: GLP-1 agonists can cause muscle (lean) weight loss, so they should be used cautiously in older, frail, thin patients who cannot afford to lose weight.

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD
statement: Empagliflozin, if used, should be taken toward the morning so increased urination does not occur overnight in older adults.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: The distinction between mild cognitive impairment (MCI, now minor neurocognitive disorder) and dementia (now major neurocognitive disorder) is based on whether cognitive decline affects day-to-day function.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: The Mini-Cog (clock drawing plus three-item recall) is a good, easy-to-administer sorting/case-finding test in primary care; an abnormal result simply means higher probability of dementia and warrants follow-up rather than immediate diagnosis.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: The Mini-Cog is especially useful in patients with lower education and can be adapted to many language contexts, and it can even be administered over video visits.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Dementia diagnosis rests on three components in the DSM-5 criteria: cognitive decline, functional decline, and a medical workup to exclude other causes.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Cognitive assessment should cover multiple DSM-5 domains, not just memory: memory and learning, language, executive function, social cognition/behavior, visual-spatial, complex attention, and motor.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Asking whether the ability to manage finances has changed is one of the best single questions to probe executive functioning.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: REM sleep behavior disorder (moving, yelling, very different sleep) is a potential sign of Lewy body dementia and is more likely in patients with Parkinson's.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: The laboratory workup for reversible/treatable causes includes B12, thyroid (TSH), and, when risk factors are present, HIV and RPR/syphilis testing; RPR and HIV can be skipped in patients with no risk factors.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Head imaging is especially warranted for red flags: anticoagulation with head injury, HIV, prior non-skin cancer, age under 60, or rapid onset (progression under about a year); a CT is acceptable in low-risk cases though MRI gives better structural detail.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Amyloid and tau biomarkers (CSF, PET, or newer blood-based assays) are not yet part of the standard primary care workup and remain in the specialist domain, typically used to confirm Alzheimer's pathology; blood biomarkers are not FDA approved and Medicare generally does not pay for them.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: In CSF, low amyloid and high tau (with tau 217 being a more sensitive/specific marker) are suggestive of Alzheimer's pathology.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: The core dementia care plan is a 'brain health plan' that can begin as soon as screening is positive—even before a formal diagnosis—because treating cognitive/functional decline is non-specific and doesn't require confirming a diagnosis.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: The brain health plan centers on encouraging physical and social activity and managing vascular risk factors (blood pressure, diabetes, high LDL), which are modifiable and associated with slower decline.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Correcting hearing and vision is a key part of the brain health plan; hearing impairment, early-life education, and LDL are among the biggest modifiable risk factors for dementia.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Deprescribing medications with anticholinergic burden and sedative-hypnotics (benzodiazepines, sleep aids) is an important part of brain health; the anticholinergic burden can be calculated at acbcalc.com.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Symptomatic dementia medications fall into two classes: acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., donepezil, or rivastigmine patch) approved for mild-to-moderate dementia, and the NMDA antagonist memantine approved for moderate-to-severe dementia.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Cholinesterase inhibitors can cause weight loss, nausea, diarrhea, insomnia, and dizziness; start low, titrate up, monitor weights, and stop if not clearly helping, since a drug that isn't clearly helping is only causing harm.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies (lecanemab, donanemab) require confirmed amyloid pathology and are only appropriate in preclinical or mild-stage disease; their main serious risk is ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities) with edema or hemorrhage, requiring proactive imaging.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Patients on anti-amyloid antibodies cannot be on an anticoagulant, and any headache or mental status change requires urgent imaging because of hemorrhage risk.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Patients with the APOE4 allele have a higher risk of ARIA complications from anti-amyloid therapy.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: About half of people with dementia die without ever receiving a diagnosis, and most are diagnosed only in moderate-to-advanced stages—an argument for more proactive earlier detection.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Prognosis correlates with stage at diagnosis: mild dementia averages roughly 8-10 years, moderate (with ADL impairment) about 3-5 years, and advanced about 1-2 years.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Dementia staging is based on function: mild when only IADLs are impaired (ADLs intact), moderate once ADL impairment begins, and severe when dependent in nearly all ADLs.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Collateral history from a caregiver is often essential because patients may be unaware of or unable to report their symptoms, particularly behavioral symptoms; it can help to split the visit to talk to patient and informant separately.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: On physical exam for suspected dementia, key elements are looking for signs of asymmetry suggesting prior vascular events and for Parkinsonian signs, plus assessing gait (slow or unsteady gait is associated with neurodegenerative disorders).

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Cognitive test interpretation must be individualized: patients with lower education often fall below standard cutoffs because tests weren't validated in those populations, while patients with higher education (greater cognitive reserve) may have many symptoms yet still score normally.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: For behavioral symptoms like leaving the stove on, practical interventions include deactivating the stove or removing the knobs, and for wandering, covering exits or placing a black mat in front of the door, which patients often won't cross.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: A quick clinical framework for typing dementia: older patients who move and speak fast suggest Alzheimer's; younger patients could be Alzheimer's or frontotemporal; slow movement with vascular risk factors suggests vascular dementia; and Parkinsonian features suggest Parkinson's or Lewy body depending on which came first.

episode_title: #478 Dementia 2.0 with Anna Chodos
statement: Detecting dementia early allows patients to participate in advance care planning while they still can, giving them autonomy and avoiding crisis-driven decisions later—a stronger rationale for early detection than currently available drug therapies.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: FDA labeling notes that statin-associated cognitive symptoms are uncommon, typically transient, and usually resolve within 3 weeks of discontinuation, with no proven permanent cognitive impairment.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: The EBBINGHAUS-OLE open-label extension found no cognitive decline over 5 years in patients achieving very low LDL with evolocumab added to statin therapy, though it was limited by being an open-label extension in a relatively young, highly educated, selected population.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: Suzetrigine is the first member of a new class of non-opioid oral pain medications (FDA approved January 2025 for acute pain), working by selectively inhibiting a sodium channel subtype expressed only in peripheral sensory neurons.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: In the phase 2 abdominoplasty trial, suzetrigine reduced pain from baseline ~7.5/10 to 4/10 versus 5/10 with placebo; in the bunionectomy trial pain fell from ~7/10 to 2.5/10 versus 3.5/10 with placebo.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: In the suzetrigine trial, patients receiving hydrocodone/acetaminophen got comparable pain relief to placebo, raising concern that the 5 mg hydrocodone comparator dose was too low to be a fair active comparator.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: Suzetrigine adverse events included constipation in about 10% and headache in about 15%, both more common than placebo.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: Concern about SGLT2 inhibitors and amputation originated from early canagliflozin trials showing about a two-fold increase in amputation risk, leading to a 2017 black box warning that was later downgraded.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: A retrospective cohort of veterans with diabetes found only about one additional peripheral arterial disease event per 10,000 patient-years with SGLT2 inhibitors versus DPP4 inhibitors—roughly one extra PAD event per 1,000 users treated for about a year.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: An active comparator new-user design (studying patients newly starting both drugs) helps minimize confounding by indication and avoids biases from comparing prevalent long-term users.

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: The MINT trial of restrictive (Hgb <7) versus liberal (Hgb <10) transfusion in acute MI with anemia was inconclusive for its primary outcome, but a subgroup with type 1 MI showed a benefit from liberal transfusion (NNT ~23).

episode_title: #477 Hotcakes: Suzetrigine for acute pain, SGLT2i and PAD, Safety of Lowering LDL, Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion Strategy in MI revisited
statement: A patient-level meta-analysis found liberal transfusion did not improve 30-day survival or reduce MI, but showed a 6-month survival advantage driven by reduced cardiac death; a reasonable approach is to favor transfusion in patients with ongoing ischemia or blood loss not yet revascularized rather than all MI patients with Hgb <10.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: There is no evidence that combining naltrexone with acamprosate provides additional benefit over either agent alone.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Acamprosate is dosed at 600 mg three times daily, which can be halved in renal injury; there are no other established alternative dosing strategies.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: The 100 mg dose of naltrexone is not FDA approved and has weak supporting data, so it is not a reliable strategy—especially if nausea already occurs at 50 mg.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Abstinence should not be the only goal for alcohol use disorder; reduced or safe consumption improves quality of life, reduces symptoms, and improves mortality, and should be celebrated as success.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: The Sinclair method / targeted naltrexone involves taking naltrexone as needed before anticipated triggers; it was most studied with nalmefene (not available in the US) but has some data for naltrexone.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Disulfiram can be used in a targeted, event-based manner (e.g., before holidays or triggering events), which patients find empowering because alcohol is completely off the menu.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Disulfiram is an acetaldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor that blocks conversion of acetaldehyde to acetic acid, causing toxic acetaldehyde accumulation and a severe hangover-like reaction within 15 to 40 minutes of alcohol consumption.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Disulfiram efficacy is undermined by double-blind trial designs because it works as a deterrent; open-label trials show dramatically greater efficacy, as demonstrated in the 2014 Skinner et al meta-analysis.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Supervised disulfiram—observed by a family member, friend, or other invested support person—is what has been shown to be profoundly effective.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: The disulfiram-alcohol reaction includes nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, facial flushing, diaphoresis, and headache, comes on fast, can last several hours, and has sent patients to the emergency room.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Cognitive impairment is a contraindication to disulfiram, since patients must reliably remember they took it and understand the consequences of drinking.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Disulfiram is not recommended in advanced liver disease (Child-Pugh class B or C or decompensated cirrhosis); it can be used cautiously in Child-Pugh class A cirrhosis with LFT monitoring, checking after about three to four weeks.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Some patients can drink through disulfiram even at doses escalated to 500 mg, and once that happens the medication will no longer be effective for them.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Alcohol-containing products like aftershave and most mouthwashes rarely trigger a disulfiram reaction, but patients should be judicious with alcohol-containing food sauces (e.g., Marsala wine, penne alla vodka).

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Topiramate has moderate effect size for reducing heavy drinking days and drinks per drinking day and reduces GGT, but requires slow titration from 25 mg twice daily up to at least 200 mg/day over eight or more weeks, with double the placebo dropout rate from side effects.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Topiramate, gabapentin, and acamprosate have essentially no hepatic clearance and are safe in advanced liver disease; naltrexone is increasingly considered safe even in decompensated cirrhosis.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Gabapentin's early enthusiasm for AUD was not confirmed by later studies, but it appears most effective for patients with a history of alcohol withdrawal, typically dosed up to 600 mg three times daily.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Naltrexone combined with gabapentin is the combination regimen with at least some supporting data, whereas other combinations lack robust evidence.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Evidence for GLP-1 agonists in alcohol use disorder is not yet robust, as most studies had non-alcohol primary outcomes, were retrospective, or were animal studies.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Mutual self-help groups are widely available and free, and evidence supports them as roughly as effective as longitudinal care with a clinical psychologist.

episode_title: #476 Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder 2.0 with Dr Stephen Holt
statement: Adding evidence-based behavioral therapies (such as CBT or motivational enhancement therapy) to pharmacotherapy improves outcomes, particularly for patients with underlying mental health disorders.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) uses circulating cell-free DNA from maternal blood to detect fetal chromosomal abnormalities and can incidentally detect maternal abnormalities, which are typically reported as abnormal or non-reportable results.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: Among 107 patients with abnormal or non-reportable NIPT results, 52 (almost 50%) were diagnosed with cancer, most commonly lymphoma (including Hodgkin lymphoma), followed by solid tumors such as colorectal and breast cancer.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: Whole body MRI was used for cancer screening in this pregnant/postpartum population because it can be done without radiation exposure.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: In a randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial, vitamin K2 (menaquinone-7, mk7) 180 micrograms nightly reduced the frequency, severity, and duration of nocturnal leg cramps in patients 65 and over.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: The vitamin K2 leg cramp trial excluded patients on vitamin K antagonists like warfarin, and this drug should not be recommended to patients taking those agents.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: The vitamin K2 leg cramp trial excluded patients taking diuretics, so its benefit is unknown in that common population with leg cramps.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: In a VA trial-emulation study, PRN antihypertensives in hospitalized patients were associated with increased acute kidney injury, with IV-only PRN antihypertensives carrying a 64% increased risk of AKI.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: Asymptomatic hypertension in hospitalized patients should prompt investigation of underlying causes (pain, volume overload, sleep, anxiety, alcohol/substance withdrawal, medications like steroids) rather than reflexive as-needed antihypertensive dosing.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: In a Swedish cohort study of over 220,000 patients with alcohol use disorder, semaglutide and liraglutide (but not other GLP-1 agonists) were associated with a significant decrease in alcohol-use-related hospitalizations.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: In the same study, established AUD medications (naltrexone, disulfiram, acamprosate) showed only a modest decrease in alcohol-related hospitalizations, with an adjusted hazard ratio of 0.98.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: GLP-1 agonists for alcohol use disorder are not yet practice-changing; observational data are confounded and randomized trials with AUD as a primary endpoint are needed before adopting this use.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: In the BALANCE trial, 7 days of antibiotics was non-inferior to 14 days for bloodstream infections, with 90-day mortality of 14.5% in the 7-day arm versus 16.5% in the 14-day arm.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: The BALANCE trial excluded patients with Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia, un-drained abscesses, prosthetic joint infection, septic arthritis, and immunocompromised patients, so shorter durations should not be applied to these groups.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: The USPSTF draft recommendation moves toward primary HPV testing (every 5 years) as the preferred cervical cancer screening method for women 30 and older, and now recommends self-collected HPV testing.

episode_title: #472 DIGEST: cfDNA & cancer in Pregnancy, Vitamin K2 for Leg Cramps, PRN anti-hypertensives, GLP-1 Agonists for AUD, Short-Course Antibiotics
statement: Self-collected HPV swabs have shown good concordance with clinician-collected specimens, but FDA approval was limited to in-clinic collection rather than home testing.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Diabetic foot infection management rests on four pillars: debridement of dead tissue, appropriate antibiotics, adequate blood flow, and a good wound healing environment (offloading, tobacco cessation, glucose control).

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Wound swabs of diabetic foot infections often do not correlate with the true pathogen; obtain tissue cultures at the time of debridement instead ('swat the swab').

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: In a clinically stable diabetic foot infection patient with a known plan for debridement and culture retrieval, it can be reasonable to hold empiric antibiotics up front.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Empiric antibiotics for diabetic foot infection should generally include MRSA coverage based on most antibiograms, while aggressive double pseudomonal coverage is less consistently required.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Diabetic foot infection patients generally need an x-ray up front, but x-ray sensitivity is limited because findings can lag by weeks; decisions about MRI should follow after considering exam findings and labs like ESR.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: A positive probe-to-bone test can be diagnostic for osteomyelitis and may give diagnostic information faster than MRI, but its accuracy depends on the practitioner's experience.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: qSOFA (2016) is a quick three-variable bedside screening tool for sepsis, but it is not as sensitive as desired; alternatives like NEWS and MEWS may be more sensitive but are clunkier at the bedside.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: In sepsis, antibiotics should be given within the first hour of identification and fluids within the first three hours.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: POCUS may help identify the type of shock but should not be used to guide fluid resuscitation or to assess adequate fluid response.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Peripheral vasopressors are safe when given above the antecubital fossa; avoid very peripheral IVs like in the hand, and an IO can be used if that's all that's available.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: In sickle cell pain crisis, aim for door-to-analgesia within 60 minutes and reassess pain within 60 minutes.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: For opiate management in sickle cell crisis, continue the patient's outpatient basal opiates and use a PCA for bolus dosing to titrate during the acute pain crisis.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: In sickle cell crisis, use scheduled non-opiate therapies such as ketorolac and Tylenol especially in the first 48 to 72 hours, and be generous with topical diclofenac and lidocaine patches.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: PCAs in sickle cell crisis are more effective and shorten length of stay.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Patients with severe mental illness have a 10 to 20 year shorter life expectancy, largely due to cardiovascular and metabolic disease related in part to their psychiatric medications.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: For delirium assessment, testing attention/concentration (e.g., World backwards, serial sevens) is more helpful than just alert-and-oriented checks, which can be falsely reassuring if recently performed by others.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Chemical restraint choice for agitation should be tailored to the patient: a young otherwise-healthy patient with substance use needs a different agent than an elderly patient with dementia and multiple comorbidities.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: In the reversible workup for altered mental status, folate is probably unnecessary, but TSH, HIV, and syphilis testing are worthwhile since they may uncover treatable causes.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Checking vitamin D in psychiatric patients has value because vitamin D deficiency is comorbid with some antiepileptic drugs and mood stabilizers these patients take.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Given the mortality gap in severe mental illness, consider adding A1c and lipid panels to the reversible workup in these patients.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Low-risk syncope etiologies such as vasovagal or orthostatic syncope are often completely diagnosed by history and physical exam.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: The Canadian Syncope Risk Score helps screen for higher-risk causes like cardiac syncope that may need a more thorough workup.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: To distinguish syncope from seizure: syncopal jerking movements are more myoclonic, shorter (less than 10 seconds), and without a postictal state, whereas seizure movements are tonic-clonic, longer (more than 30 seconds), and followed by a postictal state; lateral tongue biting has a good positive predictive value for seizure.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: For syncope, if no obvious low-risk etiology is identified, obtain a D-dimer to screen for pulmonary embolism.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: In syncope, an in-depth neuro workup or echocardiogram is not needed unless there is a compelling history or exam finding—such as a neuro deficit, seizure history, palpitations, heart murmur, or abnormal EKG.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: A drop in blood pressure without a compensatory change in heart rate suggests autonomic dysfunction.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: High-sensitivity troponin detects lower circulating levels than prior assays, so even a below-threshold value gives a trendable number, and it allows shorter testing intervals (e.g., 1-3 hours or 2 and 4 hours) versus the older 6-8 hour waits.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: ST elevations in lead aVR with ST depressions in other leads can be highly suspicious for left main or multivessel coronary disease.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Unstable angina still exists as part of the ACS spectrum; the 2020 chest pain guidelines and tools like the HEART score help decide whether to work someone up along that spectrum.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Coronary CT can now assess coronary circulation via fractional flow reserve (FFR), previously reserved for catheterization; a completely normal coronary CT carries a roughly 2-year warranty, while other normal stress tests carry roughly a 1-year warranty.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: Cardiac rehab is highly effective and should be offered to essentially anyone who qualifies with these cardiac medical problems.

episode_title: #465 Hospital Medicine Recap Extravaganza: Diabetic Foot, Sepsis, Syncope, Acute Coronary Syndrome, and more!
statement: NSTEMI/non-ST-elevation ACS patients should be classified into high-risk versus very-high-risk to orient the emergent need for catheterization, and dynamic EKG changes warrant more urgent intervention rather than a fixed 24-hour timeline.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: A once-daily single-pill triple combination of amlodipine, telmisartan, and indapamide lowered home blood pressure more than any dual combination of those agents and got more patients to goal, with only modestly more hypotension and electrolyte abnormalities.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: Single-pill combinations of multiple antihypertensive agents are increasingly recommended by guidelines and can help reluctant patients reach blood pressure goals.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: An active run-in phase increases statistical power to detect a therapeutic effect but leads to systematic underestimation of adverse effects, since patients who don't tolerate or adhere drop out before randomization.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: Arm position matters when measuring blood pressure: correct position is the arm resting on a flat surface with the mid-cuff at heart level, whereas the arm dangling at the side or resting in the lap falsely raises readings.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: Improper arm positioning can raise blood pressure readings by roughly 4 to 6 mmHg, with even larger deviations in some individuals and in patients who already have elevated blood pressure.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: In the EARLY TAVR trial of asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis in low-risk patients, early TAVR reduced a composite of death, stroke, or cardiovascular hospitalization, but the benefit was driven entirely by cardiovascular hospitalizations with no difference in death or stroke.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: In asymptomatic low-risk severe aortic stenosis, early TAVR appears safe without a signal for increased stroke, but it is not clear it improves patient-centered outcomes versus waiting until an indication for valve replacement develops.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: In the TRAIN trial of critically ill patients with acute brain injury and anemia, a liberal transfusion strategy produced significantly fewer unfavorable neurologic outcomes and fewer cerebral ischemic events than a restrictive strategy at 180 days, though there was no mortality difference.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: In acute brain injury, autoregulation of cerebral blood flow can be impaired, making adequate hemoglobin and oxygen-carrying capacity physiologically important.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: The updated 2024 ACC/AHA perioperative guidelines recommend using validated risk calculators and the Duke Activity Status Index for functional assessment, and for patients with poor or unclear functional capacity, checking a preoperative BNP (or troponin as a weaker alternative) if it will affect decision-making.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: The updated perioperative guidelines de-emphasize stress testing, reserving it for patients at elevated risk with poor or unknown functional capacity who also have an indication for stress testing independent of the planned surgery.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: The updated perioperative guidelines suggest holding SGLT2 inhibitors for 3 to 4 days prior to surgery.

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines
statement: New-onset atrial fibrillation in the perioperative/hospital setting should not be dismissed as a transient trigger-related event; recurrence rates are high and patients may need rhythm control and assessment for anticoagulation.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Bell's palsy is defined as spontaneous peripheral impairment of the seventh cranial nerve occurring in isolation of any other neurologic or systemic symptoms; involvement of any other cranial nerve or systemic signs argues against the diagnosis.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Peripheral seventh nerve lesions (Bell's palsy) involve the forehead, whereas central lesions spare the forehead; flattening and diminished wrinkling of the forehead strongly suggests a peripheral lesion.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: The seventh cranial nerve innervates the stapedius muscle which dampens sound, so Bell's palsy causes hyperacusis (sounds are louder on the affected side).

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Bell's palsy reaches maximum severity gradually within 48 to 72 hours, whereas a stroke is at maximum intensity at onset; this temporal pattern helps distinguish the two.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Because brain stem cranial nerve nuclei are packed closely together, a brain stem lesion causing seventh nerve involvement usually affects neighboring nerves (V, VI, VIII); look specifically for facial sensation changes, extraocular movement problems, and hearing loss.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: For subtle motor weakness suggesting a central cause, high-yield exam maneuvers are the pronator drift and 'orbiting' (rapidly rotating tightly clenched fists around each other), where the weaker side moves slower and the stronger side orbits around it.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Always ask about recent head or facial trauma in a patient with a seventh nerve lesion ('don't miss the baseball bat').

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Numbness or tingling of the face implicates the fifth cranial nerve and is not expected in Bell's palsy, which is predominantly a motor problem.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: The House-Brackmann scale is the most well-validated grading scale for facial nerve dysfunction, using a 1 (normal) to 6 (total paralysis) scale to quantify subjective exam findings and track before-and-after treatment.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: The mainstay of Bell's palsy treatment is oral prednisone, typically 60 to 80 mg per day for roughly 5 days then tapered, with maximum benefit when started within 72 hours of onset.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Antiviral therapy (valacyclovir) shows no benefit versus placebo alone, but may add a small, questionable benefit when combined with prednisone; consider adding it for severe cases or higher-risk patients (elderly, pregnant, immunocompromised).

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Bell's palsy prognosis is generally favorable: about 70% show some improvement within 3 weeks, ~75% have significant improvement by 3-6 months, 85% recover completely within a year, with a roughly 7-8% recurrence rate.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Red flags that argue against Bell's palsy and warrant further workup include gradual onset beyond 72 hours, vertigo, hearing loss or tinnitus, zero improvement at 3 months, bilateral palsy, other cranial nerve involvement, limb weakness, otitis media, vesicles, facial swelling, rash, HIV risk factors, and history of systemic cancer.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: In evaluating atypical facial palsy, a non-contrast CT only rules out a head bleed and possibly severe otitis media; nearly everything else in the differential requires MRI (with contrast, or an internal auditory canal protocol).

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: EMG and nerve conduction studies are painful and generally not recommended for the diagnosis of facial palsy; their only real utility is late prognostication of nerve health months out.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Patients with impaired eyelid closure are at risk of exposure keratopathy; protect the cornea with lubricating eye drops or ointments and consider an eye patch or taping the eye closed, especially during sleep.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: For persistent neuropathic ear pain (otalgia) after Bell's palsy, anti-epileptics with neuropathic pain properties such as carbamazepine and phenytoin may have stronger evidence and be less sedating than gabapentin or TCAs.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: A large portion of the differential for a seventh nerve lesion consists of steroid-responsive conditions, so worsening despite standard treatment may warrant escalating steroids after excluding other causes.

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Chronic residual Bell's palsy can produce synkinesis (unwanted co-activation of facial muscles) and 'crocodile tears' (tearing during activation of the seventh nerve, e.g. smiling); Botox may be used to reduce unwanted contractions.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: Finerenone is a non-steroidal MRA that caused more hyperkalemia than placebo and did not show the renal benefit seen in prior finerenone trials for heart failure.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: The CDC updated its guidance to recommend pneumococcal vaccination for all adults aged 50 and older (previously 65), with options of PCV20, PCV21, or PCV15 followed by PPSV23 one year later.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: Polysaccharide vaccines (PPSV23) do not activate T cells as well as conjugate vaccines, providing less durable immunity, so younger vaccinated patients may need revaccination roughly every five years.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
