statement: In the STRIDE-3 trial, PCV21 was compared to PCV20 and covers 11 new serotypes; PCV21 covers about 85% of the strains that cause invasive pneumococcal disease in adults.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: PCV21 omits coverage of serotype 4, which is more common in the Western US including some Native American reservations, so PCV20/PCV15/PCV13 may be preferred for at-risk patients (e.g., those with unstable housing) in those regions.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: Current guidance is that patients can receive either PCV20 or PCV21 (not both), and there is no revaccination recommendation at this time for people over 65.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: The REDOX trial's non-superiority margin and required sample size were changed due to slow enrollment (margin from >0.83 to >0.67, sample size reduced ~10-fold), biasing the study toward equivalence and limiting its practice-changing conclusions.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: The 2013 pooled cohort equations overestimated 10-year cardiovascular risk by an average of about 20%, most notably among Black adults.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: Applying the newer PREVENT equations instead of the pooled cohort equations would result in an estimated 17.3 million adults no longer being recommended for a statin.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: A coronary artery calcium score (usually under $300, sometimes under $100 out of pocket) can help personalize risk for patients on the fence about starting a statin.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: Checking lipoprotein(a) at least once is recommended in many guidelines for all adults to help with cardiovascular risk stratification.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Use ALT upper limits of ~20 for women and ~30 for men, since lab-reported 'normal' values are derived from population medians that often include people with liver disease.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: An AST higher than the ALT should raise concern for chronic alcohol use or cirrhosis, since AST can rise as liver disease progresses.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Phosphatidylethanol (PEth) is effectively the 'A1C of alcohol,' reflecting blood alcohol exposure over the last few weeks; counsel patients transparently before ordering it.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: A PEth cutoff of 20 captures significant alcohol use; daily one-to-two drinks yields roughly 20-50, and false positives at high levels (e.g., 500) are effectively case-reportable.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Ethyl glucuronide is a urine metabolite of ethanol that captures alcohol consumption within the last 3 to 5 days, a shorter window than PEth.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: The FIB-4 (age, platelets, ALT/AST) risk-stratifies for advanced fibrosis: >2.67 is higher risk, <1.3 is very low risk, and for patients over 65 the low-risk cutoff is <2.0 rather than <1.3.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: For patients over age 65, the FIB-4 low-risk cutoff should be less than 2.0, not less than 1.3, to avoid over-referring older patients.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: The most common cause of an elevated ferritin in the United States is MASLD, not hemochromatosis, because the inflamed liver raises ferritin.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: The platelet count is the most important test in liver disease work-up because it forms the core of FIB-4 and informs the prognosis regarding advanced fibrosis.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: A positive FIB-4 cannot diagnose cirrhosis by itself; because pre-test probability of cirrhosis is only ~3% even in diabetics, sequential testing (next being transient elastography/FibroScan) is needed.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Transient elastography (FibroScan) has replaced liver biopsy for diagnosing advanced fibrosis; it has good negative predictive value but middling positive predictive value because inflammation, right heart congestion, or a recent fatty meal can transiently stiffen the liver.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: As BMI rises above 40, transient elastography failure rate increases due to fixed measurement depth, so magnetic resonance elastography is preferred in high-BMI patients.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Use the 'rule of fives' for liver stiffness on FibroScan: 5 is normal, 10 confers ~40% chance of advanced fibrosis, 15 makes cirrhosis very likely, 20 nearly definite, and 25 suggests portal hypertension/varices.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: MR elastography values are about a third to a quarter lower than transient/shear-wave values, so a 5 on MRE is very stiff (cirrhosis) whereas 5 on FibroScan is normal.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: A liver stiffness <20 with platelets >150 makes varices probability low enough to avoid screening endoscopy, while stiffness >25 is high enough to start beta blockers for portal hypertension without endoscopy.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Weight loss thresholds in fatty liver disease: ~5% reduces steatosis, ~7.5% shuts off inflammation (resolves NASH/MASH), and ~10% starts to improve fibrosis.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: GLP-1 receptor agonists are best positioned for patients who do not yet have cirrhosis (low FIB-4 or low-to-medium liver stiffness) where the main goal is weight loss.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Resmetirom is a thyroid mimetic and the first drug FDA-approved to reduce fibrosis more than placebo in a phase 3 trial, notably doing so without weight loss; it is best suited for patients with F3 fibrosis.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Statins should not be stopped for elevated liver enzymes in patients with steatotic liver disease, since the enzyme elevation is usually from the liver disease itself and these patients have cardiovascular indications for statins.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Vitamin E at 800 international units reduces ALT and inflammation in biopsy-proven steatohepatitis but is associated with a small increased risk of intracranial hemorrhage, particularly in women.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Metformin is not harmful to the liver and probably beneficial, so it should not be stopped over concerns it is causing liver disease.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: For suspected drug-induced liver injury, use livertox.gov (an NIH-maintained compendium) to look up whether a medication is associated with liver injury and its pattern.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Tamoxifen and amiodarone are classic medication causes of fatty (steatotic) liver injury.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Consider autoimmune hepatitis with an ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibody plus IgG when a patient has an ALT around 250, especially after excluding more common causes.

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Spider angiomas carry a likelihood ratio of about seven to nine for the presence of cirrhosis, allowing a clinical diagnosis in some patients before further testing.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: In the PREVENT trial, preventive PCI plus optimal medical therapy for non-flow-limiting (FFR > 0.8) vulnerable plaques reduced a composite outcome by an absolute 3% at 2 years, but this was driven by revascularization while death from any cause and MI were not significant.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: The FFR cutoff for flow-limiting coronary lesions is 0.8: values below 0.8 are considered flow limiting, and values above 0.8 are non-flow limiting.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: In the PREVENT trial the only significant component of the composite outcome was revascularization; death from any cause and MI confidence intervals crossed one, raising concern that open-label design and the placebo effect of procedures influenced the result.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: In the ANNEXA-I trial, andexanet alfa for DOAC-associated intracerebral hemorrhage improved hemostatic efficacy by an absolute 13% but caused an absolute 5% increase in thrombosis, mainly ischemic strokes, with no benefit in death or neurologic function at 30 days.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: The positive ANNEXA-I hemostatic efficacy result was driven by the hematoma volume change of less than 35%; there was no difference in NIH stroke score change or rescue therapy, and given the 5% increase in thrombosis the drug is not recommended in unselected patients.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: Andexanet alfa has a short half-life of about 4 hours, so some patients require multiple doses, though repeat dosing is not FDA indicated.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: In a phase 2 trial, aspirin 81 mg daily reduced hepatic fat by an absolute 6.6% versus a 3.6% gain with placebo over 6 months in patients with MASLD, independent of BMI or weight change.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: Fatty liver terminology has changed to metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), with NASH now referred to as MASH-related terminology.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: A retrospective study of veterans with cirrhosis, including patients with prior decompensation, found no cases of naltrexone-attributable drug-induced liver injury, supporting that naltrexone is safe in patients with liver disease.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: In patients with alcohol use disorder, liver enzymes often improve after starting naltrexone because it reduces drinking, so isolated enzyme elevations should not automatically be blamed on the drug.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: A target trial emulation using a Pip-Tazo shortage found Pip-Tazo associated with a 5% absolute increase in 90-day mortality versus cefepime in sepsis without an anaerobic indication, but confounding by indication (sicker patients receiving anaerobic coverage) threatens the result.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: The Pip-Tazo versus cefepime mortality difference emerged only later, with no difference at 14 days, consistent with the ACORN trial which found no mortality difference at 14 days.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: The best available evidence, including the ACORN trial, suggests cefepime causes more delirium and coma (neurotoxicity) than piperacillin-tazobactam.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: A regional decolonization initiative using chlorhexidine bathing plus nasal iodophor swabs in nursing homes/LTACs and targeted decolonization in hospitals reduced MDRO colonization, MDRO-positive cultures, infection-related hospitalizations, and cost.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: In the microplastics study, patients with carotid endarterectomy plaques containing microplastics/nanoplastics had higher rates of a composite of MI, stroke, or death, though causation versus a marker of exposure remains uncertain.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: The EPIC-SR trial of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) in standard-risk patients was negative, showing no reduction in duration of symptoms; interim data known since June 2022 were not published until April 2024.

episode_title: #445 DIGEST-Hotcakes: Preventive PCI, Andexanet Alfa for ICH, Aspirin for Fatty Liver, Naltrexone in Cirrhosis, Pivmecillinam for UTI, Cefepime vs Pip-Tazo, MDRO Decolonization, Microplastics and MACE, Pax-NO-vid
statement: The benefit of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir is now much lower than early in the pandemic due to changes in COVID-19 virulence and population immunity, and it is not a fully benign drug given drug-drug interactions, need for renal dosing, and rebound risk.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: The Glasgow-Blatchford bleeding score identifies high-risk upper GI bleed patients who may need medical interventions such as transfusion, endoscopy, or surgery.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: The Oakland score helps identify lower GI bleed patients appropriate for early discharge and outpatient diagnostic evaluation.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Bedside rounding has been shown to increase patient satisfaction and improve satisfaction with medical students, and when done correctly only minimally lengthens rounds (about 95 to 98 minutes).

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Converting to bedside rounds does not significantly lengthen rounds when done appropriately, with one study showing rounds increased from about 95 minutes to 98 minutes.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: For perioperative management of rheumatologic medications, disease-specific guidelines (2022-2023) exist for lupus, inflammatory arthropathies, and IBD; a dedicated website can output recommendations based on the medication, disease, and severity.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Three major cardiac societies (AHA, European Society, and Canadian Society) agree on checking postoperative troponins in vascular surgery patients.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: The MASCC score helps determine which neutropenic fever patients can be managed outpatient versus inpatient, but expert opinion recommends admitting any patient actively on chemotherapy regardless of the score.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: In neutropenic fever, IDSA and NCCN favor continuing broad-spectrum antibiotics until ANC recovers above 500, while the How Long study found antibiotics could be safely stopped in high-risk patients with negative blood cultures who were afebrile for 72 hours.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: For endocarditis, osteomyelitis, and bacteremia, oral antibiotics are largely just as effective as IV, and the historical preference for IV was based on data that never compared IV to oral therapy.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: In endocarditis, receiving an infectious disease consult alone was associated with significantly better 30-day mortality than receiving appropriate therapy alone, suggesting ID input adds value beyond antibiotic choice.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: In the CAPE COD trial, patients with severe community-acquired pneumonia treated with hydrocortisone 200 mg IV per day had lower intubation rates, lower need for pressors, and lower mortality (number needed to treat of 18) without increased GI bleeds or hospital-acquired infections, though they needed more insulin.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: In the PREVENT CLOT trial comparing aspirin to enoxaparin for post-op VTE prophylaxis, there was no difference in all-cause mortality or bleeding, but aspirin had higher rates of DVT; the population was predominantly young, male, healthy trauma-fracture patients.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: In transient atrial fibrillation occurring in the hospital, about one-third of patients had AFib over 12-month followup, and the AHA scientific statement notes a 5-year recurrence rate of 42-68%, supporting consideration of anticoagulation especially with high CHA2DS2-VASc scores.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Inpatient hypertension in non-cardiac patients should generally be left alone, and if treatment is used, IV therapy should be avoided because outcomes were even worse with IV.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: The updated ARDS definition (May 2023) is more inclusive, allowing diagnosis in non-intubated patients on high flow or non-invasive positive pressure with characteristic bilateral non-cardiogenic infiltrates, and permits diagnosis of pulmonary infiltrates via POCUS by a trained provider.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Per GOLD guidelines, any COPD patient seen in the hospital for an exacerbation qualifies as Group E (one exacerbation leading to hospitalization), meaning they should be on a LABA/LAMA, with an inhaled corticosteroid added if eosinophils exceed 300.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: In septic shock on pressors, adding fludrocortisone to standard hydrocortisone (equivalent of 200 mg/day) showed a benefit over hydrocortisone alone in a randomized controlled trial.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Acute left-sided uncomplicated colonic diverticulitis can be treated outpatient without antibiotics; complications include abscess, phlegmon, fistula, obstruction, bleeding, or perforation, and studies excluded immunosuppressed patients.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Updated ACG guidelines for acute lower GI bleeds recommend reversal of DOACs in life-threatening bleeds and resumption of anticoagulation within 7 days of stopping, even without definitive treatment, as it reduces VTE risk and mortality.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Incidental coronary artery calcium on CT (with or without contrast) should not be ignored; radiologists can score it, and with high scores there is data supporting statin and even aspirin therapy since MACE and mortality mirror patients with known CAD.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: For stable non-cardiac, non-active-GI-bleed patients, a transfusion threshold of hemoglobin 7 applies; before transfusing, stop unnecessary daily blood draws, investigate the cause of anemia, and clinically evaluate the patient for symptoms.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: IV iron should be considered over oral in patients who are constipated, have malabsorption, or need faster correction, and specifically benefits patients with heart failure, end-stage renal disease on dialysis, and malignancy with chemo-induced anemia.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: IV iron infusion reactions are complement-mediated rather than IgE-mediated, so treatment is hydrocortisone rather than diphenhydramine, and most reactions do not require epinephrine.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: Methylphenidate can be considered for apathetic palliative care patients based on the ADMIT trial, with improvements in scoring at two months sustained at six months, benefits that may not be apparent during the inpatient stay.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: When conducting goals of care conversations, explicitly signpost/prime the patient ("I am doing advance care planning") because studies show families often report never being asked about goals of care despite providers documenting clear conversations.

episode_title: #438 Recap from SHM #Converge24
statement: For post-acute care disposition, less than half of patients (about 45%) sent to a facility actually make it home; the remainder are readmitted or stay past 100 days, so consider holding a patient an extra day to make home work, as home health outcomes can equal SNF especially in dementia patients.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: The term 'atypical angina' should be avoided in favor of 'possible cardiac chest pain,' 'cardiac chest pain,' or 'non-cardiac chest pain' because 'atypical angina' is confusing.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Relief of chest pain with nitroglycerin does not reliably indicate cardiac ischemia; one study showed Nitro relieved chest pain in 35% of patients with ACS and 41% of patients without ACS.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: In patients with COPD who may also have asthma overlap, optimize inhaled corticosteroids rather than treating with LABA/LAMA alone.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Exhaled fractional nitric oxide testing is a non-invasive measure of eosinophilic inflammation with good positive predictive value for diagnosing asthma, but poor negative predictive value; smoking and inhaled corticosteroids lower nitric oxide levels.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: In patients with advanced COPD who have exertional hypoxia but not resting hypoxia, home oxygen does not improve quality of life or function.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: The 2023 ACP colorectal cancer screening guidelines suggest considering NOT screening asymptomatic average-risk people aged 45 to 49, citing minimal net benefit, differing from USPSTF guidelines.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: ACP guidelines do not recommend stool DNA testing, CT colonography, or serum screening tests for colorectal cancer screening, citing increased cost and reduced specificity compared to fecal occult blood testing, colonoscopy, or sigmoidoscopy.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Checkpoint inhibitor toxicities can occur even months to years after therapy; skin rash tends to occur earliest, followed by colitis (profound diarrhea) and endocrinopathies (thyroid, adrenal), and should be treated promptly.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: For suspected checkpoint inhibitor toxicity, if in doubt, treatment can be initiated with prednisone 1 mg/kg, since delaying treatment increases the risk of permanent toxicity, and many endocrinopathies are not reversible.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: The EAGLES trial showed varenicline and bupropion are safe and effective for smoking cessation in patients with psychiatric disorders, so a psychiatric diagnosis should not preclude their use.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Vivid dreams are often blamed on varenicline, but nicotine replacement therapy can also affect dreams.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: For e-cigarette (vaping) cessation, there is randomized evidence supporting varenicline plus counseling, as well as nicotine replacement therapy plus counseling.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: The 10,000-steps-per-day recommendation originated from a 1964 Japanese marketing campaign with no evidence basis; recent data show mortality and cardiovascular benefits plateau around 7,000-8,800 steps per day.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: As few as ~2,500 steps per day is associated with an 8% reduction in all-cause mortality, and benefits plateau, with roughly 8,763 steps giving a 60% reduction in all-cause mortality.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Vonoprazan, a potassium-competitive acid blocker (P-CAB), is more effective than PPIs for severe erosive esophagitis and has similar efficacy for typical GERD; it is currently approved for only 6 months of use, not continuous use.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Fezolinetant is a non-hormonal neurokinin receptor blocker for menopausal vasomotor symptoms; headache and transaminase elevations are common side effects, warranting LFT monitoring.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Gepirone is a 5-HT1A partial agonist (similar to buspirone) newly approved for depression; it may help patients who can't tolerate SSRIs, with dizziness and nausea most common and no weight gain, fatigue, or sexual side effects.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Cannabis has sympathomimetic properties and acts as a coronary vasoconstrictor and peripheral vasodilator; MI, coronary disease, and stroke risk are increased about 1.2 to 1.4 times versus non-use, roughly an order of magnitude less than tobacco smoking.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Preoperative cannabis use before elective surgery is thought to increase perioperative adverse cardiac outcomes more than fivefold, making cessation an easy lever to counsel patients on.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: For acute interstitial lung disease, treatment is often prednisone 40 mg for most subtypes, but in suspected scleroderma-related ILD, prednisone can precipitate a renal crisis.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Incidentally found interstitial lung abnormalities on CT (e.g., during lung cancer screening) merit follow-up scans (about 3 months, then 6 months, then annually) since they can progress; risk factors for progression include tobacco use, age over 60, and family history.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: APOL1-mediated kidney disease is a genetic cause of FSGS that is common in Black patients (linked to protection against African sleeping sickness), and the investigational drug inaxaplin reduced proteinuria by nearly 50% in APOL1 high-risk genotypes at 13 weeks.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Cystatin C-based eGFR does not depend on muscle mass; the most accurate eGFR equations use both creatinine and cystatin C, and it can help risk-stratify adults over 65 with CKD stage 3A (eGFR 45-59).

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: The normal testosterone reference range reported by labs is based on healthy young men, so a young man with a testosterone in that range has normal testosterone and does not need treatment.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Testosterone should be checked as a morning level (7 to 10 a.m.), preferably fasting.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: About 60% of eugonadal men who start testosterone therapy stop it within the first year.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Klinefelter syndrome (XXY karyotype) occurs in about 1 in 500 to 600 men, presenting as tall stature, gynecomastia, and testicular volume less than 4 cc (normal being 15 cc or more).

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Most MRSA infections, even in the hospitalized setting, are actually community-acquired rather than hospital-associated.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: The MRSA nasal swab has a high negative predictive value and can be used to de-escalate vancomycin, but it should not supersede clinical judgment in cases with high pretest probability such as cavitary or necrotic pneumonia.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: If a culture is tetracycline susceptible, it can be trusted to be doxycycline susceptible; but if tetracycline resistant, ask the lab for doxycycline or minocycline susceptibilities, as it may still be susceptible.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Do not order broad serum specific IgE allergy panels or total IgE testing; total IgE is not predictive of atopy, and specific IgE should be targeted to clinically relevant exposures.

episode_title: #437 Clinical Pearls ACP #IM2024
statement: Patients with a nonserious or unremembered penicillin reaction who were not hospitalized and had no systemic signs or severe skin reaction (e.g., SJS) can safely undergo a 250 mg oral amoxicillin challenge in the office with one hour of observation and an EpiPen available.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Recurrent UTI is defined as three culture-positive UTIs in one year or two in six months; many patients labeled as recurrent do not actually meet this definition.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Asymptomatic bacteriuria does not need to be treated unless the patient is pregnant or undergoing a urologic surgery.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: A classic uncomplicated UTI in a young woman with prior similar episodes can be treated empirically without urinalysis or culture, but when symptoms recur quickly (e.g., 6 weeks later) a culture should be sent.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: For accurate diagnosis and post-void residual measurement, a straight catheterization provides a clean urine specimen and avoids contamination or bladder scan inaccuracies in patients with high BMI or ascites.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Nitrofurantoin (Macrobid) is a reasonable first-line agent for uncomplicated UTI in healthy young patients but should be avoided in the elderly due to pulmonary fibrosis risk and is not suitable for systemic infection since it is bacteriostatic and stays local to the urine.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Nitrofurantoin should be avoided when a patient has systemic symptoms such as fever, because it is bacteriostatic and stays mostly local to the urine.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Fluoroquinolones should not be used for uncomplicated cystitis, bronchitis, or COPD exacerbations following the black box warning issued around 2016.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Drinking at least 1.5 liters of fluid per day has been shown to decrease UTI recurrence.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Treating constipation reduces UTI recurrence because bowel-bladder dysfunction and rectal stool burden increase infection risk; aim for a soft Bristol type 3-4 stool with water and fiber.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: For cranberry/D-mannose supplements, prefer pills over cranberry juice because the juice has high sugar content and can be bladder-irritating; the data are weak but there is a plausible mechanism of preventing bacterial adherence to the uroepithelium.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Vaginal estrogen cream has strong data for preventing recurrent UTI in perimenopausal and postmenopausal women by lowering vaginal pH and restoring lactobacillus, and it stays local rather than acting systemically.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Vaginal estrogen can generally be considered even in women with a history of breast cancer, but a subgroup analysis suggested women on aromatase inhibitors may have increased recurrence, so involve the oncologist in these decisions.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Options for antibiotic prophylaxis in recurrent UTI include daily suppressive dosing, postcoital prophylaxis, and self-start therapy; the speaker prefers suppressive dosing as a last resort and attempts to wean off within a year.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Methenamine hippurate was found non-inferior to daily antibiotic prophylaxis in the ALTAR trial, though the study had limitations; it remains a reasonable option to offer.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Uncomplicated (index) patients with recurrent UTI generally do not need upper tract imaging or cystoscopy; imaging is driven by red flags such as prior kidney stones, flank pain, hematuria, or smoking history.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: For imaging workup, renal ultrasound is a reasonable starting point, while a smoker with gross hematuria warrants a CT urogram with contrast and delayed imaging to evaluate for upper tract urothelial carcinoma.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Culture-negative urinary symptoms may be due to atypical organisms like Mycoplasma or Ureaplasma, detectable with atypical cultures or PCR/DNA tests, though these can overcall harmless bacteria like lactobacillus.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: The differential for urinary symptoms with unimpressive or negative cultures includes pelvic floor dysfunction, pelvic organ prolapse, painful bladder syndrome, overactive bladder, and genitourinary syndrome of menopause.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Bladder pain syndrome (interstitial cystitis) is a diagnosis of exclusion; only a few patients have Hunner ulcers on cystoscopy, and pain is characteristically relieved when the bladder is emptied.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: A pelvic exam is important in recurrent UTI evaluation and can reveal prolapse, atrophic vaginitis, urethral diverticulum, or pelvic floor tenderness; even without a speculum, inspect for atrophic vaginitis and assess pelvic floor tone.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Pelvic organ prolapse can present as recurrent UTI, and asking about a sensation of sitting on a golf ball or having to splint (using fingers to push to void) helps identify it.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Childbirth of a baby over eight pounds is a risk factor for pelvic floor dysfunction and pelvic organ prolapse, so obtaining OB history is useful.

episode_title: #434: Ur-INe for Recurrent UTI?! with Dr. Kellen Choi, DO AKA The Bladder Teacher
statement: Overdoing Kegel exercises can cause pelvic floor dysfunction with urgency, frequency, and dysuria; the correct Kegel targets the muscle used to avoid passing gas, not the belly.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: In an open-label RCT, adding e-cigarettes to standard smoking cessation counseling increased biochemically validated continuous abstinence at 6 months (28.9% vs 16.3%), though intervention patients were more likely to still be using nicotine at study end.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: A 2024 Cochrane living review found e-cigarettes with nicotine work better than standard nicotine replacement therapy and better than e-cigarettes without nicotine for smoking cessation, with adverse events largely limited to nausea and throat irritation.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: A systematic review found that switching from tobacco to e-cigarettes improved pulmonary outcomes (asthma, COPD) and oral disease, but showed no improvement in cardiovascular disease, stroke, or metabolic syndrome.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: In a Canadian cohort study, gabapentinoid use in patients with COPD was associated with about a 40% relative increase in risk of hospitalization for COPD exacerbation, an excess of roughly seven hospitalizations per 100 patients per year, regardless of indication.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: Gabapentin has been observed to cause respiratory issues in at least two settings: worsening respiratory parameters during sleep in obstructive sleep apnea (more hypopnea and hypoxemia) and increased respiratory depression when given perioperatively.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: A double-blind crossover RCT found topical 1.8% lidocaine was no better than placebo for mechanical neck pain, with only about a 0.5-point difference on a 10-point scale, below the pre-specified 1-point threshold.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: Lidocaine patches are officially approved for post-herpetic neuralgia, which is where the strongest evidence lies, making neuropathic pain the best-supported indication.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: In a case-control study of community-acquired C. diff, clindamycin and later-generation cephalosporins carried the highest risk (odds ratios ~10-20), fluoroquinolones and amoxicillin-clavulanic acid moderate risk (OR 5-10), sulfonamides and macrolides lower risk (OR 1-3), and tetracyclines minimal or no risk.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: In the C. diff case-control study, minocycline had a significantly protective odds ratio, suggesting a possible role beyond simply being low-risk.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: The association between antibiotics and C. diff weakened as the exposure window lengthened, so risk assessment should focus on antibiotics received within a short window (about 30 days) before the C. diff diagnosis.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: Semaglutide multi-dose pens can be 'dosed by clicks' during shortages; approximately 74 clicks delivers the full 1 mg dose, so 37 clicks gives roughly half and 18 clicks about a quarter dose.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: Dosing by clicks is only possible with the multi-dose semaglutide pen; dulaglutide and tirzepatide pens are single-dose and cannot be dosed this way.

episode_title: #432 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes for smoking cessation, Gabapentin and COPD exacerbations, Lidocaine for neck pain, C diff risk by antibiotic type, and “dosing by clicks” for GLP1’s
statement: Gabapentin is a renally dosed medication frequently used off-label in multimorbid patients whose renal function declines while the dose stays unchanged, creating substantial potential for toxicity.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: The Target-TP trial used fibrinogen and D-dimer to risk-stratify outpatient cancer patients (lung and GI) starting chemotherapy, and prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin) significantly reduced thromboembolism in the high-risk cohort.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: In Target-TP, randomizing high-risk patients to enoxaparin brought their thromboembolism risk down toward the low-risk cohort incidence, and also showed a significant survival benefit not previously demonstrated in other trials.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: In Target-TP, the Khorana score did not stratify VTE risk well in this population, whereas the fibrinogen and D-dimer combination better predicted who was high versus low risk.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: Guidelines since 2019 recommend considering VTE prophylaxis for cancer patients who are high-risk by the Khorana score during chemotherapy, but this is not routinely done in the outpatient setting.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: For outpatient cancer VTE prophylaxis with DOACs, the data is strongest with apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily, with rivaroxaban 10 mg as an alternative.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: In the STARR2 trial, using peripheral eosinophilia to guide who gets systemic steroids for COPD exacerbations was non-inferior to giving steroids to all suspected exacerbation patients.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: The STARR2 trial used prednisolone 30 mg for 14 days (about 420 mg total), more than twice the typical US course of prednisone 40 mg for 5 days (200 mg total).

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: In STARR2, patients with eosinophilia treated with steroids had fewer treatment failures than those with eosinophilia not given steroids, while steroid treatment made no difference in patients without eosinophilia.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: Antibiotics for COPD exacerbations should generally be reserved for patients meeting cardinal symptom criteria (increased sputum purulence, increased dyspnea, or increased sputum volume — two of three), rather than shotgunning everyone.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: Per the most recent ACC/AHA guidelines, device-detected subclinical atrial fibrillation (atrial high-rate episodes) lasting less than five minutes does not require action.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: Decisions to anticoagulate subclinical AFib are driven by AFib burden (frequency and duration) and stroke risk, with CHA2DS2-VASc above 2 for men or 3 for women placing patients in a higher-risk group.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: In NOAH-AFNET (edoxaban) the trial was stopped early for major bleeding without stroke reduction, whereas ARTESiA (apixaban vs aspirin) did reduce stroke but with more bleeding.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: In ARTESiA, the number needed to treat was about 217 and the number needed to harm was 130, but apixaban cut the risk of disabling stroke roughly in half.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: Subclinical AFib carries a lower stroke risk (about 1% per year) compared with roughly 3% annual risk for clinical AFib patients with a comparable score.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: Updated ASCO/SSO guidelines recommend offering germline BRCA1 and BRCA2 testing to all newly diagnosed breast cancer patients aged 65 or under.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: BRCA testing for breast cancer risk in this guideline refers to germline (inherited) testing, distinct from somatic mutations found in tumor tissue.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: Direct-to-consumer BRCA tests may not include all clinically important variants, sometimes only testing Ashkenazi Jewish founder variants, so testing guided by a genetic counselor is preferred.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: The FDA granted marketing authorization for the first at-home over-the-counter test for chlamydia and gonorrhea, using vaginal swabs and urine specimens with results returned online.

episode_title: #428 DIGEST: DVT prophylaxis in patients with cancer, anticoagulation in subclinical AFib, steroids in COPD exacerbations, breast cancer genetic testing guidelines, and at-home STI testing
statement: At-home STI testing improves access and reduces stigma but risks missing counseling opportunities such as discussing PrEP, since a diagnosis of bacterial STI in the past six months is an indication to discuss PrEP.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Any major orthopedic surgery is considered high bleeding risk, conferring a greater than 2% 30-day risk of bleeding, along with abdominal, cardiac, neurosurgical, and thoracic surgeries.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: High thromboembolic risk is defined as greater than 10% risk of any arterial or venous thromboembolic event per month; high-risk conditions include antiphospholipid syndrome, protein C/S deficiency, antithrombin deficiency, homozygous factor V Leiden, recent stroke or VTE within 3 months, and mechanical valves with caged-ball or tilted-disc designs.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: A three-bucket framework guides periprocedural management: low bleeding risk procedures continue anticoagulation uninterrupted; moderate/high bleeding risk with moderate thromboembolic risk hold anticoagulation without bridging; and moderate/high bleeding risk with high thromboembolic risk interrupt anticoagulation with bridging.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: A HAS-BLED score greater than 3 suggests an increased risk of bleeding.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: The 2015 BRIDGE trial (non-inferiority) showed that forgoing bridging anticoagulation did not alter rates of thromboembolic events but decreased major adverse bleeding; overall in AFib patients with CHA2DS2-VASc less than six, bridging is probably not needed.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: DOACs have a short half-life compared to warfarin, so bridging is generally unnecessary unless the timing of surgery is unknown.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: IV heparin has a slightly increased bleeding risk compared to low molecular weight heparin, but its short half-life allows quick on/off control; therapeutic enoxaparin requires a 24-hour hold before surgery.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Enoxaparin cannot be used for bridging in patients with end-stage renal disease or creatinine clearance less than 30, where IV heparin is preferred.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: For DOACs, a 48-hour hold is sufficient for moderate-to-high bleeding risk procedures, though some guidelines opt for 72 hours; dabigatran requires a 4-day hold in patients with creatinine clearance less than 50.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: The American Society of Regional Anesthesia guidelines call for a 72-hour hold of most DOACs (apixaban and rivaroxaban) in patients receiving neuraxial anesthesia such as epidurals.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Lumbar punctures are considered moderate to high risk for bleeding due to the more devastating ramifications of a bleed.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Elective surgery should ideally be delayed after a recent stroke, VTE within three months, new-onset AFib, or acute intracardiac thrombus; unprovoked DVT/PE has higher recurrence (hazard ratio 2.3) and within the first month VTE recurrence risk can be as high as 40%.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: For elective surgery after stroke, waiting six months is ideal but three months is acceptable, since the difference in stroke recurrence between three and six months is less significant than between one and three months.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: For urgent/emergent surgery in warfarin-anticoagulated patients, reversal can be achieved with IV vitamin K and four-factor PCC.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: The ideal warfarin management is to hold it for 5 days before surgery, allow INR to normalize to 1–1.2, and bridge with enoxaparin 1 mg/kg every 12 hours starting 36 hours after the last warfarin dose, holding it 24 hours before surgery.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: With a supratherapeutic INR, warfarin can be held for 7 days before surgery; a subtherapeutic INR does not change the 5-day hold since bridged patients will be anticoagulated regardless.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: In the inpatient setting, IV heparin can be started as soon as the INR drops below 2, and if the INR remains high the day before surgery, IV vitamin K or FFP can be given to minimize surgical bleeding risk.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Warfarin can be restarted at the home dose 24 hours after surgery once hemostasis is achieved, with concomitant restart of the bridging heparin or enoxaparin.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: DOACs should be restarted more than 24 hours after surgery because they lack an easily accessible or cheap reversal agent.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Aspirin for secondary prevention can almost always be continued perioperatively except for neurosurgical procedures, certain urological cases, and posterior chamber ophthalmic cases requiring a retrobulbar block.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Aspirin used for primary prevention is generally held before high bleeding risk surgeries, based on POISE-2 data showing aspirin did not reduce death or MI but increased bleeding risk.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Clopidogrel should be stopped 5 days before surgery; P2Y12 monotherapy patients (e.g., for recurrent strokes) can be transitioned to aspirin only during the perioperative period.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: P2Y12 inhibitors can be restarted greater than 48 hours after surgery, while aspirin (reversible) can be restarted within 12 hours after surgery.

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: For elective surgery, wait 6 weeks after bare metal stent placement and a minimum of 6 months after drug-eluting stent placement, since major adverse cardiovascular outcomes are highest in the first 6 weeks and plateau after 6 months.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In a NHANES sample of US females aged 12-21, using a ferritin cutoff of 25, about 40% were iron deficient, and using a cutoff of 50, about 75% were iron deficient, yet only 6% were anemic.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Among iron-deficient young women, nearly 85% had a normal hemoglobin, raising the question of whether hemoglobin is an adequate screening tool for iron deficiency.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Randomized trials in the 2020s show no clear benefit of every-other-day versus daily oral iron dosing for treating anemia, including on side effects and hepcidin levels; either strategy is reasonable, though every-other-day may raise iron levels more slowly.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Corrected calcium (Payne's formula) performs worse the lower the albumin, which is exactly the setting where you would want it to perform well.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Uncorrected total calcium correlates with ionized (free) calcium only 70-80% of the time, and corrected calcium performs even worse; if worried about a calcium disorder, use the uncorrected value or obtain an ionized calcium.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Ionized (free) calcium samples are sensitive to temperature, air, and pH, and should be run within about 30 minutes; if delayed, place the sample in an ice slurry.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In an observational cohort, nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) was associated with significantly more virologic rebound than no treatment, about 20% versus 2%.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In this rebound study, no one was hospitalized or died from virologic rebound, and only about half of patients with rebound had symptoms.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: NIH guidelines state that recurrence of COVID symptoms and viral detection after Paxlovid treatment has not been associated with progression to severe disease, and recommend against using concern about rebound as a reason to avoid treatment in high-risk patients.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In the standard-risk group, Paxlovid did not shorten the duration of symptoms (an unpublished trial known only from a press release), while it does prevent hospitalization and death in high-risk patients.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: An FDA advisory committee recommended removing oral phenylephrine from the OTC 'generally recognized as safe and effective' monograph because it has been convincingly shown to be ineffective, largely due to poor bioavailability from deactivation in the gut and liver.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: For nasal/sinus symptoms, topical decongestants such as oxymetazoline (Afrin), intranasal phenylephrine, and saline irrigation work better and are safer than oral phenylephrine.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In the MINT trial of acute MI and anemia, the primary outcome of death or MI at 30 days occurred in 16.9% (restrictive, transfuse <7) versus 14.5% (liberal, transfuse <10), a non-significant result (rate ratio 1.15, CI 0.99-1.34, p=0.07).

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In the MINT trial subgroup of type 1 MI (plaque rupture/vessel occlusion), a liberal transfusion strategy showed benefit with a rate ratio of 1.32, an absolute risk reduction in death or MI of 4.4%, and a number needed to treat of 23 over 30 days.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Despite MINT being a negative/inconclusive primary trial, UpToDate authors now recommend a liberal transfusion strategy for acute MI with anemia, whereas prior guidelines took no stance due to lack of evidence.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In MINT, a liberal transfusion strategy required about three and a half times as many units of blood as the restrictive strategy, and transfusion reactions (TACO and febrile non-hemolytic reactions) were more common in the liberal group.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Sustained tooth pain that wakes a patient from sleep indicates infection has reached the neurovascular bundle (pulp), meaning the tooth will need a root canal or extraction rather than a filling.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For a well-appearing patient with stable vital signs and focal dental pain, avoid ordering a CBC or CT face; a dental x-ray (or panorex) is more appropriate and useful to the treating dentist.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Tooth extraction is much cheaper than a root canal (low hundreds vs often several thousand dollars), and in most states adult Medicaid covers extractions but not root canals.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For acute dental pain, a 2% lidocaine injection near the tooth roots (using a 25- or 27-gauge needle) can provide immediate local anesthesia; avoid injecting directly into an abscess as this worsens pain.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For short-term dental pain control, ibuprofen 800 mg TID plus up to 3 g of acetaminophen daily is recommended, and this combination gives pain control equivalent to opioids without the risks.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Opioids should generally be avoided for dental pain since ibuprofen and acetaminophen give equivalent control and opioids pose risks to patients and their families.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For a dental infection in a primary care patient without dental access, prescribing an antibiotic is a form of harm reduction that buys roughly 4-6 weeks of pain control, though it does not cure the infection without source control (dental treatment).

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: First-line antibiotics for a routine dental infection are penicillin VK or amoxicillin (augmentin is unnecessary); clindamycin is second-line for penicillin-allergic patients but carries C. diff risk.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Clindamycin is the recommended second-line agent for penicillin-allergic patients with dental infection, so the penicillin allergy should be confirmed given the C. diff risk.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Ludwig's angina is a true dental emergency involving infection spreading around the airway (often from a mandibular molar), presenting with trismus, tripoding, and marked submandibular swelling, and requires an immediate surgical airway.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a dental emergency, often arising from a canine tooth root, causing ascending infection with intracranial thrombosis, focal neurologic deficits, nasolabial fold flattening, and difficulty fully opening the eye.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Dry socket typically presents 3-4 days after extraction with worsening pain after initial improvement, is not an infection, will not respond to antibiotics, and is treated with topical measures such as iodoform gauze.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Frequency of eating acidifies the mouth, so constant sipping of sugary drinks all day increases caries risk; sugar-sweetened beverage decay appears near the gumline across all teeth.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Plain seltzer/carbonated water is generally fine for teeth because the main driver of decay is sugar being fermented into acid by bacteria, not the carbonic acid of carbonation.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Xerostomia can be caused by Sjogren's, smoking, anticholinergic medications (e.g., amitriptyline, antihistamines), and especially polypharmacy in older patients, warranting consideration of de-prescribing.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Dry mouth patients are at higher caries risk (decay near the gumline) due to lack of basic saliva; management ranges from frequent water sips, Biotene gels/rinses, and sugar-free candy up to cholinergic agents like pilocarpine, which is poorly tolerated due to severe sweating.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Gingivitis is inflammation of the gums that can be cleared with improved oral hygiene and needs no specialized treatment; higher estrogen levels (pregnancy, puberty) are associated with transiently increased gingivitis.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Gingival hyperplasia is most commonly caused by calcium channel blockers (e.g., nifedipine) and anti-epileptic drugs; it is painless and enlarged, and can regress with dose reduction or stopping the medication.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For suspected TMJ dysfunction, a $20 over-the-counter night guard is as effective as the $200 dentist version, and jaw physical therapy exercises (progressively stretching mouth opening) plus heat/cold therapy can genuinely help.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For patients on oral bisphosphonates for osteoporosis, the risk of osteonecrosis of the jaw is low and only relevant to invasive procedures like extractions; do not delay starting or withhold a needed bisphosphonate while waiting for often-inaccessible dental clearance.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: A bisphosphonate drug holiday before dental procedures is pointless because bisphosphonates have a half-life of about 6 years; patients should continue taking them as prescribed, and fillings/cleanings do not increase osteonecrosis risk.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For most patients on anticoagulants or antiplatelets undergoing dental procedures, do not hold the medication; the clotting risk outweighs bleeding risk, and localized bleeding can be controlled with local hemostasis measures.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For patients on warfarin, an INR should be checked within 48-72 hours before a dental procedure to ensure it is not supertherapeutic.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Endocarditis antibiotic prophylaxis before dental work is indicated only for a narrow group: prosthetic cardiac valve, prosthetic material in valve repair, prior endocarditis, cardiac transplant with valvular regurgitation, or unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: There are no guidelines recommending antibiotic prophylaxis before dental work for patients with joint replacements, and it is almost certainly unnecessary and not recommended.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Patients on methadone or buprenorphine for opioid use disorder are at higher risk of dental decay, but this is likely due to prior substance use and lifestyle rather than the medications themselves; counsel supportively and prioritize getting them dental access.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: A subacute or chronic tooth abscess may drain via a sinus tract that fistulizes to the gum surface, appearing as a bubble or 'zit' whose popping relieves pain and produces a bad taste from pus.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In patients with HFpEF and obesity, weekly semaglutide improved heart failure symptoms (KCCQ-CSS) and produced greater weight loss versus placebo, though the trial was not powered for hard outcomes like heart failure hospitalization or death.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the STEP-HFpEF trial, the between-group difference in KCCQ-CSS was 7.8 points, which falls within the range considered clinically significant for HFpEF.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the ATLANTIS trial, low-dose amitriptyline (started at 10 mg, titrated up to 30 mg) for IBS as second-line therapy in primary care showed a statistically significant but not clinically significant benefit, with a between-group difference of 27 points on the IBS-SSS (they expected 35+ for clinical significance).

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the ATLANTIS trial, baseline depression had no impact on amitriptyline treatment response and treatment did not improve depression scores, supporting the concept that TCAs work as neuromodulators reducing visceral hypersensitivity rather than via antidepressant effects.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: First-line therapies to try before second-line amitriptyline in IBS include laxatives, dietary changes, antispasmodics, and peppermint oil.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the HOST-EXAM trial, clopidogrel 75 mg monotherapy after DAPT following PCI had a lower rate of the primary composite endpoint (5.7% vs 7.7%) than aspirin 100 mg monotherapy, with a hazard ratio of 0.73.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In a post hoc analysis of HOST-EXAM, clopidogrel outperformed aspirin monotherapy across all risk strata (high and low ischemic and bleeding risk), including fewer hemorrhagic strokes with clopidogrel.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: Genotype-guided selection of clopidogrel (assessing loss-of-function mutations) did not improve outcomes in the TAILOR-PCI trial, so routine genetic testing before prescribing clopidogrel is not necessary.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the REPRIEVE trial, pitavastatin 4 mg daily for primary prevention in low-to-moderate risk adults with HIV on ART reduced major adverse cardiovascular events (4.81 vs 7.32 per 1,000 person-years), with a number needed to treat of 106; the trial was stopped early for efficacy.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: Pitavastatin was chosen for the REPRIEVE trial because it lacks the drug interactions with common antiretroviral therapy that many other statins have.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: Patients with HIV have elevated cardiovascular risk even with suppressed viral loads on ART, possibly due to chronic immune activation, and the pooled cohort equation used in REPRIEVE was not derived in HIV patients.
