episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: Cannabis is a hyperaccumulator of heavy metals and is a source of lead and cadmium exposure; exclusive cannabis users had higher cadmium and lead levels than non-users, with levels higher the more recently cannabis was used.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: Using a blood pressure cuff that is too small for a patient's arm significantly overestimates blood pressure by close to 20 mmHg, potentially misclassifying patients as hypertensive.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: Using a cuff that is too large reads slightly low compared to true blood pressure, by about 3 mmHg.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: The RSV prefusion F vaccine in older adults showed efficacy of about 67% for preventing infection with two or more symptoms and 86% for infection with three or more symptoms.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: The RSV vaccine is approved for adults over 60 through shared decision-making rather than as an outright recommendation, taking individual patient characteristics into account.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: The pivotal RSV vaccine trial was an interim analysis that did not demonstrate reduction in hospitalizations or deaths, only prevention of symptomatic RSV lower respiratory tract illness.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: The RSV vaccine trial enrolled mostly patients aged 60-69 with few over 80 and excluded immunocompromised patients, so its benefit in the sickest patients who need it most remains uncertain.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: In the FRAIL-AF trial, switching frail elderly atrial fibrillation patients who were stable on a vitamin K antagonist to a DOAC was associated with more bleeding without better stroke prevention, and the trial was stopped early for futility.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: In FRAIL-AF, bleeding at one year occurred in 15.3% of patients switched to a DOAC versus a lower rate in those who stayed on a vitamin K antagonist, with a hazard ratio of 1.69.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: For a frail elderly patient doing well on warfarin with acceptable time in therapeutic range, there is no need to switch to a DOAC, but starting or switching to a DOAC may still be appropriate for patients with labile INRs or who tolerate warfarin poorly.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: Cotton fever is a diagnosis of exclusion in people who inject drugs, presenting with fever and leukocytosis about 6-12 hours after injection and mimicking sepsis, with symptoms resolving on their own.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: Gastric belching is less frequent but more forceful and can be associated with GERD (treatable with a PPI), while supragastric belching is more frequent, goes away with distraction or sleep, and responds to diaphragmatic breathing.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: Both belching and abdominal bloating/distension can respond to diaphragmatic breathing and central neuromodulators such as TCAs or SNRIs, which decrease visceral hypersensitivity.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: Abdominal bloating and distension warrant evaluation for constipation, food allergy or intolerance, celiac disease, non-celiac gluten sensitivity, SIBO, and motility disorders.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: In the ACORN trial, piperacillin-tazobactam alone or with vancomycin was not associated with acute kidney injury compared to cefepime, but cefepime patients had slightly fewer days alive without coma or delirium.

episode_title: #414 Spooky Cakes: VKA vs DOACs in Frail Adults, RSV Vaccine, Cuff Size Matters, Bloating & Belching, AKI & Pip-Tazo
statement: In the ACORN trial there were no differences in severe AKI, death, or major adverse kidney outcomes at 14 days between cefepime and piperacillin-tazobactam.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: In the CLEAR Outcomes trial, bempedoic acid lowered LDL by about 28% in patients not on a statin and by less when added to maximally tolerated statin therapy, and it also lowers CRP.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: In CLEAR Outcomes, the reduction in major adverse cardiac events was mostly driven by non-fatal/fatal MI and coronary revascularization, without a proven reduction in cardiovascular death or all-cause mortality.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Bempedoic acid did not show an increased risk of diabetes or muscle symptoms like myalgias, unlike statins, but was associated with increased gout risk.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Bempedoic acid can raise levels of simvastatin and pravastatin, so patients should not take more than 20 mg of simvastatin or 40 mg of pravastatin while on it, and it should not be combined with fibrates due to cholelithiasis concern.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Bempedoic acid is best positioned for high-risk patients who are truly statin intolerant after trying dose reduction, decreased frequency, and agent changes, and who cannot or will not use an injectable PCSK9 inhibitor.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: The primary prevention subgroup findings for bempedoic acid (including a mortality benefit) should be interpreted cautiously, as numerous pre-specified subgroups and post hoc endpoints make false positive associations very likely.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: The CANVAS pragmatic trial supports using any available DOAC over low molecular weight heparin for treating VTE in cancer patients, as a non-inferiority trial with no major differences in major bleeding.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: In CANVAS, apixaban was prescribed in approximately 60% of DOAC cases, and apixaban is thought to have a slightly better bleeding profile than rivaroxaban or edoxaban.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Low molecular weight heparin remains an option for cancer-associated VTE but has lower adherence than DOACs, partly due to being injectable.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Cytisinicline, a plant-based partial agonist at the alpha-4 beta-2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, produced higher smoking abstinence rates than placebo (about 25.3% vs 4.4% at weeks 3-6 of the six-week course).

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Cytisinicline studies excluded non-cigarette tobacco users, so it was studied only for cigarette smoking, not chewing tobacco or vaping.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Alpha-gal syndrome is a tick-associated mammalian meat allergy with delayed symptoms occurring roughly 2 to 8 hours after exposure, unlike the rapid onset of a typical food allergy.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Alpha-gal syndrome can be triggered not only by mammalian meats but also by dairy, medications like cetuximab, gelatin, and possibly porcine heart valves.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Alpha-gal is managed mainly by avoidance: about 80% of patients do well avoiding mammalian meats, another 15% must also avoid dairy, and about 5% must avoid gelatin-containing foods.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Serum IgE testing for alpha-gal has better sensitivity when there is clinical suspicion for the disease, so awareness that the condition exists is key to diagnosis.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Orforglipron, an oral non-peptide GLP-1 receptor agonist, produced dose-dependent weight loss of roughly 8-13% (around 10%) in patients with obesity/overweight and a comorbidity but without diabetes.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: In the Trailblazer ALZ-2 trial, donanemab produced a small absolute benefit of about 3 points on a 144-point dementia scale, but 18% more patients (47% vs 29%) had no clinical progression at one year even without indefinite treatment.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Donanemab allowed treatment to be stopped once serial MRI showed sufficient plaque removal, making it the first of these drugs to show halting of progression with a time-limited course.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Zuranolone is the first oral medication to receive FDA approval for postpartum depression, dosed at 50 mg once daily for 14 days.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: In the Skylark trial, zuranolone reduced Hamilton depression scores by about 15.6 points from a baseline near 29, versus 11.6 points with placebo, with benefit persisting through day 45, though most patients did not reach remission (score <7).

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: Zuranolone can cause somnolence, dizziness, and sedation that may warrant dose reduction, and breastfeeding is not recommended during treatment as safety is unknown.

episode_title: #407 DIGEST: Bempedoic acid and cardiac risk, Alpha-Gal Syndrome, Orforglipron, Zuranolone, DOACs for VTE of Malignancy, mAbs for Dementia, New Med for Smoking Cessation, Kiwis and Constipation
statement: In a crossover trial, eating two green kiwifruit daily improved constipation-related symptoms and complete spontaneous bowel movements (by about 1.5 per week) across all groups, whereas psyllium only improved symptoms in the IBS-constipation group.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: Syphilis rates have risen substantially over the past 20 years, from about 6,000 cases in 2000 to nearly 40,000 in recent years.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: The USPSTF recommends screening for syphilis in non-pregnant adolescents and adults who have ever been sexually active and are at increased risk.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: Increased-risk groups for syphilis include men who have sex with men, those with HIV or other STIs, people who use illicit drugs, and people with a history of incarceration, sex work, or military service.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: Clinicians should also consider community prevalence, using CDC state rates and the local health department as resources; the Southern U.S. has the largest proportion of syphilis cases.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: There is no specific prevalence threshold defining high risk, and the USPSTF is calling for more evidence on primary care risk assessment tools.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: The CDC recommends screening at least annually in sexually active men who have sex with men, and the IDSA has specific guidelines for the HIV population.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: The USPSTF did not find sufficient evidence for rapid point-of-care testing compared with laboratory testing and does not recommend point-of-care testing.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: Potential harms of screening include false-positive tests requiring clinical follow-up and unnecessary patient anxiety; focusing on increased-risk patients helps counterbalance these harms.

episode_title: #364 Screening for syphilis: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Katrina Donahue
statement: There is no concrete recommended screening interval; the USPSTF notes more evidence is needed on optimal screening intervals for high-risk populations.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: The PLUS trial (~5000 ICU patients, double-blind RCT) found no significant difference between Plasma-Lyte 148 and normal saline for 90-day mortality, initiation of renal replacement therapy, or increases in creatinine.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: In the PLUS trial, death within 90 days occurred in 21.8% of the balanced solution group and 22% of the saline group, with a point estimate close to zero, making an underpowered false-negative less likely.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: The PLUS trial had substantial differential crossover: about 63% of patients in the balanced electrolyte group received at least a half liter of saline versus about 4% of the saline group receiving balanced electrolytes, which could bias toward a negative result.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: In an RCT of adults with overweight and short sleep (<6.5 hours/night), a sleep-hygiene counseling intervention increased sleep by about 1.2 hours, reduced energy intake by about 270 kcal/day, and produced weight loss compared with controls.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: Universal hepatitis B vaccination is now recommended for adults aged 19 to 59, and for those 60 and older with comorbidities/risk factors (or on request).

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: New pneumococcal recommendations use PCV20 or PCV15 and retire PCV13, while PPSV23 remains; vaccinate immunocompromised or comorbid adults 19-64 and all adults 65 and older.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: For pneumococcal vaccination you can either give a single dose of PCV20, or give PCV15 followed one year later by PPSV23.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: In the VITAL ancillary trial (~25,000 older adults, median 5-year follow-up), vitamin D with or without omega-3s produced about a 20% relative reduction in newly diagnosed autoimmune diseases.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: Despite a positive relative reduction, the vitamin D autoimmune-disease result had an absolute risk reduction of about 0.03% and a number needed to treat of roughly 700 over 5 years, suggesting any real effect is very small.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: When appraising trials, verify primary outcomes against the ClinicalTrials.gov registry, since studies reusing the same patients for different outcomes risk a type I error (false positive by chance).

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: A qualitative ultrasound JVP assessment, done with the patient sitting fully upright or in a chair, where visualizing the jugular vein above the clavicle counts as positive, correlated with elevated central venous pressure on right heart catheterization.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: Interpret the ultrasound JVP study with caution due to spectrum bias, since all patients were destined for right heart catheterization for volume-status assessment and results may not translate to other populations.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Chronic pelvic pain requires an organ-system approach because the pelvis contains multiple structures, and the concept of viscero-visceral sensitization (a spider web of nerves, not one nerve to one organ) explains overlapping symptoms.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: History clues can localize pelvic pain: worse after standing or at end of day suggests myofascial pain, timing with ovulation suggests GU, mood component suggests central, and radiating/sharp/burning/buzzy pain suggests peripheral nerve pain.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: A pelvic exam is not automatically warranted in chronic pelvic pain; use a trauma-informed approach, and a cotton swab to assess for hyperalgesia can be sufficient instead of a speculum.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: During a pelvic exam, use the word 'tolerable' rather than 'comfortable' since the exam is not going to be comfortable.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: The diagnostic workup for chronic pelvic pain is simple: screening for sexually transmitted infections, urinalysis, pregnancy test, and pelvic ultrasound if pelvic organs may be involved.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Treatment of chronic pelvic pain includes pelvic physical therapy, analgesia, and cycle suppression with OCPs or hormonal IUDs when pain connects to the menstrual cycle.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Constipation is defined as three or fewer bowel movements per week, and is chronic when symptoms persist for more than three consecutive months.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: A change to firmer stool consistency over time can represent constipation for a given patient even if they don't have Bristol stool type 1 or 2.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: The gastrocolic reflex means even a small stimulus (about 500 calories or a small amount of coffee) after a meal can trigger contractions and postprandial pain, so constipation can present with postprandial pain.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Signs of pelvic floor dysfunction/dyssynergic defecation include rocking back and forth, straining, needing to manually disimpact, and (in women) splinting the back wall of the vagina with fingers.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Secondary causes of constipation include medications (notably opioids and iron supplementation), neurologic disease (Parkinson's, which may precede other symptoms), electrolyte abnormalities like hypercalcemia, and hypothyroidism.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Red-flag features suggesting a secondary malignant cause of constipation include significant pain with bowel movements, bleeding, pencil-caliber stools, and weight loss.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: First-line constipation management is appropriate in primary care: hydration, fiber intake, and general laxatives before referral for manometry or balloon expulsion testing.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Dietary options such as two kiwis (skin off) or four prunes can be as effective as some prescribed laxatives, and fiber is first-line/backbone therapy.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Osmotic laxatives (polyethylene glycol, milk of magnesia) are preferred over lactulose, which is no more effective, is expensive, and causes gas; lactulose should largely be reserved for hepatic encephalopathy, and can cause megacolon in ileus or obstruction.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Onset of action guides laxative timing: osmotic laxatives take about 12-24 hours, stimulant laxatives (senna, bisacodyl) about 6-12 hours (best taken at night to align with morning bowel activity), and bisacodyl suppository works in less than an hour.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Fleet enemas can cause significant hyperphosphatemia in patients with CKD and should be used with caution.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: A footstool at the toilet (about six to eight inches) improves positioning for defecation, especially with tall toilets.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Long COVID may affect roughly 10-30% of patients with COVID, often in prime working years, and is covered under the Americans with Disabilities Act, so patients may need short-term disability or FMLA paperwork.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: In long COVID, standard testing is often unrevealing, so validate the patient's symptoms rather than over-testing.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: POTS (postural tachycardia syndrome) is diagnosed when the heart rate rises more than 30 beats per minute on standing (within the first 10 minutes) without a drop in blood pressure, distinguishing it from orthostatic hypotension.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Non-pharmacologic management of POTS includes compression stockings, abdominal binders, small frequent meals, proactive hydration, and liberalizing salt; medications may include midodrine, fludrocortisone, or beta blockers.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: Counter-pressure maneuvers (flexing upper body muscles or squatting) can increase blood flow to the brain and help patients experiencing near-syncope or lightheadedness.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: For fatigue in long COVID, counsel patients on the three Ps: pace, plan, and prioritize, taking on what they can and spacing out activities.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: For loss of smell after COVID, safety counseling includes ensuring smoke alarms have batteries and having a trusted person check food safety, body hygiene, and food smell; smell training with essential oils and early steroid nasal spray may help.

episode_title: #323 Pelvic Pain, Constipation, Long COVID (TFTC)
statement: COVID vaccination is still recommended for patients with long COVID symptoms; some improve, some see no change, and it is unusual for symptoms to worsen.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: In a randomized n-of-1 trial, having patients test and avoid self-selected atrial fibrillation triggers did not improve afib-related quality of life, though alcohol (per-protocol) was associated with worse quality of life and fewer afib events when avoided.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: In the ISTOP-AFib trial, per-protocol analysis found alcohol was associated with afib-related quality of life and about 40 fewer atrial fibrillation events, while caffeine was not identified as a trigger.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: The CLICK trial showed chlorthalidone significantly lowered systolic blood pressure in patients with advanced CKD, a population where thiazide-type diuretics had traditionally been thought ineffective.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: In the CLICK trial, patients were on about three and a half antihypertensive drugs at baseline with chlorthalidone added as a fourth agent, and the effect size was a 10.5 mmHg reduction in systolic blood pressure.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: Most of the blood pressure improvement with chlorthalidone in advanced CKD occurred within the first month and at the lowest dose, suggesting large doses may not be needed.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: Chlorthalidone in advanced CKD was associated with increased rates of hypomagnesemia, hypokalemia, and hyperuricemia, though there were no significant differences in serious adverse events versus placebo.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: KDIGO guidelines target a systolic blood pressure of 120 for advanced CKD (CKD4).

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: NIH recommends four FDA-authorized outpatient COVID therapies in decreasing order of preference, with nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) as first line.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) has substantial drug-drug interactions, notably raising tacrolimus levels markedly, which significantly limits its use in transplant patients.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: Among monoclonal antibodies, only sotrovimab retains activity against omicron and is NIH's second-line outpatient COVID therapy, given as a one-time IV infusion.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: Remdesivir is NIH's third-line outpatient COVID therapy, given as three days of IV treatment, and was effective at reducing hospitalizations and any medically attended visit in the PINETREE trial.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: Molnupiravir is NIH's last-resort outpatient COVID therapy given its small benefit, which may have been due to gender imbalance and was attenuated when controlled for gender.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: All patients in the four outpatient COVID treatment trials were unvaccinated, so the risk reduction benefit of these medications in vaccinated high-risk patients is unknown.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: Fluvoxamine is not included in NIH clinical guidelines for COVID; if used, patients should ideally be connected with a clinical trial.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: False positive rates on rapid antigen COVID tests are quite low, and over half of false positives came from the same batch of a particular company, suggesting batch-related issues.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: The rapid antigen false positive study was done only among asymptomatic patients, so results cannot be generalized to symptomatic individuals.

episode_title: #319 Hotcakes: Afib, Chlorthalidone in CKD, Outpatient COVID Treatment Update
statement: The ACP diverticulitis guideline states antibiotics are not needed for treatment.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The updated USPSTF recommendation is to screen all adults ages 35 to 70 who have overweight or obesity (BMI ≥25) for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The prior 2015 USPSTF recommendation started screening at age 40; the updated recommendation lowers this to age 35.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: Approximately 21% of U.S. adults with diabetes are unaware they have it, and up to 85% of people with prediabetes have never been told by their physician.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: Acceptable screening tests per ADA include fasting blood sugar (requires 8 hours fasting), hemoglobin A1C (any-time blood draw), or oral glucose tolerance test (75 g glucose with blood sugar measured at 2 hours).

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: A diabetes diagnosis requires confirmation with a second test unless it is clinically obvious, but prediabetes (abnormal but below diabetes threshold) does not require a confirmatory test.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The oral glucose tolerance test tends to diagnose more people than A1C or fasting glucose, but fasting glucose and A1C are more convenient and remain perfectly acceptable screening methods.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The UKPDS showed that in ~7,000 people with newly or recently diagnosed diabetes followed nearly 20 years, tight glucose control reduced the risk of death and heart attacks compared with routine care.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: If an initial screening test is completely normal, the ADA recommends repeating screening every 3 years, and the USPSTF considers this reasonable.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The A1C cutoff for prediabetes is 5.7 to 6.4%, with diabetes at 6.5% or higher.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: In prediabetes, lifestyle changes can reduce the risk of progressing to diabetes; in some larger studies as many as 28% of people progressed from prediabetes to diabetes over just under 3 years.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: Once a patient is in the prediabetes range, the ADA recommends screening every year, and the USPSTF considers this reasonable.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: Asian Americans are at higher risk for diabetes at a lower BMI, so the USPSTF suggests considering screening once the BMI is 23 and above.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The USPSTF recommendation defines who definitely should be screened but does not preclude clinical judgment to screen patients outside the age or BMI range who have other risk factors (e.g., gestational diabetes, smoking, strong family history).

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: Diabetes disproportionately affects Black, Hispanic/Latinx, certain Asian American, Pacific Islander, and Native American populations, which should be considered when deciding to screen.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The ADA recommends screening everyone age 45 and older, plus screening younger patients with overweight/obesity who have at least one additional risk factor, which is broader than the USPSTF approach.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The lifestyle intervention shown effective in prediabetes studies involved losing 7% of baseline weight and 150 minutes of moderate activity per week (e.g., brisk walking 30 minutes five times weekly).

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: Screening for diabetes appears to carry very little downside; studies found only temporary anxiety around diagnosis with no lasting difference.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: In EMPEROR-Preserved, empagliflozin 10 mg daily reduced the composite of cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalization in patients with HFpEF (EF >40%), independent of diabetes status.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: EMPEROR-Preserved required an elevated NT-proBNP for enrollment: >300 in patients without atrial fibrillation and >900 in patients with atrial fibrillation, in addition to EF >40%.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: In EMPEROR-Preserved the reduction in CV death or all-cause mortality was not statistically significant; the benefit was driven by reduced heart failure hospitalizations.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: Reported adverse effects of empagliflozin in HFpEF included hypotension (~10%) and a slight increase in urinary tract infections and genital fungal infections, with no difference in renal failure between groups.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: In the GRASP trial of rotator cuff disease, progressive physiotherapy was not superior to a single best-practice physiotherapy session, and subacromial steroid injection showed only a small benefit at 8 weeks that did not persist at 6 or 12 months and did not meet the minimum clinically important difference.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: A single physiotherapy session with home exercises may be sufficient for rotator cuff disease, since most patients improve by 8 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months regardless of receiving injections or ongoing physical therapy.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: The 2020 colorectal surgery guidelines recommend against routine antibiotics for uncomplicated diverticulitis, supported by trials showing no benefit in symptoms or prevention of complications.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: In the DYNAMO non-inferiority trial, patients were CT-diagnosed, immunocompetent, non-pregnant, without significant comorbidities, had one or fewer SIRS criteria, and were stabilized for 24 hours before enrollment, so results apply to selected, non-septic patients.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: The 2021 CHEST VTE guidelines suggest that isolated subsegmental PE with no proximal DVT and low risk of progression/recurrence may not require anticoagulation; exceptions include pregnancy, active cancer, immobility, or acute hospitalization.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: For cancer-associated VTE, DOACs are now recommended as first-line over low molecular weight heparin (strong recommendation, moderate certainty), but apixaban or LMWH should be chosen over rivaroxaban when there is a luminal GI malignancy due to GI bleeding risk.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: Vitamin K antagonists (warfarin) remain preferred over DOACs for anticoagulation in antiphospholipid antibody syndrome because of higher recurrence risk on DOACs, with the benefit of warfarin most clear in triple-positive patients.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: Extended-phase anticoagulation beyond 3 months is not recommended for VTE with transient identifiable risk factors, but is appropriate for persistent risk factors (e.g., active malignancy) or unprovoked VTE.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: For extended-phase VTE anticoagulation, reduced-dose apixaban (2.5 mg twice daily) or rivaroxaban (10 mg daily) can be used, though this was a weak recommendation with very low certainty evidence.

episode_title: #307 Spooky Tofurkey Cakes: SGLT2 for HFpEF, Diverticulitis, VTE update, Rotator Cuff Disease
statement: For patients with unprovoked VTE or a persistent risk factor who decline anticoagulation, aspirin is recommended as an option to prevent recurrent VTE.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: When approaching antibiotics, first ask whether the syndrome is infectious or non-infectious before deciding what to cover, to avoid prematurely anchoring the differential.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Penicillin still works for strep throat (pen VK) and for syphilis.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Amox-clav and ampicillin-sulbactam provide great coverage of oral flora and streptococcal species, making them good choices for oropharyngeal infections; simple cellulitis can be treated with amoxicillin alone.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: As cephalosporin generations progress from first to fourth, gram-negative coverage increases; cefepime adds anti-pseudomonal coverage and fifth-generation ceftaroline is an anti-MRSA agent.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Cephalosporins do not cover Listeria, atypicals (mycoplasma, chlamydia), MRSA, and enterococci (mnemonic LAME), except that ceftriaxone can be combined with ampicillin for enterococcal endocarditis or bacteremia due to synergy.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Carbapenems cover gram positive, gram negative, and anaerobes but do not cover MRSA; ertapenem also lacks anti-pseudomonal coverage while imipenem and meropenem cover pseudomonas.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Linezolid is bacteriostatic, so if used for bacteremia it should ideally be paired with another bactericidal anti-MRSA agent.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Daptomycin does not work for MRSA lung involvement because it is inactivated by surfactant.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Clindamycin and quinolones carry a high risk of C. diff; the main use for clindamycin is the first 48 hours of necrotizing fasciitis for its anti-toxin effect.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: For community-acquired MRSA skin infections, doxycycline or cefadroxil can be effective, but check the local antibiogram since clindamycin may carry a 30% or higher treatment failure rate.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Antibiograms should be checked at the unit level, since different units (BMT, transplant, cancer, medical floor, surgical ICU) have their own susceptibility profiles, especially for unpredictable organisms like E. faecium and E. faecalis.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Doxycycline is a versatile twice-daily oral antibiotic but its two most common side effects are photosensitivity and esophagitis, so counsel patients to take it with plenty of water.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: High-dose trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole can cause hyperkalemia and blood dyscrasias (anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia) plus G6PD-mediated hemolytic anemia, so check a CBC and metabolic panel at least once during a 7–10 day course.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Piperacillin-tazobactam already covers anaerobes, so adding metronidazole is unnecessary.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Check the QT interval before using azithromycin or levofloxacin, as many patients have a QTc greater than 470.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Anaerobic coverage (metronidazole or clindamycin) is no longer recommended for routine aspiration pneumonia; it is reserved for empyema or lung abscess, since acute aspiration pneumonitis typically resolves in 36–48 hours.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: A reasonable inpatient regimen for community-acquired pneumonia without MDRO risk factors is ceftriaxone plus either azithromycin or doxycycline for atypical coverage; azithromycin and doxycycline are preferred over levofloxacin, which may be held if not discharging.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: A negative MRSA nasal swab has strong negative predictive value for MRSA pneumonia, so if empiric vancomycin is started for a sick pneumonia patient and the swab is negative, vancomycin can be dropped.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: The combination of piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin increases nephrotoxicity risk the longer it is used, so limit the combination or stop vancomycin after the first 48 hours when possible.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Double anti-pseudomonal coverage should be reserved for sick, septic ICU patients on multiple pressors or immunocompromised, heavily antibiotic-exposed patients; stable floor patients do not need it because each anti-pseudomonal dose raises C. diff risk.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Community-acquired pneumonia can be stopped at five days if the patient reaches clinical stability (afebrile, breathing normally, back to baseline).

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Transition from IV to PO antibiotics can be done when the patient improves (improved respiratory rate, afebrile, feeling better); being in the hospital alone does not justify continuing IV vancomycin.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: For a diabetic foot, simple streptococcal cellulitis (red, hot, painful foot without fluctuance) does not need MRSA coverage, whereas a fluctuant abscess needs MRSA coverage and incision and drainage.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: For a chronic diabetic foot wound with necrosis in an uncontrolled diabetic, a reasonable empiric regimen is vancomycin plus cefepime plus metronidazole, but engaging surgery for source control is the most important step.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: If a diabetic foot amputation achieves clean margins with negative cultures, antibiotics can be stopped; only add up to 48 hours of perioperative coverage if there is overlying cellulitis surrounding the amputation.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Plain films are a reasonable first imaging step for suspected foot osteomyelitis; if positive for clear osteomyelitis, an MRI is not needed for confirmation, but MRI (higher sensitivity) is warranted if plain films are non-conclusive and suspicion is high.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: The pendulum is shifting toward oral antibiotics (e.g., fluoroquinolones or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) for osteomyelitis given their good oral bioavailability, offering a more patient-centered alternative to prolonged IV therapy.

episode_title: REBOOT #284 An Antibiotics Primer, with @IDdocAdi
statement: Antibiotic stewardship means using the right antibiotic at the right dose for the right duration in the right patient, not merely stopping antibiotics.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Check a one-time Lp(a) to help predict cardiovascular risk; it only needs to be checked once and does not need to be trended over time.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: ApoB is most useful when a patient is near their LDL goal but you're still worried about residual risk, or to guide how aggressive to be; it isn't very helpful until the patient is on maximum tolerated statin.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Lp(a) is a good target test for patients with a strong family history of premature ASCVD.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: A coronary artery calcium (CAC) score is relatively inexpensive out of pocket, often hundreds of dollars rather than thousands, and can help refine individualized cardiovascular risk.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Counsel hypothyroid patients that even after the TSH normalizes, it may take two or three months to feel back to normal.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: For hypothyroid patients still feeling unwell after 2-3 months with a normalized TSH, some experts add T3 a couple times a day.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Hold biotin supplementation for at least 48 hours before checking thyroid function tests, because high-dose biotin can mimic a Graves pattern with suppressed TSH and elevated T3/T4.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: High-dose biotin supplements can produce thyroid function tests that resemble Graves disease, with a suppressed TSH and high T3 or T4.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Approach the thyroid exam from the front using the thumbs rather than from behind the patient.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Atelectasis is not a cause of postoperative fever, despite being commonly cited on rounds.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Do not underestimate urinary retention as a cause of problems such as elevated blood pressure, delirium, and acute kidney injury.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: In acute kidney injury, roughly 70-90% of cases can be fixed by a bag of LR or a Foley catheter.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: The core approach to AKI is to hydrate them, fix the obstruction, or do nothing (as with ATN) while avoiding further harm to the patient.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Urine eosinophils are not helpful for diagnosing acute interstitial nephritis outside of a board exam context.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Don't let concern about contrast-induced nephropathy dissuade you from a needed study; if the patient needs contrast, give it.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: In AKI, bladder scan the patient or try a Foley to rule out retention rather than waiting for a renal ultrasound to find obstruction.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Screen refugees for trauma with two questions: whether they experienced trauma or violence in their former country, and if so, whether they would like to talk about it.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: SGLT2 inhibitors increase the risk of genital fungal infections but do not increase the risk of urinary tract infections.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: The FDA black box warning about toe amputations with SGLT2 inhibitors was removed, though vigilance is still warranted.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Patients most at risk for euglycemic DKA on an SGLT2 inhibitor are those with glucotoxicity who are relying partly on glucose for substrate; removing that glucose pushes them toward ketogenesis.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: One expert-opinion approach is to wait until the A1c is at least 10 or less, ideally 9 or less, before starting an SGLT2 inhibitor to reduce euglycemic DKA risk.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: SGLT2 inhibitors lower blood pressure almost as much as thiazide-like diuretics, and this effect is more pronounced in less well-controlled diabetics.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Use a hypothesis-driven physical exam: inspect and palpate first so you know exactly what finding you are trying to confirm or exclude before applying the stethoscope.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: In acute heart failure you must increase the diuretic concentration on admission; the DOSE study used 2.5 times the home dose.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Time twice-daily oral furosemide doses (e.g., 8 a.m. and 2 p.m.) so the drug clears before bedtime and lets the patient sleep.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: IV diuretics should begin acting within about 15-30 minutes; if you don't see urine within an hour the dose was inadequate, so you can double it immediately rather than repeating the same dose.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Do not stop an SGLT2 inhibitor in a diabetic admitted for heart failure; it can potentiate the diuretic effect of the loop diuretic, but watch volume status to avoid over-diuresis.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Not all UTIs in men must be treated as complicated; if a man has simple cystitis and is otherwise well, first-line agents can be used without a 14-day fluoroquinolone course.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: A useful framework for UTIs: simple cystitis is bladder and below, while complicated is above the level of the bladder.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Enteric bacteria convert nitrates to nitrites but it takes about four hours, so a daytime sample can give a false-negative nitrite result; obtain an early morning sample or account for timing.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Leukocyte esterase suggests you will likely find pyuria (>10 WBCs/hpf); the absence of pyuria is helpful, but the presence of pyuria alone does not mean the patient has a UTI.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Interstitial lung disease can be organized into four buckets: connective tissue disease, sarcoidosis, hypersensitivity, and idiopathic.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: In interstitial lung disease, PFTs and physical findings like inspiratory crackles may lag the underlying disease, so keep a low index of suspicion when symptoms precede objective findings.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Do not fear statins in fatty liver disease; unless transaminases are roughly three times the upper limit of normal, statins are appropriate, especially since MAFLD carries significant cardiovascular risk.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Even minimally elevated ALT can indicate underlying liver pathology; consider fatty liver disease when ALT is greater than 25 in females or 33 in males.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Washing a potential rabies bite wound with soap and water reduces the incidence of rabies by about 50%.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: In severe hypothermia with altered consciousness, keep the patient on the ground rather than standing, because standing circulates warm core blood to cold extremities and can cause them to spiral.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Give hypothermic patients calories (even soda and candy) to fuel shivering, which requires a lot of energy to rewarm.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Distinguish scarring from non-scarring alopecia; scarring alopecia is more time-sensitive, so refer these patients to dermatology quickly.

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: Non-scarring alopecia includes alopecia areata (autoimmune, patchy hair loss), androgenic alopecia (central part widening and shiny bald spots from increased sebum), and telogen effluvium (following an antecedent stressful event).

episode_title: #248 Top Pearls of 2020: Recap Extravaganza
statement: In severe hypothyroidism, deiodinase enzyme activity shifts to preferentially produce more active metabolites; beta blockers can be considered to ameliorate symptoms rather than stopping levothyroxine.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: PE can be a subtle diagnosis; not every patient is tachycardic, hypoxic, or hypotensive, and some present only with a cough.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: VQ scans are frequently unhelpful, with about 30% returning indeterminate; CT angiography for PE and ultrasound for DVT are the preferred confirmatory tests after a positive D-dimer or high clinical suspicion.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: The Wells criteria for DVT/PE are validated for outpatient or ED patients; for hospitalized patients, use clinical gestalt (with D-dimer to decide on further testing).

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: The modified/revised Geneva score can be used for inpatients; a heart rate greater than 95 gives a score of five, placing the patient immediately in the intermediate category warranting further evaluation.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: In the US, whole leg ultrasound is standard; if you are suspicious of a calf vein clot, specifically ask for it, since European practice often does not image below the knee because isolated below-knee clots are not routinely treated.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Ask about non-prescribed supplements, including testosterone boosters, when evaluating for VTE, as these can be a triggering agent.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Surgery is the strongest VTE risk factor (about 100 times average risk), tapering off over weeks to months afterward.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Obesity roughly doubles VTE risk, and OCP-associated VTE tends to occur shortly after initiation, so long-standing OCP use is a less likely trigger.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: The age-adjusted D-dimer (age × 10 for patients over 50, using the 500 cutoff) can help avoid unnecessary scanning; be sure to adjust for your institution's units.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Using ESC guidelines, massive PE is defined by hypotension from the PE; anything else is submassive.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: An sPESI score of zero can allow home treatment with a DOAC in appropriate patients; a score of one or greater warrants admission for at least observation to assess for RV strain.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: The simplified PESI (sPESI) includes low blood pressure, high heart rate, age greater than 80, history of cancer, history of cardiopulmonary disease, and oxygen saturation less than 90%, with no other labs required.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Syncope as a presenting complaint and an elevated lactate should raise particular concern in PE patients, suggesting a larger clot burden and higher acuity.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Do not send home any PE patient with RV strain until they have been stable for a while; RV strain can be seen on CT (RV:LV ratio) or echocardiogram.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: For submassive PE with RV strain and positive biomarkers, use unfractionated heparin or low molecular weight heparin rather than DOACs, since DOACs lack evidence in submassive PE and these patients may need catheter-directed thrombolysis.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: For low-risk PE patients suitable for home treatment, apixaban is the preferred DOAC based on the AMPLIFY trial, though insurance coverage may dictate the actual agent chosen.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Most VTE with a clear, resolved precipitant (e.g., surgery) is treated for three months; truly unprovoked VTE favors lifelong anticoagulation unless there is a good reason to stop.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: The HERDOO2 rule helps identify low-risk women with unprovoked VTE; obesity plus age over 65 automatically makes a woman not low-risk, favoring continued anticoagulation. No validated calculator exists for men, who are at higher recurrence risk.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Calf DVT (based on expert opinion) is treated for roughly 6 to 12 weeks, particularly if symptomatic.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Upper extremity DVT is treated for a minimum of 3 months, or until an associated line is removed; if a line stays in longer, treat until it is removed, but always at least 3 months even if the line is pulled early.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: For unprovoked VTE, do not routinely perform a cancer workup or thrombophilia workup; the main exception is antiphospholipid antibody syndrome when clinically suspected.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Antiphospholipid syndrome should be suspected in younger patients with unexplained arterial or venous clotting, recurrent clots, or women with recurrent pregnancy loss after the first trimester.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Recent trials (published by ASH) including patients with BMI over 60 found DOACs non-inferior and safe/effective for VTE in morbidly obese patients, contrary to prior recommendations to avoid DOACs above BMI 40.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: For patients with altered GI plumbing (e.g., Roux-en-Y, ileocolectomy), avoid warfarin (due to impaired vitamin K absorption) and use a DOAC, since DOACs are absorbed throughout the GI tract (about 50% of factor Xa inhibitors in the ascending colon).

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: DOACs are safe and effective for VTE in patients with active malignancy regardless of chemotherapy, but for upper GI cancers prefer apixaban over rivaroxaban due to increased bleeding risk, and avoid dabigatran.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: Apixaban actually has a longer half-life than rivaroxaban, which is one reason apixaban is dosed twice daily (split dosing) to improve safety outcomes.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: In antiphospholipid syndrome, warfarin (not DOACs) is the preferred anticoagulant; note that INR may be unreliable if a lupus anticoagulant is present.

episode_title: #238 DVT / PE Triple Distilled
statement: IVC filters have poor evidence for benefit and increase recurrent DVT risk; the main indication is inability to anticoagulate, and removable filters should always be taken out because struts can break, migrate, or perforate.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: In the RECOVERY trial, dexamethasone 6 mg daily (oral or IV) for up to 10 days reduced 28-day all-cause mortality in hospitalized COVID-19 patients.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: The mortality benefit from dexamethasone in COVID-19 was greatest in the sickest patients—those requiring mechanical ventilation had roughly a 12% absolute reduction in 28-day all-cause mortality, with a relative reduction on the order of one-third.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: Hospitalized COVID-19 patients not requiring oxygen did not benefit from dexamethasone, and there was a (non-significant) suggestion of possible harm—so avoid steroids in those not requiring oxygen.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: In veterans over age 75 without known ASCVD, starting a statin was significantly associated with lower all-cause mortality, lower cardiovascular mortality, and fewer ASCVD events.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: For an 80-year-old, life tables predict roughly 8-9 more years of life, while statin time-to-benefit is about 2-5 years, supporting statin use in reasonably healthy older patients.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: Statins appear to have roughly a two-year legacy effect after stopping, which may explain why discontinuing statins in the last year of life probably does not matter.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: In the TICO trial (ACS patients after drug-eluting stent), all patients got 3 months of dual antiplatelet therapy, then were randomized to ticagrelor monotherapy or continued DAPT for the rest of the 12-month period.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: In the TICO trial, ticagrelor monotherapy resulted in less bleeding without an increase in major adverse cardiac events compared with continued dual antiplatelet therapy.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: Ticagrelor carries a warning for patients at risk of symptomatic bradycardia, including those with second- and third-degree heart block, where the medication should be avoided.

episode_title: #229 Hotcakes: COVID on steroids, statins over 75, and shortened duration DAPT
statement: Prasugrel's main warning is bleeding in patients who are older, underweight, or have had a recent prior stroke.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: In a New Orleans cohort of PCR-confirmed COVID-19 patients where black patients made up about 30% of the served population, black patients accounted for 76% of hospitalizations and 70.5% of deaths.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: After adjusting for comorbidities, socioeconomic status, and clinical variables, black race was associated with increased odds of hospitalization but NOT with increased odds of in-hospital death.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: In this cohort, the unadjusted case fatality rate was actually higher for white patients (30.1%) than black patients (21.6%), partly explained by white patients being older.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: Black patients requiring hospitalization were on average nearly nine years younger than white patients and had a greater burden of comorbidities such as diabetes, obesity, and hypertension.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: In the RECOVERY trial, dexamethasone 6 mg once daily reduced all-cause mortality in hospitalized COVID-19 patients, with the greatest benefit in mechanically ventilated patients (death reduced by one-third) and those requiring supplemental oxygen (reduced by one-fifth); no benefit was seen in patients with symptoms under 7 days.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: RECOVERY subgroup analysis showed the greatest dexamethasone benefit in ventilated patients (death reduced by one-third) and those on supplemental oxygen (reduced by one-fifth), with no benefit in patients symptomatic for fewer than seven days.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: If antidepressant monotherapy fails, augmenting with T3 (triiodothyronine) is an APA recommendation for major depressive disorder and can accelerate response, though evidence is stronger for TCAs than for SSRIs/SNRIs.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: Brexanolone, a progesterone derivative given as a 60-hour infusion, was FDA-approved for postpartum depression and works within 24 hours, making it an option for severe refractory symptoms when SSRIs (first line) are insufficient.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: There is no evidence to screen for testosterone in patients with depression alone; testosterone may help depressive symptoms only in patients who also have hypogonadal symptoms and a clinical testosterone deficiency.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: Hormone replacement therapy is not indicated for depression alone, but in perimenopausal/menopausal patients with physical symptoms who also have depression, a short course of HRT can be considered.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: The AGA found only low-certainty evidence for probiotics in symptomatic C. diff infection and could not recommend them for treatment; fecal microbiota transplant is what has been shown to work.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: The AGA made a conditional recommendation, based on low-quality evidence, that probiotics could be used in patients on antibiotic therapy for reasons other than C. diff, but this should not be extrapolated to severely immunosuppressed, active cancer, or organ transplant patients.

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: For probiotic formulations found to have benefit, Saccharomyces boulardii (a yeast) was effective on its own, while bacterial strains required at least two combinations (e.g., Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Streptococcus salivarius).

episode_title: #223 Hot COVID Cakes: COVID19 Disparities, Steroids, Hormones for Depression, Probiotics
statement: The Charlson comorbidity index is a validated score used across clinical research to capture comorbid factors like hypertension, diabetes, chronic lung disease, and malignancy rather than listing individual conditions.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: Race is a social and political construct without a true biological basis, so racism—not race itself—should be understood as a driver of health disparities.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: Common clinical tools apply race-based corrections—such as the eGFR correction for Black race justified by assumptions of higher muscle mass—and some institutions are now removing these corrections.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: Pulmonary function test calculations incorporate race-based corrections that trace back to Samuel Cartwright's spirometer and the belief that Black individuals had lower lung capacities.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: In a large atrial fibrillation registry of about 12,000 patients, Black patients were about 37% less likely to receive DOACs than White patients, and after adjusting for social factors they were still about 20% less likely.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: The DOAC prescribing disparity persisted in a Medicare beneficiary cohort of about 40,000 patients, where Black patients remained about 20% less likely to receive DOACs even after removing insurance differences.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: Making the right therapeutic choice the easy choice—addressing drug cost and prior authorization barriers rather than individual counseling—may reduce prescribing disparities.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: A 2000 study showed Black patients with long bone fractures were less likely to receive pain medication than White patients, and a 2016 study found medical students still believed Black individuals have thicker skin or fewer nerve endings.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: Resident clinic patients had poorer coronary artery disease, diabetes, and cancer screening outcomes than attending patients, and were more likely to be racial/ethnic minorities, non-English speaking, uninsured or on Medicaid, and living in poverty.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: Clinicians should avoid stigmatizing labels like 'non-compliant,' 'non-adherent,' or 'frequent flyer' and instead examine the structural factors influencing patient behavior.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: National conversations about police killings, not just local events, have a measurable negative impact on mental health, as shown in a Lancet study.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: COVID-19 testing sites in Texas, Wisconsin, and Chicago were more likely to be located in majority-white neighborhoods than in minority neighborhoods, reflecting access disparities.

episode_title: #222 Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Medicine Utibe Essien MD, MPH
statement: A practical framework for addressing racism in medicine is to listen (create space for those affected), learn (use available anti-racism resources), and lead (leaders invest in diversity, inclusion, and health equity efforts).

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: In the ACTT-1 trial, IV remdesivir significantly shortened recovery time versus placebo, but the mortality difference did not reach statistical significance.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: In ACTT-1 the greatest benefit from remdesivir appeared in hospitalized patients requiring supplemental oxygen (but not yet on high-flow, non-invasive ventilation, or ECMO), so treatment should be considered early before progression to intubation.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: The ACTT-1 primary outcome was changed before any outcome data were known, and the original outcome would still have been significant, so the change is not a major concern.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: Overall mortality in ACTT-1 was 7.1% with remdesivir versus 11.9% with placebo, but this was a secondary outcome and did not demonstrate a significant mortality benefit.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: Once a standard of care exists, future therapeutic trials should ideally be active-controlled against remdesivir (or use a non-inferiority design) rather than placebo-controlled.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: In the Lancet Hong Kong trial, triple therapy (interferon beta-1b, lopinavir-ritonavir, ribavirin) shortened time to negative nasopharyngeal swab to 7 days versus 12 days for lopinavir-ritonavir alone.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: The benefit in the triple therapy trial was driven by patients who actually received interferon, reducing time to negative swab from 12.5 to 6.5 days, with no significant difference in those who did not receive interferon.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: Interferon in the triple therapy protocol was only given to patients presenting within 7 days of symptom onset to avoid worsening the pro-inflammatory phase that can develop in the second week of COVID-19.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: Time to negative nasopharyngeal swab is a surrogate rather than a patient-centered outcome, limiting the clinical conclusions that can be drawn from studies using it.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: RT-PCR false negative rates for SARS-CoV-2 vary with timing relative to symptoms: a swab taken 5 days before symptoms (presumed day of infection) has a ~100% false negative rate, and even on the day of symptoms the median false negative rate is 38%.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: The lowest false negative rate for RT-PCR occurs about 3 days after symptom onset, catching roughly 80% of infections.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: A high clinical suspicion with a negative RT-PCR swab supports retesting, since sensitivity increases as the patient approaches and develops symptoms.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: Prolonged PCR positivity (up to weeks) reflects detection of viral fragments amplified 40 times, not necessarily replicating or infectious virus.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: No replication-competent virus has been isolated after day 10 of symptom onset, so patients remaining PCR positive for weeks after recovery are likely not infectious.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: When interpreting antibody seroprevalence studies, remember that even a specificity of 96% can yield more false positives than true positives when disease prevalence is low.
