episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: The Abbott SARS-CoV-2 IgG (nucleocapsid) antibody test reaches ~100% sensitivity and specificity around 13 days after a positive PCR or 17 days after symptom onset, with poorer performance earlier.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: In pre-COVID stored samples, the Abbott IgG test had only 1 false positive out of 1000, giving ~99.9% specificity, likely reflecting low cross-reactivity with endemic coronaviruses.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: The CDC did not remove fomite information based on new evidence but reorganized it, and surfaces remain a real but not primary mode of SARS-CoV-2 transmission.

episode_title: #217 COVID Cakes the Fourth: ACTT-1, Triple Therapy, PCR, Serology
statement: Most SARS-CoV-2 viral load on surfaces diminishes quickly, with more than 50% gone within about six hours, so letting dry goods sit or rinsing produce is reasonable rather than aggressive disinfection.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Rather than asking 'are you homeless,' ask where a patient has been staying and whether it is a place they control, working toward 'when's the last time you stayed in a place that was your own,' since this is less stigmatizing and more informative.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Homelessness is a state, not a trait; roughly 60-80% of people who experience homelessness have single episodes that end, so it comes and goes rather than being permanent.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: In the literature, preferred terminology has shifted toward 'persons experiencing homelessness' to reinforce that homelessness comes and goes and to keep the focus on the person.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Mortality rates among individuals experiencing homelessness are three to nine times higher than age-matched populations in the same region.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: There is a very high prevalence of childhood abuse in this population, including childhood sexual abuse in over 50% of women who are homeless.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: In a survey of about 4,700 homeless veterans using VA primary care, roughly 39-40% had severe chronic pain, so pain is commonly part of the clinical picture.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Building a trustworthy relationship by focusing on the patient's imminent concerns should take priority over completing standard checklists like eye exams or labs in early visits.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: For continuity, verify the phone number and backup contacts at the first visit, since patients often have a reachable place that is not their address.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: For hypertension in a homeless patient, prefer once-daily medications and lean toward calcium channel blockers or ACE inhibitors, avoiding diuretics that require frequent bathroom access.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Insulin storage is relatively robust if not in extreme heat; store it refrigerated or at least in a part of the backpack not facing the sun, per the National Healthcare for the Homeless Council.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: The most common addiction among individuals who are homeless is alcohol, and alcohol was the predominant substance reported in self-reported overdoses among homeless veterans.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Buprenorphine-naloxone is far easier to manage than methadone maintenance in homeless patients because methadone programs require frequent in-person visits and pose transportation barriers.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Harm reduction matters: any reduction in substance use, not just abstinence, can be a meaningful win that increases the chance a patient stays alive and has more chances to change.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Foot problems are highly prevalent (9-65% in cross-sectional samples), so examine the feet; low-tech interventions like providing new socks and shoes can help significantly.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: When examining a patient's feet, ask permission and position your head lower than the patient's as a gesture of humility, being mindful of embarrassment and concerns about smell.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Failure to perform a foot exam risks missing serious pathology; anchoring on a social history like alcohol withdrawal can delay recognition of florid sepsis or gas gangrene in the foot.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Hepatitis C can be treated with oral therapy in homeless patients with high success rates (over 80% SVR in one Boston study), but patients must be counseled that reinfection is possible even after cure.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: The presence of serious medical illness like cirrhosis can be used as leverage to obtain housing resources, since managing such conditions is nearly impossible without stable housing.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Congregate living settings like shelters are ripe for COVID-19 outbreaks, with 30-50% testing positive when a cluster is identified, versus relatively low rates when no cluster exists yet.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Tailoring primary care for homeless patients (extra visit time, on-site social workers, clothing closet, shower) improves patient experience and longitudinal relationships, though evidence for better health outcomes is weaker.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Homeless medical respite programs serve patients too sick for shelters or streets but too well for acute hospitals, and can bill Medicaid in expansion states; over 50 exist nationally.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Customize treatment decisions to the patient's situation by directly asking whether the proposed plan will work for them, as adult patients have common sense about what is feasible.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: For healthy individuals taking metronidazole, having a few alcoholic drinks is very likely safe; the disulfiram-like reaction warning is not supported by controlled data.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: A small controlled study of 12 healthy volunteers given metronidazole for five days versus placebo before alcohol showed no difference in blood alcohol, symptoms, or acetaldehyde levels.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Rat models showed no inhibition of aldehyde dehydrogenase by metronidazole, undermining the proposed mechanism for a disulfiram-like reaction.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Most pill-form medications, especially antibiotics and painkillers in emergency/travel kits, remain effective for years past their expiration date and generally do not need to be replaced.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: The FDA requires 90% potency, and manufacturers typically only test to about a year and a half; the largest military study of ~3000 lots found the majority retained ≥90% potency for three to five years or more.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Expired pills do not become toxic; the only known example was an old tetracycline formulation causing Fanconi's syndrome, which has been off the market since the late 1970s/early 1980s.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Aspirin is a notable exception that breaks down substantially over time, dropping to less than 1% potency in old samples, so aspirin used for important indications should be kept fresh.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Expired epinephrine (EpiPens) often retains viable drug (many working at 40 months), but because of the high stakes in anaphylaxis, keep pens up to date and use expired ones only as a backup while still seeking emergency care.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: For important daily medications found opened and expired, there is no data, so extrapolation is unwise and getting a fresh prescription is preferred when feasible.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Recurrent 'sinus headaches' are usually migraine variants and should be treated with migraine therapy; true recurrent sinus headaches are rare.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: In migraine variant presenting as sinus pain, the trigeminal nerve provides sensation to the sinuses, and pain can be bilateral, which can mislead clinicians away from a migraine diagnosis.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Recurrence is the key clue: patients reporting multiple 'sinus infections' per year strongly suggests migraine, whereas a first-time presentation with fever, chills, and severe sinus pain may be bacterial sinusitis.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: In a study treating self-diagnosed sinus headache patients (with normal CT and sinus endoscopy) with migraine-directed therapy, 92% responded, with the majority responding to triptans.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Lidocaine with epinephrine is safe and often preferable for finger laceration repair; it decreases bleeding and reduces the need for a digital tourniquet.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Older case reports of digital ischemia from epinephrine were confounded by bicarbonate that altered tissue pH, not the epinephrine itself.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: A randomized study of 60 patients found lidocaine with epinephrine performed slightly better (less need for digital tourniquet) than lidocaine alone with both appearing safe for hand suturing.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: A hand surgery series of over 3,000 cases using epinephrine injections reported no episodes of digital ischemia and no need for phentolamine reversal.

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw
statement: Atelectasis does not cause postoperative fever, a myth that has been disproven since the mid-1990s.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: In the Wang et al. Lancet randomized trial, remdesivir was not associated with a reduction in time to clinical improvement, mortality, or viral shedding in hospitalized patients with COVID-19, but the study was underpowered due to early stopping.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: The Lancet remdesivir trial was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled superiority trial of 237 hospitalized PCR-confirmed COVID-19 adults at 10 hospitals in Wuhan, funded by the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: The remdesivir dosing in the Lancet trial was 200 mg IV on day 1 followed by 100 mg daily for a total 10-day course, in addition to usual care.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: Power calculations matter most for negative/null studies: a negative result may reflect either no true association or simply insufficient observations to detect the expected effect.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: The remdesivir trial enrolled only about half its planned sample of 453 patients because the outbreak came under control in China, leaving too few events to detect a difference.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: When baseline imbalances appear in a randomized trial, you don't need a p-value to judge their importance—if you think a difference could matter clinically, that clinical intuition is what counts.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: In the remdesivir trial, baseline imbalances suggested the remdesivir group was sicker (more hypertension, diabetes, coronary disease, tachypnea, higher lower-tract viral load, and more patients with symptoms >10 days), which could bias toward a false negative.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: The clinical significance of viral load from respiratory swabs is uncertain because a positive PCR may reflect ongoing infectious virus production or merely fragments of virus already killed by the immune system.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: Awake proning in hypoxic COVID-19 patients produced a marked oxygenation improvement, with median saturation rising from 84% on oxygen to 94% after five minutes of proning in a New York case series.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: Awake proning is a low-risk intervention with essentially no side effects, so even anecdotal reports of success may justify trying it—in contrast to the higher evidence bar needed for drug therapies.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: The COVID-19 awake proning case series was high-risk: of 50 patients, 13 required intubation within 24 hours, including 7 in the first hour.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: During the pandemic there has been a marked decrease in stroke patients presenting to EDs and ICUs, suggesting patients with stroke symptoms are dangerously delaying care.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: COVID-19 is associated with a hypercoagulable state and vascular events, including strokes in unexpectedly young patients, as in a New England Journal report of five patients under age 50 in NYC.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: Once a COVID-19 patient is intubated, chances of successful extubation are poor, making interventions that avert intubation especially valuable.

episode_title: #214 COVID Cakes 3: Llamas, Remdesivir, Strokes, the Future, and More!
statement: Llamas can generate neutralizing antibodies against SARS-CoV-2 using a small nanobinding domain, and these neutralizing antibodies are notable because they block viral entry into the cell rather than merely alerting the immune system.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: When examining a patient with unilateral ear complaints, examine the unaffected ear first to establish a baseline for comparison.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Look through the speculum as you insert it into the ear canal, since ear canals can be tortuous and curve superiorly or posteriorly; inserting straight can bump the canal and make the patient pull away.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Clear, dull fluid behind the eardrum can be a sign of CSF, so consider it in the right clinical context.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: For pneumatic otoscopy (insufflation), the speculum must be snug and sized to the ear canal; a smaller speculum lets air escape and gives a poor exam.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Unilateral ear pain with a normal ear exam should prompt evaluation of surrounding structures including TMJ, muscles of mastication, sinuses, and throat, since throat, base of tongue, and laryngeal cancers can present as referred ear pain.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Myofascial pain from muscles attached to the TMJ (temporalis, masseter), rather than the joint itself, is often the true source of what gets labeled as TMJ pain and can cause ear discomfort.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Herpetic lesions in the outer ear with ear pain can indicate Ramsay Hunt syndrome, which can result in facial palsy.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: When ear exam, nasolaryngoscopy, and surrounding exam are all normal in a patient with persistent ear pain, obtain a CT neck with contrast to evaluate for tumors or masses causing referred pain.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Eustachian tube dysfunction may show a more opacified/scarred eardrum with negative pressure retraction; severe cases show the eardrum plastered onto the ossicles like Saran wrap.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: First-line treatment for Eustachian tube dysfunction is nasal steroids (fluticasone) plus an antihistamine, with allergy testing and possible allergy shots for those who test positive.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Combining an antihistamine nasal spray (e.g., azelastine) with a steroid nasal spray (e.g., fluticasone) works synergistically and helps more than either alone for allergies and sinus symptoms.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: For high-volume saline sinus irrigation, warm the saline in the microwave for 10 to 15 seconds so it is lukewarm and more comfortable.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: A homemade sinus irrigation solution can be made with a teaspoon of regular, kosher, or sea salt plus a quarter teaspoon of baking soda in two cups of distilled water.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Refractory Eustachian tube dysfunction may be treated with ear tubes or balloon Eustachian tube dilation, but dilation carries risk since the balloon passes right next to the carotid artery and is only for select candidates.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Barotrauma can occur from jumping into a pool and landing on the ear the wrong way, scuba diving, or flying with a URI/congestion where serous middle ear fluid causes trauma on descent.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: For adult otitis media, topical drops are not very effective; treat with appropriate oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanic acid), and consider adding fluticasone and antihistamine, with oral steroids for severe concurrent sinonasal disease.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Complications of otitis media can be serious, including meningitis and venous sinus thrombosis, so do not wait a full year to refer patients with recurrent or chronic infections to ENT.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Cholesteatoma appears as white keratin debris (keratin pearls) typically in the posterior-superior quadrant, the weakest part of the eardrum; visible debris may be just the tip of the iceberg with more disease deeper in the middle ear and mastoid.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Fungal otitis externa causes more friable skin that bleeds easily with a cottage-cheese chunky debris; white fuzzy hyphae suggest candida, while black debris (that can be confused with cerumen) suggests aspergillus, a dangerous combination with diabetes.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Malignant (necrotizing) otitis externa is essentially osteomyelitis of the temporal bone; diabetic patients may have severe disease with pain, drainage, and cranial nerve deficits but symptoms may not match the severity.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Culturing the ear before referral, especially after failed drops, allows better-directed therapy and saves cost and time versus empirically trying another antibiotic.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: A preventive/treatment drop for swimmers with recurrent otitis externa is a 50/50 mix of rubbing alcohol and distilled white vinegar, using a couple of drops after swimming.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Ophthalmic drops can be used in the ear but ear drops should never be used in the eye; buying separate ofloxacin ophthalmic and dexamethasone ophthalmic bottles is cheaper than the combination otic product.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Eye drops are pH balanced so they do not burn or sting in the ear, unlike some ear drops.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Patients with a tympanic membrane perforation or opening into the middle ear should not use over-the-counter ear drops or cerumenolytics; only prescription ear drops should be used.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Ofloxacin otic is not contraindicated with a perforated eardrum; over-the-counter drops are riskier because they are not pH balanced and can worsen infection.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: For impacted cerumen, use one or two drops of mineral oil every other or every third day to soften the wax; instrumenting tightly packed wax that sits on the eardrum causes pain and swelling.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Ear irrigation carries risk: if water gets trapped behind cerumen it causes otitis externa, and patients with prior perforations, ear tubes, or surgery should not be irrigated due to perforation risk.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Hydrogen peroxide is absorbed by cerumen causing it to swell without dislodging it, making it less effective than mineral oil for wax removal.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: To prevent cerumen recurrence, patients can use one or two drops of mineral oil at bedtime once a week; those using earplugs, earbuds, or hearing aids accumulate more wax and should keep the devices clean.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: Itchy ears are often eczema or psoriasis of the ear canal rather than otitis externa; fluocinolone oil both lubricates and provides anti-inflammatory steroid, relieving symptoms and reducing wax burden.

episode_title: #413: Common Ear Complaints, Ear Exam 101, Cerumen Remedies, and more!  Get an EARful with Dr. Angela Peng!
statement: The American Academy of Otolaryngology (AAO) publishes clinical practice guidelines on ENT topics including otitis media and otitis externa that are useful for practitioners and patients.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Value in healthcare is defined as quality divided by cost, where quality includes both immediate and long-term benefits, and cost includes both financial costs and non-financial costs like time out of work, discomfort, and suffering.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: US healthcare spending reached 3.3 trillion dollars in 2016, roughly ten thousand dollars per person and about 18 percent of GDP, with major waste contributors being unnecessary services, excess administration costs, and inefficiency.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Over-ordering of tests can be categorized into three areas: knowledge/cognitive factors (like diagnostic uncertainty), process/systems factors (where it is easier to order than not), and culture (institutional and patient-driven).

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: A resident-led QI project reduced unnecessary daily labs by combining EHR changes to make reordering harder with a rounds checklist item to actively discuss lab necessity with the attending each day.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: An uninsured patient facing an ACL surgery faces the entire face value bill including hospital, doctor, anesthesia, and OR fees, running in the range of $20,000 to $50,000, not including physical therapy and equipment.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Government-based insurances (Medicaid and Medicare) achieve the best price negotiations because they insure so many people, giving them the biggest negotiating power to get prices down to the lowest amounts.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Insurance coverage is associated with better financial security, better self-reported health, earlier diagnosis and treatment (e.g., cervical cancer), more curative surgeries for colon cancer, improved blood pressure, and decreased depression.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Patients and clinicians can look up estimated costs of tests, procedures, and doctor's visits by zip code using patient-friendly databases such as healthcarebluebook.com and fairhealthconsumer.org.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Fewer than 1% of people actually perform any kind of price comparison for medical tests, largely because pricing is not transparent, and most patients simply go where their doctor refers them.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: The likelihood ratio of a BNP is not fixed but increases as the BNP value gets higher above the upper limit of normal, so a markedly elevated BNP changes your probability more than a value just over the threshold.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: In the heart failure exam, jugular venous distention has a likelihood ratio of 5.1, while the third heart sound has the highest likelihood ratio; a BNP over 200 carries a likelihood ratio of 3.7.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Likelihood ratios do not multiply the probability of disease directly; roughly, an LR of 2 raises probability by about 15%, an LR of 5 by about 30%, and an LR of 10 by about 45%.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: A likelihood ratio nomogram lets you draw a straight line from pretest probability through the likelihood ratio to obtain post-test probability without calculations; a symptomatic outpatient with risk factors starting at 27% pretest probability of heart failure rises into the high 50s post-test with a BNP over 200.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Being explicit about clinical reasoning with patients (e.g., explaining that symptoms argue for a virus and that an antibiotic will not help and may cause diarrhea) is more effective than invoking abstract concepts like antibiotic resistance, because it directly connects to the patient's own care.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: An EHR hard-stop prompting clinicians to justify transfusion for a hemoglobin greater than seven reduced unnecessary transfusions by 70% overnight.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: A reasonable compromise for patients demanding antibiotics is a delayed prescription (fill only if not better in 3-4 days), though in practice many patients simply fill it immediately.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: When interpreting a CBC, focus on the absolute counts (absolute neutrophil count, absolute lymphocyte count, etc.) rather than the differential percentages.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Blasts on the peripheral blood smear warrant an urgent hematology consult.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Smoking is a common cause of neutrophilia; for a smoker with unexplained neutrophilia, repeat the count, then advise smoking cessation and repeat again.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Monocytosis, particularly in older patients, should raise concern for CML or chronic myelomonocytic leukemia.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Basophilia is never normal and raises suspicion for a myeloproliferative neoplasm, warranting prompt hematology evaluation.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: An absolute eosinophil count over 1500 gets a hematologist's attention; lower counts may reflect allergic reactions or medications.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: For most abnormal CBC counts, repeat the test first; if it persists (and the patient smokes), advise smoking cessation and repeat again.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: A leukemoid reaction refers to a high neutrophil count due to a benign (non-malignant) etiology.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Physical exam in leukocytosis should include a spleen exam, lymph node exam, and assessment for signs of infection.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Flow cytometry is helpful when there is lymphocytosis (absolute count over 5000, or less) or lymphadenopathy concerning for a lymphoproliferative disorder; it can be sent before hematology referral.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Chronic asymptomatic neutropenia without recurrent infections may be benign ethnic neutropenia, confirmable with RBC phenotype showing Duffy-null (negative for Duffy a and Duffy b).

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: A hemoglobin over 16.5 in a man warrants attention and evaluation for erythrocytosis.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: For erythrocytosis, check an EPO level first: a low EPO suggests primary erythrocytosis (e.g., polycythemia vera), while normal or elevated EPO suggests a secondary cause such as hypoxia, renal disease, or tumors.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Check an EPO level before ordering a JAK2 mutation in erythrocytosis, as it better guides initial decision-making and resource use.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Aquagenic pruritus (itching, burning, or stinging triggered by water exposure) and erythromelalgia (erythema of the hands and feet) are associated with polycythemia vera.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: A high red cell count (as in erythrocytosis) raises the risk of thrombosis, especially in myeloproliferative neoplasms like polycythemia vera, so bringing the numbers down is reasonable.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: A normal platelet count is 150,000 to 450,000; when thrombocytopenia is found, first determine whether it is isolated or accompanied by other cytopenias.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: A falsely low platelet count from EDTA-induced platelet clumping (seen on smear) can be resolved by running the sample in a blue (citrate) top tube.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: ITP is a diagnosis of exclusion; HIV and hepatitis C must be ruled out. Other causes of thrombocytopenia include B12/folate deficiency, thyroid disorders, liver dysfunction/cirrhosis, H. pylori, and rheumatologic conditions.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia should be suspected regardless of the actual platelet count if it falls a certain percentage from baseline with the right timeline.

episode_title: #167 LIVE! Common CBC Abnormalities with Mary Kwok MD
statement: Distinguishing whether one cell line versus multiple cell lines are abnormal is key: isolated cytopenias have a different differential than two or three lines down, which points toward a bone marrow problem.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: During intern year, focus on becoming a competent internist and mastering the basics rather than prematurely planning your career path.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Seek out multiple mentors for different needs (clinical, research, work-life balance) rather than relying on one mentor for everything; mentors can be attendings, chief residents, or near-peers.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Be an effective mentee by bringing concrete questions to meetings and understanding your mentor's preferred communication style, which makes the relationship successful for both parties.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: PGY-2 is the critical year to decide on fellowship versus a generalist career, since fellowship applications occur in the summer of the third year.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Signing a job contract during second year is generally too early; review any offer with a mentor first, as new graduates may not know how to advocate for themselves.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: For the job search, polish your CV, cover letter, and references over the summer (July-August), then start applying in September-October to be in the first wave of interviews and offers.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: If you are geographically limited, start your job search earlier and apply more broadly to all possible jobs in your target area, being prepared to be more flexible about the role.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Involve your program director in your job search, as they can provide introductions and serve as your best advocate; a warm introduction increases the likelihood your email is prioritized.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Use professional society and journal career centers (such as the ACP and New England Journal of Medicine) to explore available jobs and learn about CV polishing and employment contracts.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Recruiters can present opportunities you might not have considered, but remember they work for the hiring institution, not for you, and their reimbursement may come from what could have been offered to you.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: For phone interviews, prepare a 30-second elevator pitch describing who you are, your skills, and what you're looking for in a job.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: On interview day, wear comfortable shoes since you may walk a lot, bring several copies of your CV, and research the institution and interviewers beforehand.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Being overly polished and rehearsed in interviews is a red flag; stay authentic and don't say yes to everything just to secure a job, as the role must match who you are.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: A 1.0 clinical FTE equals a full-time job, with each 0.1 FTE representing one half-day of clinic per week; anything less than 1.0 is part-time, with the remainder typically going to admin or academic time.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Academic promotion and tenure criteria are highly institution-dependent; check the institution's website and ask more junior faculty or alumni about these sensitive questions rather than only the division chief.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: To ease the transition to attending, ask ahead of time to be paired with a junior faculty clinical mentor who knows the new system, since much of the challenge is learning the system rather than the medicine.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: As an attending it is acceptable and normal to ask for help and not know everything; you can admit uncertainty and look things up together rather than needing to show off knowledge.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Reflect on the behaviors of your favorite educators to identify practices you can emulate as you transition into being a teaching attending.

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Be aware that potential employers will review your social media (LinkedIn, Facebook, Twitter); anything that could discredit the institution can be a hard stop in hiring.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: When a patient fails an adequate trial of one SSRI, a reasonable next step supported by the STAR*D trials is switching to a different SSRI such as escitalopram or citalopram.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Bupropion is a good choice for depression with atypical features (hypersomnia, overeating, cognitive deficits) because it increases norepinephrine or dopamine, while serotonergic agents suit patients with prominent anxiety and irritability.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Paroxetine has multiple drawbacks—severe withdrawal risk, weight gain, the worst sexual dysfunction, many interactions, and potent 2D6 inhibition—so it is generally avoided as a first choice.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Switching within the SSRI class can be done by tapering the current drug quickly (e.g., sertraline 150 to 100 for a week, then 50, then stop) and starting the new SSRI the next day at a low dose like escitalopram 5-10 mg.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: SNRIs like venlafaxine and duloxetine carry a more profound withdrawal risk, so when switching off a high dose, cross-titrate by lowering venlafaxine to 75 or 37.5 mg and briefly overlapping with a low-dose SSRI.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Antidepressant withdrawal is characterized by profound dizziness, flu-like symptoms, increased anxiety, and electrical phenomena often described as 'brain zaps'; it is uncomfortable but not life-threatening.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: For patients with severe paroxetine withdrawal even during a slow taper, switching to fluoxetine can help because fluoxetine's long half-life (active metabolite ~1 week) makes it essentially self-tapering.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Ultra-rapid CYP2D6 metabolizers may have a much shorter drug half-life and can experience withdrawal symptoms even when taking their SSRI only a few hours late.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Most SSRIs are safe during pregnancy with the exception of paroxetine, and the two main risks are a short-lived neonatal abstinence syndrome (24-48 hours) and slightly earlier delivery with slightly lower birth weight.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: The risks of untreated depression during pregnancy—including miscarriage—are greater than the risks of antidepressants during pregnancy.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Sertraline is generally considered the antidepressant of choice during pregnancy, and SSRIs appear safe across the board with breastfeeding.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: The treatment goal for depression is remission, defined as a PHQ-9 score less than 5, rather than simply feeling better.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Bupropion is a useful augmenting agent added to SSRIs, offering improved cognitive symptoms, appetite control, and energy, and in STAR*D was more effective than buspirone.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: For antidepressant-induced sexual dysfunction, bupropion at 150 mg helped about 50% of patients in a randomized controlled trial, with another ~50% of the remainder responding at 300 mg.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Patients should continue an antidepressant for a minimum of six months after achieving remission (not from the start of treatment) before considering discontinuation.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: When stopping an antidepressant, taper slowly and counsel patients that the highest risk of relapse is about two months after discontinuation, distinct in time course from withdrawal.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: T3 (liothyronine) augmentation at micro-doses (about 25 micrograms) is rapidly effective in STAR*D and appears specifically effective in women rather than men.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Atypical antipsychotics like quetiapine are effective for depression augmentation but carry a substantial risk of metabolic syndrome requiring monitoring; aripiprazole may be preferred for lower metabolic risk.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Mirtazapine is sedating and appetite-stimulating at low doses but becomes more noradrenergic (less sedation, less appetite stimulation) at higher doses, and it also blocks 5-HT3 receptors.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Gabapentin and pregabalin can be used to augment treatment for patients with prominent residual anxiety who don't respond to buspirone, though neither is a surefire solution.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: A common primary care error in treating depression is starting at doses that are too high; fluoxetine 5 mg and sertraline below 50 mg may be adequate for many patients with much less side-effect burden.

episode_title: #140 Psychopharmacology 2.0, Antidepressant Master Class
statement: Psychotherapy goes hand-in-hand with medication and its benefits may be longer-lasting than those of medication.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Lack of central clearing does NOT exclude Lyme disease; many erythema migrans rashes are gradually spreading erythematous roundish lesions without central clearing.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: The most endemic areas for Lyme disease in the US are the Mid-Atlantic and New England states, plus the Wisconsin/Minnesota area, but the range is expanding southward into the Carolinas and Virginia.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Ticks tend to settle in the scalp, behind the ear, the collar/neck, axillae, waistband, groin, behind the knee, at the ankle where socks touch, and the gluteal cleft; the whole body should be checked, not just the scalp.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: For classic erythema migrans in an endemic area during Lyme season, you can treat without testing, since a negative or positive test won't change management.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Lyme serology uses two-step testing (ELISA screen then Western blot); the IgM Western blot is very prone to false positives, so a positive IgM in a patient with symptoms lasting longer than 3-6 months likely represents a false positive.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: There is no test of cure for Lyme disease; antibodies can remain positive for months or years after successful treatment, so follow-up serology is not useful.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Doxycycline is the drug of choice for Lyme disease; alternatives are amoxicillin and cefuroxime, whereas azithromycin is clearly less effective.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Alternatives to doxycycline for Lyme are amoxicillin and cefuroxime, which work well, but azithromycin has been tested and is clearly less effective.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Doxycycline causes photosensitivity (worse in fair-skinned patients) and can cause severe pill esophagitis, so patients must remain upright for at least an hour after taking it.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: A Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction can occur with Lyme treatment, causing worsening symptoms, high fever, and shaking chills for 12-24 hours; it resolves with NSAIDs or acetaminophen.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Doxycycline probably protects against C. difficile infection in hospitalized patients.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Fever without a localizing source in an older patient (e.g., 72-year-old) is unusual and almost always signals a systemic process, usually infection; a viral syndrome is unlikely in summer.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: The big three tick-borne illnesses to consider are Lyme disease, anaplasmosis (a rickettsial organism), and babesiosis (an erythrocytic parasite).

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Anaplasmosis more commonly causes low white count and low platelets, while babesiosis more commonly causes low hematocrit (anemia) and low platelets, but there is tremendous overlap and co-infection.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: For a febrile patient with over a week of chills after outdoor exposure, send blood cultures, anaplasma PCR, Babesia PCR, CBC with parasite smear, and a Lyme antibody; the anaplasma and Babesia PCRs are highly accurate (unlike the Lyme PCR).

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Risk factors for severe babesiosis include asplenia/poor splenic function, immunosuppression (especially rituximab, which can cause months of persistent babesiosis), and older age.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Babesiosis treatment differs entirely from Lyme; it is a combination of atovaquone (an anti-parasitic) and azithromycin.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Post-Lyme disease syndrome refers to residual, nonspecific symptoms after treated Lyme (not due to living organisms), correlating with severity of initial infection and delay in treatment; it is distinct from 'chronic Lyme' which implies ongoing living organisms and is not endorsed by IDSA.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Neurologic Lyme disease is treated with a month of ceftriaxone (intravenous therapy).

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Clinical trials comparing 10 vs 14 vs 21 days of Lyme treatment showed no difference; a reasonable approach is 10 days if the patient has antibiotic side effects and up to 21 days otherwise, with longer courses reserved for disseminated or neurologic disease.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: For an attached tick, a single 200 mg dose of doxycycline is effective post-exposure prophylaxis for Lyme disease; ticks merely crawling on you do not transmit infection.

episode_title: #105: Lyme Disease and Tick-Borne Illness with ID Expert Paul Sax: Why you should never go outside or to Boston in the summer
statement: Patients in hyper-endemic areas who get repeated tick bites can be given a supply of doxycycline to take a dose whenever they find an attached tick.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: The first MTP joint is the most common site of gout, followed by anywhere in the foot, ankle, and knee.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: In diuretic-associated hyperuricemia, generally any diuretic (including chlorthalidone) can raise uric acid, not just hydrochlorothiazide.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Dietary factors have limited evidence as major contributors to hyperuricemia; the bigger factors are renal urate handling and genetics, and overemphasizing diet leads to patient blaming and shaming.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Erythema extending far beyond a joint (e.g., up to the mid-tibia when evaluating the ankle) makes gout unlikely and should raise concern for cellulitis.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Crystal-proven diagnoses of gout are uncommon; in the Health Professionals Follow-up Study only about 7% had a crystal-proven diagnosis.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Even in intercritical periods (days into a flare), synovial fluid can still show extracellular MSU crystals, so aspiration remains worthwhile despite lower yield.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: The classic MSU crystal is needle-shaped and negatively birefringent: yellow when parallel to the polarizer's arrow and blue when perpendicular.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: A typical gout flare peaks within about 24 hours and, even without treatment, subsides within about seven to ten days (up to two weeks).

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Colchicine is less effective when started later in a flare, so timing matters; it can still be used in CKD stage 3 with dose adjustment.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Stopping a short course of steroids abruptly for a gout flare can cause a rebound flare; a taper is preferred.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: A reasonable prednisone regimen for a monoarticular gout flare is 30 mg daily, decreasing by 5 mg every two days (a 12-day course).

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: For ibuprofen in a gout flare, start 600 to 800 mg three times daily for the first few days, then decrease to about 400 mg three times daily for the remainder of the 7-10 day course.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: The colchicine flare regimen is 1.2 mg immediately, then 0.6 mg one hour later, often followed by 0.6 mg twice daily until the attack resolves.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: In sufficient renal insufficiency, a colchicine flare regimen should not be repeated within a two-week period, and colchicine is contraindicated with CYP3A4 inhibitors such as diltiazem.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: A patient already on colchicine for prophylaxis who has renal insufficiency cannot then add a colchicine flare regimen for acute management.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Urate-lowering therapy should not be started during an acute flare, but after resolution it is indicated if there is evidence of tophi, since some patients present with tophi without a prominent flare.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: In the 2012 ACR guidelines, CKD stage 2 or higher was an indication to start urate-lowering therapy after the first flare, to avoid future NSAID exposure.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Hyperuricemia is necessary but not sufficient for gout: hyperuricemia prevalence is roughly 20+% while gout prevalence is about 4%.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: About two-thirds of urate is excreted through the kidneys and about one-third through the GI system, with renal urate transport abnormalities being the major driver of hyperuricemia over endogenous purine production.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: MSU crystals trigger NLRP3 inflammasome activation, cleaving pro-IL-1 beta into mature IL-1 beta, the key cytokine driving the intense inflammation of a gout flare.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Tophi are not inert crystal masses but biologically active chronic granulomatous reactions that can cause bony erosions and joint destruction if hyperuricemia is inadequately managed.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: For an acute gout flare in a patient with CKD stage 3 and diabetes, NSAIDs are avoided due to CKD and steroids are less ideal due to diabetes, leaving colchicine (if timing and drug interactions permit) or a short glucocorticoid course, with IL-1 inhibitors as another option.

episode_title: #112 Gout Flares: Bathtubs and Firefighting
statement: Pegloticase is a pegylated uricase, FDA approved around 2009-2010, given intravenously and reserved for severe refractory tophaceous gout, primarily used by rheumatologists.

episode_title: #57: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 2: More thinking and thoughts
statement: Metformin is the first-line agent for diabetes, but pioglitazone (a thiazolidinedione) is a reasonable alternative when metformin is not tolerated or additional help is needed.

episode_title: #57: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 2: More thinking and thoughts
statement: Thiazolidinediones appear more effective than metformin at preventing progression to diabetes and show particular promise in patients with pre-diabetes.

episode_title: #57: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 2: More thinking and thoughts
statement: The fluid retention and edema associated with thiazolidinediones occur at higher doses than the ~15 mg dose recommended for pre-diabetes.

episode_title: #57: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 2: More thinking and thoughts
statement: Thiazolidinediones should still be avoided in patients with heart failure and in those with osteoporosis.

episode_title: #57: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 2: More thinking and thoughts
statement: Pioglitazone is considered the only true insulin sensitizer at the level of muscle; metformin is not truly an insulin sensitizer according to diabetes experts.

episode_title: #58: Concussion, traumatic brain injury, and post-concussive syndrome
statement: In older adults with diabetes, a low or near-normal A1C on insulin or sulfonylureas should raise concern for overtreatment and hypoglycemia risk, which can impair cognition especially in the elderly.

episode_title: #58: Concussion, traumatic brain injury, and post-concussive syndrome
statement: The way a low A1C is achieved matters: a naturally low A1C from weight loss or diet is acceptable, whereas concern is for those on hypoglycemia-causing medications like insulin and sulfonylureas.

episode_title: #58: Concussion, traumatic brain injury, and post-concussive syndrome
statement: Metformin does not need to be stopped simply because the A1C is low, as it carries low risk of harm, though deprescribing can be trialed if the patient dislikes taking it.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: In multimorbidity, use 'now versus later' to weigh treatments: the benefit of tight diabetes control is later, but the harms of diabetes treatment are now.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: For older multimorbid patients with a 'now' timeframe, aim for an A1c in the middle, somewhere between 8 and 9, to avoid symptomatic hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia rather than tight control.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: Treating hypertension and treating afib with anticoagulation reduce all-cause mortality in older adults, whereas diabetes, osteoporosis, and cholesterol treatment do not.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: The SPRINT trial demonstrated a number needed to treat of about 65 to prevent one composite outcome over roughly 3.2 years; NNT is higher over shorter timeframes and lower over longer timeframes.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: SPRINT excluded dementia, unintentional weight loss, nursing home residents, diabetes, history of stroke, and certain CKD—effectively excluding frailty and multimorbidity—so its blood pressure targets may not apply to frail multimorbid patients.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: Intensification of antihypertensives in the hospital is associated with short-term harms without long-term benefits, especially in geriatric patients.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: In older adults, statins do not improve all-cause mortality and may decrease non-fatal MIs but not stroke, so their benefit for future prevention is limited in frail patients.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: The number needed to screen for cancer screening is often in the hundreds to thousands, roughly a 10-fold magnitude less benefit than treating chronic diseases (NNT often ~40-100).

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: Cancer screening efficacy does not have an inflection point where it becomes ineffective at a set age; instead the number needed to screen gradually rises with age, frailty, and multimorbidity.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: Systolic heart failure beta-blocker studies showed benefit over just 30 to 90 days, making it a short-timeframe (feel-better-now) intervention, unlike blood pressure and cholesterol treatments whose trials spanned roughly 2 to 4 years.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: For anticoagulation and fall risk, remember a patient only needs to fall once the wrong way to sustain intracranial hemorrhage—the '295 falls' estimate can be misleading.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: A practical framework for multimorbidity is to classify each treatment by what it achieves (longevity, function, or quality of life) and when it achieves it (now versus later), then match to the patient's goals and timeframe.

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD
statement: When counseling on medications in older adults, think of it like Jenga—each added medication is a block pulled out and stacked on top, so approach adding medications with more caution.

episode_title: #52: Anemia: Tips and tools for diagnosis and treatment
statement: For any abnormal blood test result, the first step is to repeat the lab to confirm it is real before acting on it.

episode_title: #52: Anemia: Tips and tools for diagnosis and treatment
statement: When evaluating microcytic anemia, iron deficiency is at the top of the differential, followed by anemia of chronic disease.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Screening (presumptive/point-of-care) urine drug tests use antibody technology and are rapid and inexpensive, but are prone to false positives from cross-reacting substances.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: The opiate screen does not detect all opioids; fentanyl, methadone, and buprenorphine are not included under the opiate screen.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Confirmatory testing uses chromatography (gas or liquid) paired with mass spectroscopy to specifically identify and quantify substances without false positives.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Use confirmatory testing when the result will impact patient care—such as legal implications, CPS involvement, monitoring programs, or confirming medication compliance.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: A single small use of most substances (THC, cocaine, amphetamine) is typically detectable in urine for one to three days.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Fentanyl is highly lipid soluble; with repeated heavy use it accumulates in fat and can remain detectable in urine for weeks, whereas a single administration is usually gone within 48 hours.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Oral fluid/saliva testing detects THC for only about 48 to 72 hours even in heavy users, useful for confirming recent cessation.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Hair testing can detect substances over the length of the hair, roughly 90 days for an average hair length of about one and a half inches.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Standard benzodiazepine screens target oxazepam and will NOT detect clonazepam; clonazepam must be ordered as a separate specific test.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Fentanyl screening immunoassays can give false positives from risperidone, ziprasidone, and trazodone, so a positive fentanyl screen should be confirmed.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Sertraline can cross-react with the benzodiazepine screening assay to cause a false positive.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Bupropion and pseudoephedrine can cause false-positive amphetamine/methamphetamine screens, making the amphetamine assay one of the messiest requiring confirmation.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: The Vicks inhaler contains L-methamphetamine, which produces a true positive on the methamphetamine assay but has no psychoactive effects, only vasoconstriction.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: PCP/phencyclidine assays have many cross-reactants (dextromethorphan, chlorpheniramine, cyclobenzaprine, tricyclics, venlafaxine), so a positive is more likely false than true.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Naloxone and naltrexone can cross-react with the oxycodone screen.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Cocaine is rapidly metabolized to benzoylecgonine, which is what urine tests detect, and cocaine has few false positives making it a fairly specific result.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Specimen validity testing (creatinine, pH, temperature) is essential; a dilute or non-physiologic specimen invalidates any interpretation of the drug results.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: When counseling patients about unexpected results, be supportive and non-accusatory, avoid stigmatizing terms like 'dirty' or 'clean,' and describe what was found rather than labeling the drug screen.

episode_title: #18 Osteoporosis, bone health and the calcium, vitamin D controversy.
statement: Oral/saliva buprenorphine testing only shows presence (no metabolite profile) and can give false negatives if inadequate saliva is obtained, especially in patients with dry mouth from anticholinergic medications.

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: Use of complementary medicine in patients with curable cancers was associated with higher refusal of conventional treatment (e.g., 53% radiation refusal vs 2% in controls) and poorer 5-year survival (82% vs 86.6%).

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: The survival difference associated with complementary medicine disappeared after accounting for adherence to conventional treatment, suggesting refusal of conventional therapy—not complementary medicine itself—drove worse outcomes.

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: The practical takeaway is to proactively and non-judgmentally discuss complementary medicine with patients, emphasizing that conventional gold-standard treatment should be the foundation while other measures may support quality of life.

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: In a meta-analysis, low-dose aspirin (75-100 mg) reduced cardiovascular events only in patients weighing 50-69 kg (about 23% reduction, HR 0.77), with no cardiovascular benefit but persistent bleeding risk in those weighing 70-90 kg.

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: The authors estimated that roughly 80% of men and 50% of women in these trials would not have benefited from the low-dose aspirin they received based on body weight.

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: Marijuana use showed a low-strength association with respiratory symptoms (cough, sputum, wheezing) but insufficient evidence to link it to obstructive lung disease or changes in FEV1/FVC.

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: About 10% of patients have a documented penicillin allergy, yet ~95% of reported penicillin-allergic patients actually tolerate the drug, and 80% who had immediate hypersensitivity are no longer allergic after 10 years.

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: Patients with a documented penicillin allergy had higher adjusted hazard ratios for MRSA (1.69) and C. diff (1.25) infections, driven by increased use of alternative antibiotics like macrolides, clindamycin, and fluoroquinolones.

episode_title: #111: Hotcakes – Complementary Medicine in Cancer, Dosing Aspirin by Body Weight, Marijuana & Respiratory Symptoms, Penicillin Allergies and More!
statement: Testing to refute or confirm a penicillin allergy takes about three hours and involves a series of intradermal injections followed by an oral challenge.

episode_title: #71: Asthma Made Simple
statement: Dizziness is spatial disorientation or imbalance without a false sensation of motion, whereas vertigo specifically refers to the sensation of movement where no movement exists.

episode_title: #71: Asthma Made Simple
statement: Patients often struggle to describe their symptoms and may change the story on repeat questioning, so drilling into timing and exactly what happens helps build the differential.

episode_title: #71: Asthma Made Simple
statement: For acute vertigo, determine whether it is an acute vestibular syndrome (continuous for hours up to 24+ hours) or an episodic syndrome.

episode_title: #71: Asthma Made Simple
statement: The differential for an acute vestibular syndrome centers on distinguishing stroke from vestibular neuritis or labyrinthitis.

episode_title: #71: Asthma Made Simple
statement: Episodic vertigo is subdivided into provoked and unprovoked types; the classic provoked example is benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, where head movement triggers the room spinning.

episode_title: #71: Asthma Made Simple
statement: Orthostatic hypotension is a provoked, episodic cause of dizziness/vertigo triggered by standing up.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: In evaluating new AKI in cirrhosis, start with a routine urinalysis to confirm a bland urine (no blood, no protein) consistent with hemodynamic injury, since 95-99% of patients with alcoholic cirrhosis will have a hemodynamic cause.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: AKI in cirrhosis with a bland urinalysis breaks down into three main hemodynamic causes: pre-renal, ATN, and hepatorenal, all reflecting inadequate blood flow to the kidney.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: In cirrhosis, a low urine sodium or low FENa does not rule out ATN, pre-renal, or hepatorenal syndrome—the full differential remains open; only an elevated urine sodium/FENa points toward ATN.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: In cirrhotics with elevated bilirubin, benign hyaline casts get bile-stained and are often misread as dirty granular casts, leading to a premature diagnosis of ATN.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Urine eosinophils have a positive predictive value around 98% for interstitial nephritis on boards but only about 40% in real life, making it a poor clinical test.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Before volume-expanding a cirrhotic with albumin, assess volume status—expanding a patient with pleural effusions, pulmonary edema, or clear volume overload can be detrimental.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: A cirrhotic patient without any peripheral edema and a soft abdomen may actually be volume depleted, warranting a trial of IV albumin for the first 24 hours.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: In cirrhosis, saline is poorly effective because it rapidly distributes into ascites, so colloids (albumin) are generally preferred for volume expansion.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: For volume overload in cirrhosis, start a loop diuretic combined with an aldosterone antagonist (aldactone), watching potassium; avoid thiazides/metolazone which can worsen hyponatremia.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: In cirrhotics with failing kidneys, be cautious loading up spironolactone because of the risk of hyperkalemia; furosemide is more comfortable to use until good urine output is confirmed.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Cirrhotic patients have decreased muscle mass and reduced hepatic creatinine production, so serum creatinine overestimates GFR—dose loop diuretics higher than the usual 20-times-creatinine estimate.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Renal ultrasound is mandatory in the assessment of CKD but not strictly mandatory in AKI; however, most clinicians still order it because obstruction is a low-risk, reversible, and unacceptable-to-miss cause of renal failure.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: When assessing uncertain volume status, do not give diuretics and volume resuscitation simultaneously—pick one based on best judgment, and if you do both at once the next day you cannot interpret the result.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: When giving a fluid challenge, give enough volume to definitively land the patient on one side of the fence within 24 hours rather than a token 75 cc/hr that leaves the result uninterpretable.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: The traditional albumin challenge in suspected hepatorenal syndrome is 1 gram/kg/day (commonly 25 grams IV every 6 hours) for the first 24-48 hours, then reassess.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Beyond acting as a volume expander, albumin may modulate endothelial dysfunction and bind circulatory vasoconstrictor factors, directly attacking the pathogenesis of hepatorenal syndrome.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Vasoconstrictors treat hepatorenal syndrome by countering splanchnic vasodilation and resetting the signal to the kidney; in the US the standard cocktail is midodrine plus octreotide plus albumin, while terlipressin is used in Europe.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Renal recovery in hepatorenal syndrome correlates strongly with the achieved mean arterial pressure; norepinephrine is often preferred because it raises the MAP more effectively and gives an answer faster.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: A common failure in HRS treatment is leaving the midodrine/octreotide at a starting dose without titrating to the MAP goal, so the team concludes 72 hours later that nothing worked when the dose was never adjusted.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
