statement: In a cirrhotic, a MAP of 75 may be inadequate because the autoregulatory curve is shifted; such patients may need a MAP of 85-90 to adequately perfuse the kidneys.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Norepinephrine for HRS almost always requires a central line and ICU transfer, which is a practical limitation, and MAP goals of 85 are frequently not executed because ICU staff default to a MAP goal of 65.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Untreated classic type 1 HRS is a mortal condition; if the patient is otherwise a good transplant candidate, ICU transfer for treatment is worth the effort because it may be the last chance to save them.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: For alcoholic cirrhotics not currently transplant candidates due to recent drinking, dialysis can still be offered as a bridge, since keeping them alive and abstinent for six months may make them a transplant candidate, though this occurs less than 10% of the time.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: In hospitalized cirrhotics with AKI, you are almost always dealing with type 1 HRS (rapidly progressive AKI over ~2 weeks); type 2 HRS is a chronic, insidious, relatively benign course seen more in outpatients.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Large volume paracentesis is a classically cited precipitant of HRS, yet most HRS clinical trials permitted paracentesis, so its role is uncertain—decisions should be individualized to the patient.

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Once HRS is being successfully treated with a vasoconstrictor and renal perfusion (MAP) is restored, it is reasonable to perform large volume paracentesis with albumin and even restart diuretics, since HRS is a hemodynamic disorder, not a volume-depleted state.
topic: Diuretics and paracentesis during HRS treatment

episode_title: REBOOT #145 Hepatorenal Syndrome #NephMadness￼
statement: Common triggers of type 1 HRS include SBP or peritonitis, large-volume variceal paracentesis, UTI, hepatic encephalopathy, and medication nonadherence, as these chronically volume-challenged patients tip over easily.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: The diagnosis of osteoarthritis can be made on the basis of history and physical exam alone, without any imaging.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Imaging or blood work in OA is only warranted to rule out an alternative diagnosis that cannot be excluded on history and exam; plain films are insensitive to early OA and there is structure-symptom discordance.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: OA pain is typically mechanical—worse with weight-bearing or load—whereas prolonged morning stiffness, joint swelling, and improvement with use suggest inflammatory arthritis.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: The standard cutoff for morning stiffness suggesting inflammatory arthritis is 30 minutes; OA patients may have brief stiffness or a gelling phenomenon but it is usually not prolonged.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Hand OA classically affects the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints and the first CMC (base of the thumb), and does not produce the joint effusions seen in rheumatoid arthritis.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Osteoarthritis is not a non-inflammatory arthritis; small effusions and synovitis are common, and the inflammatory tissue pathology resembles RA to a lesser extent.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Risk factors for OA include increasing age, female sex, obesity, prior knee injury (meniscus/ACL), genetic associations, and biomechanical factors such as leg length discrepancy.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: OA is best understood as total joint failure—a whole-organ disease of cartilage, bone, and synovium driven biomechanically—not simply wear and tear or normal aging.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: There are currently no treatments proven to alter structural progression or prevent OA; management aims to relieve symptoms and slow progression via joint protection, weight reduction, and physical activity.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: The two OA preventive measures best supported by evidence are preventing joint injuries and preventing excessive weight gain that overloads the joint.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: The mainstays of OA management regardless of joint site are education, self-management, exercise, weight loss, and physical therapy (with occupational therapy for the hand).

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Simple walking programs combined with strength training produce meaningful improvement in OA pain; framing activity as walking rather than exercise helps engage patients in pain.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Acetaminophen has minimal efficacy in OA per meta-analyses but remains a reasonable trial for patients who cannot take NSAIDs.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: For OA limited to one or two joints amenable to topical therapy, topical NSAIDs or topical capsaicin are preferred over systemic therapy; hip joints are not amenable to topical therapy.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Topical NSAIDs can be used in patients with contraindications to systemic NSAIDs because there is very little systemic absorption.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Diclofenac has a worse safety profile than other NSAIDs and should be considered a non-preferred NSAID for OA, while celecoxib was shown to have a favorable safety profile.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Apparent NSAID failure may reflect inadequate dose or frequency rather than true drug failure, and patients should be counseled to avoid doubling up with over-the-counter NSAID-containing products.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Intra-articular corticosteroid injections may provide short-term (two to three months) benefit in OA, but a 2017 JAMA trial showed no long-term benefit and injections should not be done too frequently.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: There is a long-acting intra-articular steroid formulation whose main advantage over standard formulations is a smaller effect on blood glucose.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Intra-articular hyaluronic acid injections have small, near-null efficacy in low-bias non-industry trials, and may only be considered in patients with comorbidities precluding other therapies or those awaiting joint replacement who are poor candidates.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Duloxetine has a positive OA trial and may be useful in patients with depressive symptoms, sleep issues, or widespread pain, though tolerability is a clinical challenge.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Glucosamine and chondroitin show no efficacy for knee and hip OA in low-bias non-industry trials, though a recent well-conducted hand OA trial of chondroitin showed a positive symptom benefit.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Tai Chi has randomized trial evidence for efficacy in fibromyalgia and osteoarthritis, and mind-body practices like yoga and mindfulness carry little downside when done safely.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Ongoing peripheral nociceptive input in OA can lead to peripheral and/or central sensitization; sensitized patients have more pain severity, do worse after joint replacement, and are more likely to develop persistent rather than activity-related pain.

episode_title: #177 Osteoarthritis Master Class with Tuhina Neogi MD, PhD
statement: Physicians underutilize physical therapy, which is a first-line management partner for osteoarthritis.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: A low bicarbonate with a normal anion gap could be either a metabolic acidosis or a respiratory alkalosis; confirm with a VBG/ABG by looking at the pH before proceeding.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: The most common cause of non-anion gap metabolic acidosis is diarrhea/GI losses; a low potassium accompanying it further supports GI bicarbonate loss, so don't reach for RTA before excluding infectious diarrhea.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Normal saline has a chloride concentration of 154 mmol/L, which is non-physiologic and can raise serum chloride and produce a non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (chloride intoxication).

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Two New England Journal of Medicine trials (in ER and ICU patients) found a rare but real harm from normal saline versus a balanced solution like lactated Ringer's; favor LR especially as fluid volume and duration increase.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Chloride intoxication is classically seen in the recovery phase of DKA: after the gap closes patients retain chloride while urinating out ketones, leaving a non-anion gap metabolic acidosis.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Topiramate acts as a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor causing a drug-induced proximal RTA with alkaline urine and can produce calcium phosphate kidney stones.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Chronic untreated RTA causes buffering of acid by bone, leading to osteoporosis and occult bone deterioration.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Amphotericin B causes a distal (Type 1) RTA by ripping holes in the epithelium that maintains the hydrogen gradient, allowing secreted hydrogen to back-diffuse.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Distal (Type 1) RTA produces alkaline urine that cannot be acidified, leading to calcium phosphate stones — so a patient with non-anion gap acidosis and stones points to distal RTA, and the stone type is calcium phosphate (not oxalate).

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Distal RTA can present with hyperkalemia when the defect is impaired sodium reabsorption (e.g., amiloride, sickle cell, chronic urinary obstruction) rather than the classic hypokalemic form.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Distal (Type 1) RTA is easier to treat than proximal RTA because you only need to replace the daily acid load (~1 mEq/kg), titrating bicarbonate to a serum bicarbonate above 22.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg tablets contain about 8 mEq of bicarbonate and 325 mg tablets about 4 mEq; alternatively one teaspoon of baking soda (~60 mEq) daily can be used, cautioning patients not to mistake a teaspoon for a tablespoon.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Type 4 RTA is caused by chronic hyperkalemia impairing ammoniagenesis; patients acidify their urine normally (pH ~5) but cannot store hydrogen as ammonium, and the acidosis is mild compared to the hyperkalemia.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Type 4 RTA is most commonly due to long-standing diabetes causing hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism; treatment focuses on correcting the hyperkalemia (e.g., stopping the ACE inhibitor, adding a thiazide or loop diuretic), after which the acidosis resolves.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: The urinary anion gap (urine sodium + potassium - chloride) indirectly estimates urinary ammonium: a negative value points to GI losses/diarrhea, while a positive value points to an RTA (mnemonic: negative = gut).

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: For diarrhea, the priority is volume repletion rather than correcting the acid-base abnormality; WHO oral rehydration solutions provide only modest alkali (~8-12 mEq/L citrate), and restoring renal perfusion lets the kidney correct the acidosis.

episode_title: #104: Renal tubular acidosis with Kidney Boy, Joel Topf MD
statement: Proximal RTA does not cause stones because once bicarbonate settles at the new lower set point the distal nephron functions normally and can acidify the urine, avoiding the alkaline urine needed for calcium phosphate stones.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: When assessing breathlessness remotely, establish the patient's baseline dyspnea (especially in COPD/CHF patients) to determine whether their symptoms are markedly different from usual.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: The Roth score, in which a patient counts loudly in their native language and you gauge hypoxia by the number reached or duration counted, is not validated for COVID-19 but may add to the clinical picture.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: During the pandemic, outpatient best practices include postponing routine testing, choosing therapies that don't require monitoring (e.g., a calcium channel blocker over an ACE inhibitor for blood pressure), using more empiric treatment, and only ordering tests that will change management.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: Reports of COVID-19 reinfection likely reflect misinterpretation of continued PCR positivity as reinfection rather than true reinfection; based on SARS and MERS, most patients mount an antibody response within about ten days that wanes over months to years.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: Hydroxychloroquine has in vitro activity against many viruses and immunomodulating effects (used in lupus and RA), but human data have not shown benefit for viral infections; it was ineffective when studied in influenza.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: The French hydroxychloroquine study was an open-label, non-randomized prospective cohort of 42 patients (26 hydroxychloroquine, 16 usual care) using hydroxychloroquine 200 mg three times daily for 10 days, with azithromycin added at clinician discretion for bacterial superinfection.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: A major flaw of the French hydroxychloroquine study is that the reported primary outcome (viral shedding at day six) differed from the planned outcome (shedding at days one, four, seven, and fourteen), a form of selective outcome reporting bias.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: Allowing patients to choose whether to receive an experimental drug introduces allocation bias, since sicker patients may be more likely to consent while less severe patients opt out.

episode_title: #205 COVID Cakes and Hot Takes with Rahul Ganatra MD
statement: A subsequent randomized trial of 62 patients (31 hydroxychloroquine, 31 usual care) suggested about a one-day reduction in duration of fever and cough and more improvement in chest CT at five days, but was small, short, and lacked power calculations.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: In heart failure with iron deficiency, IV ferric carboxymaltose reduced hospitalization from 19.4% to 7.6% with a number needed to treat of about 8.5.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: Iron deficiency in heart failure is defined by ferritin less than 100, or ferritin less than 300 if transferrin saturation is less than 20%.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: IV iron given during an acute decompensated heart failure hospitalization did not increase short-term rehospitalization risk within a 12-week period and is considered safe.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: Iron deficiency in heart failure can occur in non-anemic patients; over 50% of screened patients had iron deficiency with average hemoglobin between 12.3 and 12.5.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: Heart failure is a chronic inflammatory condition with elevated hepcidin, so patients do not absorb oral iron well and have higher baseline ferritin, supporting use of IV iron.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: The soluble transferrin receptor level correlates better with true iron stores than ferritin because it is not affected by inflammation, though it costs about $50 versus $25 for ferritin.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: IV iron benefit in heart failure (reduced hospitalizations) is best supported in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and is not well studied in HFpEF.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: In the PEP trial, adding 160 mg aspirin post-op prevented about seven symptomatic VTE per thousand patients at the expense of three bleeds and two non-fatal MIs.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: A hybrid VTE prophylaxis approach with a lead-in of low molecular weight heparin or rivaroxaban followed by aspirin after orthopedic surgery appears safe and effective.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: For a primary care patient discharged on aspirin 81 mg daily after orthopedic surgery, it appears safe to continue, with no evidence of greater risk of PE or death at one year.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: A 2010 NEJM trial found apixaban likely superior to enoxaparin for thromboprophylaxis after hip replacement, with low reoperation rates for bleeding-related complications.

episode_title: #163 Hotcakes: Burnout, iron for heart failure, aspirin & VTE prevention
statement: Perceived capacity of one's clinic to address patients' social determinants of health appears most protective against physician burnout.

episode_title: REBOOT #49 Vertigo and Dizziness: How to Treat, Who to Send Home, and Who Might Have a Stroke
statement: Check TSH in the workup of unexplained weight change, since missing hypothyroidism as a cause of weight loss is an easy and common miss.

episode_title: REBOOT #49 Vertigo and Dizziness: How to Treat, Who to Send Home, and Who Might Have a Stroke
statement: Older patients are commonly B12 deficient due to decreased intake and medications, and it is easy to correct.

episode_title: REBOOT #49 Vertigo and Dizziness: How to Treat, Who to Send Home, and Who Might Have a Stroke
statement: Don't neglect infectious causes of weight loss such as HIV or hepatitis C in older patients; agism can lead to under-testing.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: The Lyon Diet Heart Study showed a 72% reduction in cardiovascular events over five years in the Mediterranean diet group (secondary prevention).

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: In the Lyon Diet Heart Study, patients were advised to eat more fruit, more whole grains in place of refined, less red meat, more fish and nuts, and to use olive oil and a canola-based margarine, yielding a 72% risk reduction for cardiovascular events over five years.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: The PREDIMED study demonstrated nearly a 30% reduction in cardiovascular events in the Mediterranean diet group for primary prevention.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: A JAMA article showed suboptimal diet is responsible for 45% of cardiometabolic deaths (deaths due to coronary disease, stroke, and diabetes).

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: Processed meats are classified as carcinogenic by the WHO and are strongly related to coronary disease and diabetes, more so than unprocessed meat; the ideal amount is zero.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: Boiled intact whole grains are preferable to whole-grain flour because grinding grain into flour increases surface area, raising the glycemic index and sugar release.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: When counseling patients to eat less of one food, pair it with a healthier substitute, since cutting one macronutrient (like fat) without cutting calories usually leads to eating more of another (often unhealthy carbs).

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: Extra-virgin olive oil has a high polyphenol content that lower grades lack; lower-grade olive oils produce a decreased HDL response compared to extra-virgin.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: Nuts are an ideal snack: satiating, healthy, non-perishable, and a New England Journal paper found nuts and yogurt were the two foods most associated with weight loss.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: Being overweight or obese but physically active places patients in a much lower cardiovascular risk category than being the same weight but sedentary, so exercise benefits health independent of weight loss.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: For weight loss, macronutrient distribution (low-carb vs low-fat) matters less than the quality of the macronutrients; a study showed healthy low-carb and healthy low-fat diets produced identical average weight loss.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: Marine omega-3s (EPA and DHA) come from fish, while plant omega-3s come from chia, flaxseed/flaxseed oil, and walnuts.

episode_title: #119 Nutrition Pearls, Heart Health, Bacon
statement: A minimal but high-yield nutrition intervention is to tell patients that medication alone won't achieve optimal health and that nutrition and lifestyle changes are still required, so patients don't assume diet doesn't matter.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Methadone is a full mu-opioid agonist that sidles onto the receptor without displacing other opioids, whereas buprenorphine is an agonist-antagonist with high receptor affinity that can precipitate withdrawal, making methadone easier to start in the fentanyl era.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Methadone has a wide therapeutic window with no single correct dose—some patients are stable at 60 mg while others need 200 mg twice a day—so dosing must be individualized much like thyroid replacement.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Methadone has excellent oral bioavailability (about 95-98%), whereas sublingual buprenorphine bioavailability can be as low as 30-40% depending on administration.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Both methadone and buprenorphine reduce risk of overdose death and all-cause mortality by about 50%, making them among the most effective medications available.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Methadone can only be prescribed for opioid use disorder through licensed opioid treatment programs (OTPs), not in the primary care setting.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Fentanyl is highly lipophilic and stored in adipose tissue, so drug tests can remain positive for fentanyl up to 40 days after last use, which interferes with buprenorphine induction.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: On the first day at a methadone clinic patients typically receive only 30 mg because habit size is unknown, then are titrated up slowly.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Under the April 2, 2024 federal final rule, counseling is not a federal requirement for methadone treatment and patients cannot be denied methadone for refusing counseling, though individual states or insurers may still mandate it.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: A full physical examination by an MD, DO, NP, or PA must be completed within 14 days of starting at a methadone clinic, and drug screens (oral swab or other) must be done at least eight times a year.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Patients cannot be discharged or denied methadone for refusing a lab evaluation under the new federal rule; those with poor venous access can simply sign a form.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Under the final rule, take-home methadone bottles can be given as early as day one (up to a week's worth), and up to two weeks' worth after about two weeks in treatment, though few clinics actually do this.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: The most important take-home criterion is absence of an active substance use disorder, which is distinct from toxicology results—patients may have intermittent cocaine or lingering fentanyl on drug screens without meeting DSM-5 criteria for active SUD.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Active benzodiazepine or alcohol use disorder typically precludes take-home methadone bottles because these sedatives combined with methadone can cause harm.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Take-home methadone does not require stable housing; unhoused patients can receive take-home bottles as long as the medicine can be kept safely locked and away from others.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Clinicians should weigh the risks of NOT providing take-home methadone—loss of take-homes can drive patients back to daily dosing, job loss, treatment dropout, return to fentanyl, and fatal overdose.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Being on 80-100 mg of methadone is a threshold associated with preventing overdose death, and the key goal is having methadone in the body every single day.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Under the final rule, primary care can perform the OUD screening and send documentation (vital signs, brief exam, diagnosis of moderate-to-severe opioid use disorder) as a low-barrier entry point for OTP methadone initiation; the OTP must still complete the physical within 14 days.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: It is a violation of federal law and the Americans with Disabilities Act for skilled nursing facilities to deny admission to patients on methadone or buprenorphine.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: Under the final rule, hospitals, long-term care facilities, and correctional facilities that hold a DEA clinic or hospital license do not need to be an OTP to continue methadone; they can order it on their formulary and dispense it, but must arrange a warm handoff to an OTP at discharge.

episode_title: REBOOT #25 Master Hyperglycemia and DKA
statement: In the nine states that did not expand the Affordable Care Act, addiction coverage is not mandated by public insurance, so methadone clinics often operate as cash businesses, limiting access.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: In COVID-19, the threshold to intubate has been lower because patients develop rapidly progressive hypoxemic respiratory failure with less lag time than seen with flu, and non-invasive methods raise concerns about viral aerosolization and spread.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: Although COVID-19 is a novel virus, the serious illness it causes (ARDS) is well known, and established strategies work: lung protective ventilation, inhaled prostacyclins, neuromuscular blockers, and early proning.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: In COVID-19 respiratory failure, patients tend to have marked hypoxemia with less stiff lungs and less hypercapnia, and using a lot of PEEP has been very helpful for these patients.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: High-flow nasal cannula, non-invasive positive pressure (BiPAP), and even nebulizers are aerosolizing procedures that can spread the virus; avoid them unless there are highly individualized reasons, especially with limited PPE and non-negative-pressure rooms.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: To reduce viral aerosolization, avoid nebulizers when possible and use inhalers with spacers for patients who need them, reserving nebulizers only for those with a clear underlying indication.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: There is concern that healthcare worker mortality may be higher than the age-based population rate, possibly because a larger inoculum dose during intubation or aerosolizing procedures increases risk; clinicians should be extremely cautious even if otherwise low-risk.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: For COVID-19 intubations, aim for first-best success with the fewest people in the room by having the most senior available anesthesiologist perform the procedure.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: A useful four-step hierarchy for caring for COVID patients: first protect yourself and your team, second focus on good proven ICU care, third consider clinical trial enrollment for disease-specific interventions, and fourth remember compassion.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: To minimize room entries and conserve PPE, coordinate care before entering (e.g., call the nurse to ask what tasks to complete) and learn skills like changing an IV pump so fewer people need to enter the room.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: Innovations to reduce PPE use and exposure include moving IV pumps out into the hallway and relocating the ventilator control panel to the hall so settings can be adjusted without entering the room.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: COVID-19 disproportionately affects vulnerable populations: neonates and children seem less often affected, but for chronically ill elderly it is a truly deadly disease.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: Pandemic control measures may cause harm to vulnerable patients through disruptions such as interruption of medication-assisted therapy for substance use disorder and delayed care for multimorbid patients, leading to missed acute exacerbations.

episode_title: 2018 Recap Extravaganza: The Curbsiders Favorite Pearls
statement: Proper donning and doffing of PPE is essential and easy to get wrong; clinicians should relearn correct technique (the CDC has an 18-minute video) and avoid habits like walking in holding a mask or pulling gown sleeves up.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Sexual harassment is defined by scholars as behavior that derogates, demeans, or humiliates an individual on the basis of that individual's sex.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Sexual harassment comprises three categories of behaviors conceived of as an iceberg: quid pro quo, unwanted sexual advances, and gender harassment, with gender harassment being the most common and the base of the iceberg.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Gender harassment includes offensive remarks about bodies, sexual insults, vulgar name calling and slurs, and has profound effects on physical, psychological, and professional well-being of its targets.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Behaviors that meet the social scientific definition of sexual harassment may still fail to meet the legal definition, which requires behavior to be severe and pervasive enough to create a hostile work environment.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Predictors of sexual harassment include hierarchical workplaces where women don't share equally in power and authority.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: A key principle is to fix the system, not the woman; harassment requires systems-level interventions rather than placing the burden of remedy on individual targets.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: The lowest rates of sexual harassment occur in organizations that proactively develop, disseminate, and enforce sexual harassment policies.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: In a study of Generation X K-awardees (high-achieving academic faculty), 30% of women reported encountering unwanted sexual comments, attention, or advances from a superior or colleague, compared with 52% in a 1995 study.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Female medical students were 220 percent more likely than students from non-STEM disciplines to have experienced sexual harassment by faculty or staff.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: A meta-analysis of trainees showed nearly 60% had experienced at least one form of harassment or discrimination during training.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Identifying repeat offenders is critical, so allies in senior positions can help facilitate reporting in ways that minimize impact on the target, including delayed or anonymized reporting.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Confidential reporting can be structured so a person shares information contingently, e.g., requesting no contact unless a threshold number of other complaints are made against the same individual.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Even in a highly career-oriented cohort of K-awardees, women spent eight and a half hours more per week on parenting and domestic tasks, even after adjusting for spousal employment status.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: One predictor of sexual harassment is the perception that it will be tolerated by the institution.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Women's identity as sex intersects with other identities like race, religion, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and gender identity, and those inhabiting multiple minority identities may have different experiences and needs.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Only about 16 percent of medical school deans are women, and this proportion has been static or declining as new schools appointed male deans.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: Only 27 percent of radiation oncology residents are women, reflecting a pipeline issue.

episode_title: #162 Gender & Sexual Harassment in Medicine, #MeToo
statement: The MGH Claflin Distinguished Scholars Program provided small grants to women caring for young children to fund helping hands like a postdoc, and 31 responding scholars later brought in $51 million in grant funding against a program cost of $2.1 million.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: PGY-3 is not simply more of PGY-2; it is the year to build your identity as a leader, clinician, and educator while growing in independence.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Senior residents set the culture of the program: showing up on time with a good attitude will be emulated, while lateness and disrespect create a toxic environment.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Coaching uses deliberate practice: set a goal, do the task, get specific feedback, then repeat—much like a sports coach or music teacher.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Residents have the most readily available coaches of their careers, but you are far more likely to receive coaching if you actively ask for it rather than assume it will happen.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Use the SMART framework when setting personal or professional goals—specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Frame goals by timeframe—sprint (short-term), mile (intermediate, e.g., publishing a paper), and marathon (long-term)—to make them more concrete and actionable.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Board preparation should be a longitudinal marathon of repeated practice questions, committing to an answer before checking, and understanding why right answers are right and wrong answers are wrong.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Actively commit to an answer before checking it—this active retrieval helps knowledge stick in long-term memory.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Target your studying at the in-training exam objectives you got wrong rather than reinforcing subjects you are already strong in.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Spaced repetition with retrieval and comparing/contrasting right and wrong answers are among the most proven methods for retention and passing boards.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Give feedback frequently (e.g., weekly Feedback Fridays) rather than saving it all for the last day of a rotation, which is frustrating and unhelpful.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: As an attending, don't rescue the resident—shoot decisions back to them and make them commit, since committing to a decision is how learning happens.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: On rounds, have medical students and interns present to the senior resident rather than the attending, physically empowering the resident to act as the decision-maker.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Build resident confidence by explicitly affirming good management decisions and, when you disagree, explaining your thought process so they reason differently in similar future cases.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Encourage learners to phrase plans as commitments rather than questions—moving from 'I think we should diurese?' to 'I think we should diurese, this is what we're going to do.'

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: For residents interested in quality improvement, the Institute for Healthcare Improvement (IHI) offers well-done courses and sometimes scholarships to residents.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Demonstrating interest and skills in quality improvement is an attractive way to distinguish yourself to hospital employers and groups.

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2
statement: Feeling overwhelmed or experiencing impostor syndrome is common—even normal—during PGY-3 as increased responsibilities mount and you envision yourself as an independent clinician.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Persistent dental pain (pain that comes on and stays) usually signals involvement of the neurovascular bundle and typically requires a definitive intervention such as tooth extraction or root canal.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: NSAIDs are acceptable and sufficient for most dental pain; opioids are not needed.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: For dental infections, basic antibiotics such as amoxicillin or penicillin VK are usually sufficient because oral flora are not typically resistant; clindamycin is an option for penicillin allergy.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Gingivitis is inflammation of the gum and does not itself cause tooth loss, but if it progresses to periodontitis (inflammation at the base of the tooth) teeth may be at risk.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Roughly one in eight patients admitted with diastolic heart failure may have cardiac amyloidosis.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: AL amyloidosis (light chain) classically presents with proteinuria, large tongue, periorbital purpura, and easy bruising, while TTR amyloidosis is suggested by bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, biceps tendon rupture, and trigger finger.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Consider cardiac amyloidosis when a patient has amyloid-suggestive features plus an echocardiogram showing thickened ventricles.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Workup for suspected cardiac amyloidosis includes serum and urine immunofixation electrophoresis with free light chains; if positive it suggests AL type needing hematology, and if negative a technetium pyrophosphate scan showing heart uptake greater than bone suggests cardiac (TTR) amyloidosis.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: In diabetes patients, reducing insulin dose can paradoxically lower A1C because overnight hypoglycemia triggers overeating and makes the body insulin resistant for up to 24 hours, raising subsequent blood sugars.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: For patients with type 2 diabetes, postprandial glucose can often be controlled with a GLP-1 agonist or SGLT2 inhibitor rather than jumping to short-acting mealtime insulin.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: As of April 2023, CMS covers a continuous glucose monitor for anyone with type 1 or type 2 diabetes using at least one insulin injection per day, or for recurrent problematic hypoglycemia.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Cervical myelopathy (cord impingement, often from bony hypertrophy) causes upper motor neuron signs, hand clumsiness, gait disturbance, and later urinary symptoms; early surgical intervention can halt progression even if it does not reverse deficits.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Cervical radiculopathy has a natural history of improvement, similar to sciatica, so patients generally should not be rushed to surgery.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: When you need an accurate calcium value in workup of hypercalcemia, the ionized calcium is preferred; correcting calcium for albumin is falling out of favor and may not be helpful.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: When working up hyperparathyroidism, draw calcium and PTH at the same time, and be aware that biotin supplementation can interfere with parathyroid (and thyroid) assays.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: In suspected primary hyperparathyroidism, obtain a 24-hour urine calcium to exclude familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia, which does not improve with parathyroidectomy.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Patients who develop hypercalcemia in response to thiazide diuretics are more likely to actually have underlying primary hyperparathyroidism, so stopping the thiazide may only unmask a pre-existing condition.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Normocalcemic hyperparathyroidism (often found when working up osteoporosis with an elevated PTH but normal calcium) is generally treated the same as classic primary hyperparathyroidism using the same surgical indications.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Universal Hepatitis B screening uses a triple panel: surface antigen (active infection), core antibody (prior infection), and surface antibody (natural or vaccine immunity).

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Hepatitis B vaccination is recommended for non-immune patients under 60, and for those over 60 with risk factors such as chronic liver disease or dialysis.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: For Hepatitis B, household contacts can be screened but should avoid sharing razors and toothbrushes, and sexual partners should be vaccinated.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Phentermine is less likely to cause high blood pressure than bupropion, which is worth remembering when a patient on bupropion has worsening blood pressure.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: For patients on GLP-1 medications, counsel them to scale back portion sizes and have a plan to avoid constipation, since GI side effects are the main reason people discontinue.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Traveler's diarrhea severity is graded by functional impact: moderate means you alter your schedule, severe means you cannot leave your room, and bloody diarrhea is by definition dysentery (severe); moderate or severe diarrhea can be treated, and patients can be sent with standby antibiotics.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: First-line antibiotic treatment for traveler's diarrhea is azithromycin, with fluoroquinolones as an alternative if azithromycin cannot be used.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Endometriosis is very common (comparably more prevalent than type 2 diabetes) and is frequently under-recognized, with a diagnostic delay often of 7 to 12 years.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Imaging is not sensitive for endometriosis—pelvic ultrasound and even MRI may miss it—and laparoscopy remains the gold standard for diagnosis.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Oral contraceptive pills are first-line therapy for endometriosis even without a surgical diagnosis, and skipping the placebo week to fully suppress menses can be tried if symptoms persist.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Vasomotor rhinitis is a common non-allergic rhinitis triggered by weather/barometric changes, temperature, or strong perfumes, and reflects an imbalance in parasympathetic and sympathetic input to the nose rather than an allergic response.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Atrovent (ipratropium) nasal spray works well for patients with a runny/dripping nose, especially used before anticipated triggering situations.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Allergen immunotherapy (allergy shots) requires routine shots over a typical duration of 3 to 5 years and is best for highly motivated patients needing desensitization to specific allergens.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Recurrent UTI is defined as three positive cultures within 12 months or two within six months, and must be distinct completed infections rather than one incompletely treated infection.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Before labeling recurrent UTI, confirm positive cultures matching symptoms, since mimics such as myofascial pain and pelvic floor dysfunction can cause similar pelvic discomfort.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Vaginal estrogen helps prevent recurrent UTI in the genitourinary syndrome of menopause by decreasing vaginal pH and improving dryness.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Methenamine hippurate requires an acidic pH to work and can be tried for recurrent UTI prophylaxis before resorting to a daily broader-spectrum antibiotic.

episode_title: #464 Recap Extravaganza: Dental Pain, Cardiac Amyloidosis, Diabetes & CGMs, Hyperparathyroidism, Neck Pain, Endometriosis, Rhinitis, and more!
statement: Practical measures to reduce recurrent UTI include treating constipation, ensuring good hydration, and referring to a pelvic floor physical therapist when pelvic floor dysfunction is contributing.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: Abdominal pain can arise from extra-peritoneal or systemic causes, including pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, adrenal insufficiency, and hypercalcemia, so the workup should not be limited to intra-abdominal etiologies.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: Eosinophilia can be approached by asking whether it is primary (hematologic malignancy), secondary (neoplasm, allergies, parasites/infection, sarcoidosis, adrenal insufficiency), or idiopathic (hypereosinophilic syndrome); allergies are the most common cause in the US and parasites worldwide.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: The first step in evaluating ascites is to sample the fluid to determine whether it is a high SAAG (portal hypertension) or low SAAG (infiltrative etiology like cancer).

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: A very low SAAG ascites points toward cancer, and in TB-endemic regions, tuberculosis, as leading differential diagnoses.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: A nodular omentum on imaging narrows the differential toward an infiltrative process such as malignancy, but tuberculosis should also be considered.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: A negative sputum AFB does not rule out extrapulmonary tuberculosis, and a negative peritoneal TB culture does not fully exclude TB; diagnosis sometimes requires biopsy to identify the organism.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: Adenosine deaminase (ADA) testing for tuberculosis is both fairly sensitive and specific, with a sensitivity around 93%.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: Disseminated coccidioidomycosis should be considered in a patient from an endemic area (e.g., the West Coast/San Diego) with pulmonary opacification and chronic inflammation, and it can involve the peritoneum causing ascites.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: Poor diabetes control is significantly associated with increased risk of disseminated coccidioidomycosis and relapse.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: Disseminated coccidioidomycosis can be treated with fluconazole, with clinical improvement.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: Failure to improve on broad-spectrum antibiotics is a clue to consider non-bacterial etiologies, including fungal infections.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: Influenza has been associated with an increased risk of myocardial infarction, as the flu-related inflammatory process can set patients up for ACS.

episode_title: #132 A Case of Abdominal Pain and Eosinophilia
statement: A normal-sized cardiac silhouette on chest x-ray argues against pericarditis or myocarditis, which typically enlarge or distort the silhouette.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Depression and suicidal thoughts should be considered occupational hazards of training and practicing medicine, affecting most physicians at some point.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: While only about 4% of interns qualify as clinically depressed at the start of intern year, roughly 41.8% will meet criteria for clinical depression at some point during intern year.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Physicians should establish care with a therapist while healthy, before a crisis, because it takes time to find someone you connect with and depression itself saps the energy needed to seek care.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: By the time a colleague is visibly making mistakes or struggling at work, they have likely been sick for a long time, because physicians tend to prioritize work above everything else.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Sleep deprivation is an independent risk factor for suicidal thoughts, which should inform how we structure work hours and whether we shame residents for napping.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Around 28 states ask on licensing applications whether you have ever been treated for a mental health disorder, but licenses are very rarely denied for a yes answer—usually only more documentation is required.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Being appropriately treated for depression does not mean you cannot safely practice medicine; questions about conditions impairing your ability to practice can honestly be answered no when you are treated.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: When worried about a colleague, take the extra step to directly ask whether they are having thoughts of hurting themselves—shifting from the friend role to the doctor role—rather than only normalizing their feelings.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: For clinicians who take care of clinicians, 'burnout' can be a dangerous euphemism because depressed physicians may label themselves burned out, and the term lets administrators treat the problem as less serious than depression.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Substance abuse, particularly alcohol used to unwind, is common in physicians beginning in residency, and clinicians should critically reflect on the role alcohol plays in their lives during training.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: For highly empathetic clinicians, the goal is not simply 'be more empathetic' but to treat empathy as a discipline—recognizing others' emotions while maintaining personal boundaries and emotional health.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Model programs such as Stanford general surgery use debriefing sessions run by mental health professionals who have no evaluative role in the residency, so residents can share without feeling pressured.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Program directors and chief residents should avoid becoming unintentional barriers to care—requiring residents to get permission or notify supervisors before attending therapy is inappropriate and deters those who need it most.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: Residents spend as little as seven minutes of face time per patient while doing roughly two hours of documentation for every hour of clinical care, contributing to work compression that duty hours alone do not address.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: The doubling time of medical knowledge has shrunk from about seven years in 1980 to roughly 73 days, compounding the cognitive load on trainees.

episode_title: #129 Depression and Suicide: Occupational Hazards of Practicing Medicine
statement: There appears to be a spike in physician suicide during the first year out in practice or in fellowship, driven largely by loss of support system, purpose, and increased isolation.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: The normal potassium range used in most hospitals is 3.5 to 5.0 mEq/L, and there are no strong guidelines defining the ideal potassium level for an acute MI patient.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Guidelines (AHA/ACC) recommend targeting potassium greater than 4 mainly in patients with ventricular fibrillation or ventricular tachycardia to prevent recurrent arrhythmia, not for all acute MI patients.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: A 2012 JAMA observational study of ~40,000 acute MI patients showed a U-shaped mortality curve, with lowest mortality at potassium 3.5-4.4 and increased mortality (~10%) at 4.5-4.9 and ~11% below 3.5.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Repleting potassium as a rapid IV bolus can precipitate fatal arrhythmias in patients with an excitable heart, since rapid changes in potassium affect cardiac activity.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: For acute MI patients, target potassium 3.5-4.0 based on observational data; replete if between 3 and 3.5, and avoid pushing above 4.5.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: For patients without high-risk conditions, a potassium above 3 is acceptable; a higher goal of over 3.5 is reasonable for acute MI, acute heart failure, and cirrhosis at risk for hepatic encephalopathy.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Increasing supplemental oxygen dramatically raises PO2 but only increases total oxygen content by about 6%, so raising the PO2 from 100 to 500 delivers little additional oxygen to tissues.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Increased PO2 causes vessels to vasoconstrict because oxygen free radicals (superoxide, hydrogen peroxide) are toxic, which can reduce blood delivery to tissues including the heart and brain.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: A 2015 randomized controlled trial in Circulation found that acute MI patients given supplemental oxygen (8 L) had increased recurrent MI and larger infarct size compared to those given no supplemental oxygen.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Hypercarbia from oxygen in acute COPD exacerbation is primarily driven by loss of hypoxic vasoconstriction in the lung, not just the traditionally taught loss of hypoxic drive.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: For acute MI and acute stroke, 2018 BMJ recommendations advise against supplemental oxygen if saturation is above 92% (strong) or above 90% (weak), and to turn oxygen down or off for other patients if above 96%.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: A practical bedside approach is to not start supplemental oxygen if saturation is 90% or above, actively wean if above 96%, and use a lower threshold of 88% for acute COPD.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Supplemental oxygen should not be routinely used to treat dyspnea, because dyspnea often has a non-hypoxemic mechanism that oxygen does not address.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Delirium is an acute, fluctuating change in cognition characterized most importantly by inattention, distinguishing it from dementia.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Asking the patient the day of the week and to recite the months backwards is a sensitive bedside screen for delirium (sensitivity ~93%, specificity ~64%), good for ruling delirium out.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Up to a third of hospitalized patients older than 70 develop delirium during their stay.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: There are no medications proven to prevent delirium in the hospital except possibly melatonin/ramelteon, which may reduce delirium occurrence and shorten ICU stay with little downside.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Antipsychotics, benzodiazepines, and acetylcholinesterase inhibitors do not effectively treat delirium; benzodiazepines should only be used in withdrawal.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: The American Geriatrics Society recommends against prescribing antipsychotics or benzodiazepines to treat older adults with delirium who are not agitated and threatening substantial harm to self or others.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Antipsychotics carry FDA black box warnings (atypicals since 2005, typicals ~2008) and cause real harms including EPS, aspiration pneumonia, and arrhythmia, with increased death in dementia patients.

episode_title: #195 TWDFNR 3: Potassium, Oxygen, and Antipsychotics
statement: Delirium management should focus on non-pharmacologic measures: remove unnecessary IVs/catheters/lines, stop unnecessary labs and vitals, correct metabolic disturbances, treat pain and infection, mobilize patients, and let them sleep.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Sarcopenia is now considered a muscle-specific disease state (analogous to chronic skeletal muscle failure), with the definition shifting emphasis from absolute muscle mass toward muscle strength.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Sarcopenia and cachexia are not interchangeable: most cachectic patients are sarcopenic, but sarcopenic patients may not have cachexia; frailty is a broader age-related syndrome that also includes psychological and social dimensions.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Sarcopenia can occur at any age, not just in the elderly; acute sarcopenia can develop rapidly, with loss of up to 30–40% of skeletal muscle mass in the first 7–10 days of hospitalization for multi-system organ failure.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Hand grip strength is a well-validated measure that increases up to age 20–30, declines mildly between 30–50, then accelerates after 50; on average someone may lose up to 50% of skeletal muscle mass between ages 20 and 80.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Up to 25% of the population over age 65 has sarcopenia by current diagnostic criteria, and low BMI/underweight status correlates well with low muscle mass.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: A repeat chair sit-to-stand test (stand and sit five times with arms crossed) taking longer than 15 seconds is a positive screen for low physical performance in sarcopenia.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: A gait speed cutoff of 0.8 meters per second can be used as a physical performance measure for sarcopenia and is easy to integrate into clinic workflow.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Calf circumference less than 31 cm was an original sarcopenia screening cutoff but is prone to error, particularly false negatives in older patients with edema masking muscle loss.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: In hospitalized patients, a very low creatinine can serve as a proxy for low muscle mass; for men an admission creatinine dropping below about 0.7 (down to 0.4) is associated with progressively increased in-hospital mortality, and for women the cutoff is around 0.4-0.3.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: In COPD patients, quadriceps strength is a better predictor of mortality than FEV1, and resistance training may be more feasible and beneficial than aerobic activity.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: The 2018 Physical Activity Guidelines recommend twice-weekly resistance training plus 150–300 minutes of moderate-intensity or 75–150 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity per week, achievable in 5-minute bouts.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Exercise intensity can be communicated via the talk test: able to talk in complete sentences but not sing = moderate intensity; able only to speak in short sentences with breathlessness = vigorous intensity.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: The 10,000 steps-per-day target is based on marketing, not evidence; over 4,000 steps/day is associated with lower all-cause mortality and CVD, with further benefit at 7,500 steps/day.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: The injury risk from resistance training is very low (about 1–2 injuries per 1,000 participation hours), comparable to walking (about 1.2 per 1,000 hours), and recommending walking alone is an underdosed intervention for sarcopenia.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: The APTA Choosing Wisely guidelines advise against prescribing underdosed resistance training programs to older adults, as underdosing is a common problem with medically prescribed exercise.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: For sarcopenic CKD patients advised to limit protein, favor protein sources high in essential amino acids, or consider essential amino acid supplementation, which has shown benefit with resistance training in small RCTs.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: High protein intake is not associated with harm in healthy individuals; long-term data show intakes up to 4.4 g/kg/day for a year without adverse health outcomes (though GI distress may occur).

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: When recommending protein supplements, look for a cGMP stamp (contents match label, low contamination risk) and, for drug-tested athletes, an NSF or Informed Sport certification.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: A practical way to communicate protein targets is a palm-sized portion (~25-35 g / 4-5 oz protein) at each of 3-5 meals per day to reach recommended intake.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Chronic inflammatory and disease states cause anabolic resistance—a subnormal muscle-building response to dietary protein and exercise—which makes secondary sarcopenia harder to treat and often requires increasing the dose of intervention.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Even with underdosed resistance training (bands and light ankle weights) plus essential amino acids, an RCT showed sarcopenic patients gained ~3% muscle mass, ~9% muscle strength, and normalized gait speed within 3 months.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: There is large inter-individual variability in response to resistance training across ages and sexes; setting achievable physical function goals is more useful than promising specific strength gains.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Depression and suicide are occupational hazards of training and practicing medicine, given the inherently traumatic nature of caring for the sick and dying.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Physicians should connect with a therapist proactively—starting in medical school and before a crisis—since it can take time to find someone you connect with and you may lack energy once depressed.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Substance abuse is common among physicians beginning in residency; clinicians should reflect on the role alcohol is playing in their lives, especially during training.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: About 28 states ask on licensing applications whether you have ever been treated for a mental health disorder, but very few physicians are actually denied a license for answering yes—usually only additional documentation is required.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: At the start of intern year only about 4% of interns meet criteria for clinical depression, but at some point during intern year 41.8% will have clinical depression.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Sleep deprivation is an independent risk factor for suicidal thoughts, which should inform how we structure work hours and whether we shame residents for napping.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: By the time a colleague is visibly making mistakes or struggling at work, they have likely been sick for a long time, because physicians prioritize work above everything else.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: When worried about a struggling colleague, step out of the friend role and explicitly ask whether they are having thoughts of hurting themselves.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Depression and suicidal thoughts often coexist but do not have to; passive suicidal ideation—reckless behavior or wishing something would happen—is common and reported by many physicians during training and early career.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Highest burnout on surveys is typically seen in younger, early-career physicians and trainees.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: The doubling time of medical knowledge has shrunk from about 7 years in 1980 to closer to 73 days, contributing to work compression and the strain on physicians.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Residents spend roughly seven minutes of face time per patient while doing about two hours of documentation for every hour of clinical care.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: The term 'burnout' can be a dangerous euphemism when patients are actually clinically depressed, and it can give administrations cover to treat the problem as less serious; naming depression conveys appropriate severity.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: On licensing applications, do not lie about mental health history but disclose only what is required; being appropriately treated for depression does not mean you cannot safely practice medicine.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Debriefing sessions for residents should be run by mental health professionals who have no evaluative role in the residency, so trainees are not pressured about what they share.

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Program directors and chief residents should not require permission or notification to attend therapy, as such requirements become unintentional barriers that deter people who most need care.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Set clear, explicit expectations with your team upfront, as frustration most often arises when an unsaid expectation goes unmet.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Adapt your leadership approach based on whether an intern is able and/or willing: build up the confidence of the able-but-unwilling, avoid micromanaging the able-and-willing, and set specific triggers/close supervision for the unable-but-willing, who are the most dangerous combination.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: For interns you supervise closely, give explicit call parameters (e.g., hypotension, tachycardia, angry family, angry consultant, crucial lab value) so they know when to reach you.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Avoid pre-labeling interns as 'good' or 'bad'—ability and willingness are situational and rotation-specific, not intrinsic properties, so meet each learner where they are.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Be deliberate about which rooms you enter to teach and what the teaching purpose is; teaching every teachable moment makes rounds too long, while missing them means no teaching happens.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Always explicitly label feedback as feedback, because if you're not explicit people mistake it for casual conversation or compliments.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: When addressing an intern who is struggling, first assess their level of insight with open then specific questions, and frame the conversation around whether the patient received our best rather than the learner's ego.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Frame corrective feedback as a collaborative 'next time try' rather than 'you did this wrong,' inviting the learner to problem-solve alongside you.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: The highest-yield studying strategy is reading about your own patients and their specific unanswered clinical questions, which sticks far better than reading a textbook cover to cover.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
