statement: Review articles such as Annals 'In the Clinic' and NEJM/Annals reviews are excellent for building the breadth of basics and clinical reasoning, before drilling into primary literature.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Residents and students should focus on question banks, textbook knowledge, and review articles rather than trying to scan the primary literature—keeping up with primary journals is largely an attending-level task.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Always close the loop when you assign a learner to look something up; if they never get to report back, they will stop doing the reading.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: As a resident-leader, keep the intern approachable to you—if trainees stop bringing you problems, they either think you don't care or can't help, so respond to every problem with 'thank you.'

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Anyone who is sexually active should be screened for HIV at least once, because the test is straightforward, false positives are vanishingly rare, and early diagnosis has strong benefits.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: For screening in a longitudinal primary care setting, order the standard fourth-generation HIV 1/2 antibody/antigen test, which detects both established infection and the P24 antigen present in acute infection.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: The fourth-generation test will miss only patients infected within the last 10 to 12 days.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: The P24 antigen turns positive around 15 to 20 days (as early as 12 days) after infection, HIV RNA around day 10, and antibody around day 30.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Patients in stable low-risk relationships may need testing only every 5 to 10 years, while those with frequent partners should be tested every 3 to 4 months and offered pre-exposure prophylaxis.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Any time you treat a patient for a new sexually transmitted infection, you should check their HIV status.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Rapid oral HIV tests are close but slightly less accurate (about 98%) than fourth-generation serum tests (99.5-99.7%); a negative rapid test in a higher-risk patient should be followed up with a standard test.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: In the United States there is little reason to distinguish HIV-1 from HIV-2; HIV-2 is mostly found in West Africa, so check for it only in patients from or traveling to that region.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: An HIV diagnosis should always be delivered in person, never over the phone, so you can read the patient's reaction, and the patient should return with a support person since they retain little after hearing the result.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: A patient on treatment with an undetectable viral load will not transmit HIV to anyone through sexual activity.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: The confirmatory test after a positive antibody/antigen screen is an HIV RNA (viral load); almost universally an antibody-positive patient will have detectable HIV RNA.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: At the initial diagnosis visit, check a CD4 count, viral hepatitis screening, broad STI screening (including RPR and site-specific NAAT for GC/chlamydia), and an HLA-B*5701.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Check HLA-B*5701 before prescribing abacavir; positive patients (about 5% of Caucasians, 1% of blacks) are at risk of a hypersensitivity reaction that can be fatal on rechallenge.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: The goal is to get patients started on antiretroviral therapy within about two weeks of diagnosis; rapid same-day initiation improves outcomes especially where access to care is limited.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Preferred first-line HIV regimens are integrase inhibitor-based (dolutegravir or bictegravir based), chosen for potency and a very low, well-tolerated side effect profile since therapy is lifelong.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: If starting same-day therapy, lean toward a bictegravir-based regimen; dolutegravir is co-formulated with abacavir, which requires an HLA-B*5701 result before use.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Bictegravir and dolutegravir are glucuronidated rather than metabolized by CYP3A4, so there are few significant drug-drug interactions (statins, antacids, etc.), though calcium/antacids require attention to timing with food.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: In women who could become pregnant, avoid dolutegravir (and possibly bictegravir) due to potential fetal toxicity and use efavirenz-based therapy instead.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: HIV patients are at increased cardiovascular risk even when virally suppressed, roughly equivalent to diabetes, due to chronic inflammation persisting despite viral suppression.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Patients having receptive anal intercourse should undergo rectal pap smears at intervals similar to cervical pap screening.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Once the viral load is suppressed below detection and the CD4 count has risen above 250 with three or four undetectable viral loads in a row, you can stop checking CD4 counts as it no longer changes management.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Once suppressed, monitor viral load every six months; the CD4 count is a marker of how long/how damaged the immune system has been, not of immune function, and only becomes clinically meaningful for OI risk when below 200.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: It is the circulating high-level viremia binding immune cell receptors that causes immune dysfunction; removing the virus with therapy allows immune function to return, which drives the immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) seen 4 to 6 weeks after treating advanced disease.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: After completely stopping antiretroviral therapy the virus usually rebounds about 2 to 4 weeks later; upon restarting, viral replication shuts down within days to a week or two and the CD4 count can rise quickly.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: The CD4 count is a calculation (total white count x percent lymphocytes x percent CD4), so when values fluctuate the CD4 percentage is a more reliable marker to interpret.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: A single no-show visit is associated with roughly a 2.3 relative risk of death, so minimizing missed visits and re-engaging patients is a key priority.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Start Pneumocystis (PCP) prophylaxis with one double-strength bactrim daily when CD4 is below 200, and continue until CD4 rises above 200 or, with a suppressed viral load for over a year, CD4 stays between 100 and 200.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) prophylaxis is no longer recommended even at CD4 below 50, because modern antiretroviral therapy restores immunity quickly.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Once virally suppressed, HIV patients should receive routine primary care essentially the same as anyone else, with expert consultation reserved for special situations like live vaccines (e.g., yellow fever) in travelers.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Primary care physicians can refer newly diagnosed HIV patients to a local Ryan White-funded clinic or HIV provider; the HIV Medical Association (part of IDSA) can help find providers.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: If you do not routinize taking a sexual history, you will not know your host and won't know what to include on your differential diagnosis for STIs.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: A syphilitic chancre is classically painless and firm, distinguishing it from the painful, undermined ulcer of chancroid.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: The primary chancre appears on average about 30 days after contact (range 10 to 90 days) and typically heals before secondary syphilis develops.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: The secondary syphilis rash involves the palms and soles only about half the time, but if you do see palms and soles you should think about syphilis.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: In syphilitic hepatitis, the alkaline phosphatase is elevated out of proportion to the transaminases (e.g., transaminases in the hundreds but alk phos around 800).

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Early symptomatic neurosyphilis can present with ocular involvement (uveitis), otic symptoms like sudden sensorineural hearing loss, vertigo or tinnitus, cranial nerve abnormalities, or aseptic meningitis.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: About 75% of untreated early syphilis patients enter latency with no further symptoms, while about 25% progress to late syphilis (neurologic, cardiovascular, or gummatous disease).

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Diagnosing syphilis requires two serologic tests: a non-treponemal test (RPR or VDRL) and a specific treponemal test (e.g., TPPA), typically one of each flavor.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: The reverse screening algorithm starts with an automated specific treponemal immunoassay; if positive, an RPR is done, and if discordant, a TPPA tiebreaker test is performed.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Non-treponemal tests (RPR, VDRL) are quantitative and used to assess disease activity, help stage, and follow response to treatment.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Early syphilis (primary, secondary, and early latent) is treated with a single dose of penicillin, whereas late latent syphilis requires a longer duration of penicillin.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: When duration of latent syphilis is unknown, default to treating as late latent because it is more conservative to presume slower spirochete replication requiring longer therapy.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Warn patients treated for secondary syphilis about a possible Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (shaking chills and fever 12-24 hours later), which is normal and can be managed with acetaminophen.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Patients with infectious syphilis are considered non-infectious after one week of treatment, so advise no sex for a week.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Sexually active women under 25 should be screened for chlamydia and gonorrhea, as about 80-85% of chlamydia occurs in this age group.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Nucleic acid amplification tests are sensitive only at the site of exposure, so rectal and pharyngeal swabs are needed for those with receptive anal or oral exposure; urine will miss these focal infections.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: For women, self-collected vaginal swabs are FDA-cleared and more sensitive than urine testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea, so ditch the urine on women.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: For male urine collection, have the patient collect the first third of a cup (urethral fraction) and finish in the toilet, which is 95-96% sensitive.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Gonorrhea is treated with dual therapy (ceftriaxone plus azithromycin) even if chlamydia has been ruled out, to hinder emergence of resistance.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: The current recommendation for gonorrhea is ceftriaxone 250 mg IM plus azithromycin 1 gram as dual therapy.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: For cephalosporin-allergic patients with gonorrhea, an alternative is gemifloxacin plus gentamicin 240 mg IM, though gemifloxacin shortages may force use of azithromycin 2 grams.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Mycoplasma genitalium is an independent predictor of cervicitis, male urethritis, PID, and probably adverse birth outcomes; moxifloxacin appears to be the go-to drug for azithromycin-resistant cases.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Single-dose treatment for chlamydia (azithromycin 1 gram) is preferred over 7-day doxycycline because compliance with doxycycline is only about 38%.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: For chlamydia, gonorrhea, and trichomonas, rescreening (not test of cure) is recommended at 3 to 4 months, primarily as a proxy for reinfection from untreated partners.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Test of cure is reserved for cases treated with a non-first-line antibiotic (e.g., due to allergy), for resistant gonorrhea, or in pregnant women, done about 3 to 4 weeks later.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Expedited partner therapy allows you to prescribe treatment for a patient's partner (up to three partners) and is legal in all but five states, having been shown to lower reinfection rates.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Most people with any STI have no symptoms, which is why asymptomatic screening is important.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Disseminated gonococcal infection presents with tender pustules and a few peripheral lesions, not a symmetrically distributed rash or oral lesions.

episode_title: #127 STIs Syphilis, Gonorrhea and Chlamydia
statement: Condylomata lata are heaped-up, wide, flat-topped lesions of secondary syphilis in intertriginous areas that can look like warts and are teeming with organisms (dark-field positive).

episode_title: #56: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 1: Living better through chemicals
statement: Bupropion is a reasonable first-line antidepressant choice in patients particularly concerned about weight gain or sexual side effects, especially when depression is the low-energy type rather than the anxious type.

episode_title: #56: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 1: Living better through chemicals
statement: Bupropion can lower the seizure threshold, so it should be used with extreme caution or avoided in patients with a history of alcohol use disorder or seizures.

episode_title: #56: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 1: Living better through chemicals
statement: Bupropion is a good option to augment an SSRI in patients who have partially responded but need additional benefit.

episode_title: #56: Polypharmacy and deprescribing Part 1: Living better through chemicals
statement: Blood pressure should be monitored when using bupropion.

episode_title: #60: Masters of MKSAP on Medical Education
statement: Guidelines often advocate treating symptomatic isolated distal DVT, though serial ultrasound monitoring is an option in some circumstances.

episode_title: #60: Masters of MKSAP on Medical Education
statement: For DOAC dosing, patients generally have a wide therapeutic index and are okay even with BMI over 40, as long as they are not more than 150 kg.

episode_title: #60: Masters of MKSAP on Medical Education
statement: Post-bariatric surgery patients are a group of concern for anticoagulation because absorption can be altered; in these cases consider monitoring anti-Xa (10A) levels.

episode_title: #60: Masters of MKSAP on Medical Education
statement: When evaluating apparent anticoagulation treatment failure, press the patient on adherence, missed doses, and new medications to determine whether it was a true failure or an extenuating circumstance.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: In a randomized trial from the UK National Health Service, e-cigarettes achieved an 18% smoking abstinence rate at one year versus 10% for nicotine replacement therapy, with abstinence verified by carbon monoxide levels.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: For comparison, nicotine replacement plus bupropion has about a 20% quit rate at a year, and varenicline about a 26% abstinence rate at 24 weeks.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: About 80% of people who quit smoking with e-cigarettes were still vaping at one year, raising concern about substituting one nicotine addiction for another with unknown long-term effects.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: First-line smoking cessation should still favor varenicline or nicotine replacement therapy; e-cigarettes may be considered only after FDA-approved therapies have failed.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: In a retrospective study of patients 65 and older diagnosed with UTI, deferred or no antibiotic treatment was associated with higher all-cause mortality and higher rates of bloodstream infections, especially in men over 85 from lower socioeconomic status.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: Asymptomatic bacteriuria discovered incidentally does not require antibiotic treatment unless the patient has another infection requiring them.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: Updated UK guidelines recommend treating uncomplicated cystitis in women for three days with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or nitrofurantoin, or a single dose of fosfomycin; men get trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or nitrofurantoin for seven days.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: A complicated UTI is now defined as one where the patient is systemically ill (e.g., systemic symptoms, tachycardia, sepsis physiology), and these patients can be treated longer.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: In the PIONEER-HF trial, sacubitril/valsartan was initiated in hospitalized patients with acute decompensated heart failure once clinically stabilized (not hypotensive, off inotropes), compared to enalapril 10 mg twice daily.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: In PIONEER-HF, sacubitril/valsartan reduced CHF rehospitalization compared to enalapril (8% vs 14%), and safety outcomes such as hyperkalemia, AKI, symptomatic hypotension, and angioedema were not significantly different between arms.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: Starting an ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) requires a 36-hour washout from a prior ACE inhibitor or ARB, and angioedema risk should be considered.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: BNP or NT-proBNP levels should be checked for prognosis (e.g., on admission or discharge) rather than to guide acute therapy, and trials targeting these levels have not shown benefit.

episode_title: #160 Hotcakes: E-cigarettes, UTIs, Heart Failure
statement: Arranging outpatient follow-up within 30 days of heart failure discharge can lower the rehospitalization rate from just north of 20% down to about 13-14%.

episode_title: #68: Tremors: One degree of Kevin Bacon?
statement: The classic 12-month trying-to-conceive requirement before starting an infertility workup is unnecessarily strict and mainly applies to patients under 35 without risk factors.

episode_title: #68: Tremors: One degree of Kevin Bacon?
statement: If a patient has an underlying condition that may impact fertility, such as PCOS, endometriosis, or sexual dysfunction, you can start the infertility workup without waiting a full year.

episode_title: #68: Tremors: One degree of Kevin Bacon?
statement: Patients requiring donor gametes or embryos, including same-sex couples, can be referred to reproductive endocrinology right away rather than waiting.

episode_title: #68: Tremors: One degree of Kevin Bacon?
statement: STI testing should be ordered before patients undergo a fertility workup, and ordering these labs in primary care may make them more likely to be covered by insurance.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Refugees, asylees/asylum seekers, and migrants differ legally: a refugee has fled persecution and been granted legal protected status, an asylee is fleeing persecution but has not yet been granted protection and is going through a prolonged legal process, and a migrant moves for educational, economic, or family opportunities.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: The pre-departure visa medical examination is a broad general physical and mental exam looking for active TB, serious mental illness, and other glaring illnesses; it does not screen for latent TB or chronic diseases.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: The CDC initial domestic medical exam screens for latent/active TB, hepatitis B, and syphilis, tests for chlamydia and gonorrhea in high-risk patients, updates vaccinations, and may include a CBC and BMP with glucose; HIV is no longer recommended pre-departure but is recommended post-arrival.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Since records of prior screening are often unavailable and guidelines are inconsistently implemented, providers should try to obtain records and repeat tests or preventive screenings that cannot be confirmed.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: For vaccinations, Tdap and MMR have usually been given prior to departure, but hepatitis B, varicella, Pneumovax, and flu shots often have not; for hepatitis B and varicella, check titers rather than automatically revaccinating.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Refugees have federal medical assistance for about eight months after resettlement, after which many become uninsured or struggle to apply for Medicaid, leading to fragmented care and often presenting with uncontrolled chronic conditions and multiple complaints.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Use official interpreters rather than family members when caring for refugee patients.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Mental health conditions (anxiety, adjustment disorder, depression, PTSD) are among the most common chronic problems in refugees; PTSD and depression rates may be as high as 30%, and suicide rates are about twice that of US-born individuals.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Depression and anxiety in refugees often present as somatic symptoms such as headache, fatigue, abdominal pain, or insomnia rather than classic complaints of sadness.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Validated trauma screening tools for refugees include the Harvard Trauma Questionnaire and the Hopkins Symptom Checklist; the PCL-5 (developed for veterans and mapped to DSM-5), GAD-7, and PHQ-9 can also be used.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: The CDC recommends screening all refugees for trauma with two questions: whether they experienced violence or trauma in their former country, and whether they would like to talk about it.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Chronic illnesses such as diabetes and hypertension present at younger ages in refugee populations, so check an A1c and lipid profile even in younger refugee patients rather than following standard USPSTF age thresholds.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Screen refugees for hepatitis B and C, especially those from sub-Saharan Africa, Hmong populations in Thailand, or Burmese populations, since rates are high and screening provides a chance to prevent hepatocellular carcinoma.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Most refugees are empirically treated for helminths before migration; test stool for ova and parasites and schistosomiasis only in those from endemic water-rich areas, and use serologic testing for Strongyloides since it is more sensitive than stool.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Strongyloides is important to detect because it persists in the same host and can cause a hyperinfection syndrome with high mortality if the patient becomes immunocompromised.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: A migration history is a key part of the social history: ask openly about where the patient was born, when and how long they lived in refugee camps, access to food/education/healthcare, the migration process, and the resettlement experience.

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: When taking a past medical history, refugees may answer that they have no medical problems, so ask indirectly—whether they were taking medications, when they last saw a doctor, or had surgeries.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: For adults, at least 7 hours of sleep per night is generally recommended; less than 5 hours or greater than 10 hours is considered pathologic and warrants evaluation.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Insomnia and short sleep are associated with cardiovascular disease; in one Norwegian study of over 50,000 patients naive to cardiac disease, those with insomnia plus short sleep hours were four times at risk for an acute MI.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: First-line therapy for chronic insomnia is cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I), recommended by both the American Academy of Sleep Medicine and the American College of Physicians, rather than sedatives or alcohol.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Online CBT-I programs (e.g., SHUTi) and a free VA app called the Sleep Coach are effective resources when access to in-person CBT-I is limited.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Because women metabolize zolpidem differently and have higher levels with residual driving impairment, the FDA recommended in 2013 lowering the ambien dose in women to 5 mg (immediate release) or 6.25 mg (extended release).

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Z-drugs (non-benzodiazepine hypnotics) are intended for short-term use, and their most worrisome side effects are parasomnias such as sleep walking, sleep eating, and sleep driving.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: CBT-I can help patients taper down or discontinue chronic sleep aids, and after years of use the effect is often more psychological than physiologic.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Prophylactic naps should generally be kept short (20 to 40 minutes) to improve alertness for at least two hours; longer naps risk sleep inertia and interfere with nighttime sleep.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Melatonin is best used for circadian rhythm disorders, and physiologic doses as low as 0.5 mg are effective, whereas common over-the-counter doses of 3, 5, or 10 mg are supraphysiologic.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: When melatonin is used for insomnia, it should be taken at the same time every night, since inconsistent timing shifts the circadian rhythm and can worsen sleep.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Testosterone is secreted during sleep and peaks about three hours in; sleep deprivation, particularly in the first three hours, lowers testosterone, and improving sleep may raise levels.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: In OSA, low testosterone is driven primarily by obesity rather than the apnea itself; weight loss increases testosterone, while CPAP effects on testosterone are equivocal.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Adolescents need more sleep than commonly assumed (at least 10 and up to 12 hours) and tend to be phase-delayed, contributing to chronic sleep deprivation with early school start times.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Pathologically phase-delayed adolescents can be effectively treated to advance their circadian rhythm using melatonin and light therapy.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Shift work has been declared a probable carcinogen and is associated with increased cardiovascular disease and cancer, affecting an estimated 10 to 15 percent of the US workforce.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Women often have REM-predominant OSA, and because REM sleep apnea may drive cardiovascular outcomes, women matched to men for age and BMI have equivalent REM apnea-hypopnea severity, risking underdiagnosis when only total AHI is used.

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Consumer sleep trackers are reasonably good at detecting sleep but poor at detecting wake and inaccurate at distinguishing sleep stages, so patients should not rely on their sleep-stage data.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: The first two questions of the PHQ-9 (feeling down/sad and loss of interest in activities) are a good, patient-completed screen for geriatric depression.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: In older adults, depression often presents as anxiety or irritability rather than the classic sad, tearful picture.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Older patients who repeatedly present with vague complaints for no clear reason may be depressed or anxious—consider doing a PHQ-9.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Always rule out contributors that mimic depression in older adults, including pain, constipation, infection, apnea/hypoxia, and hypothyroidism—check the TSH if not done recently.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Do not forget suicide risk in the elderly—most elderly people who die by suicide have visited their primary care doctor within two to three weeks beforehand.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Men tend to use more lethal means such as guns for suicide, while women more often use overdoses—ask directly about access to firearms and offer to have the gun or ammunition held by a trusted friend.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: For a frail older patient who isn't sleeping and isn't eating, mirtazapine is a good first choice because it is sedating and stimulates appetite.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Among SSRIs, favor agents with fewer drug interactions such as escitalopram or sertraline over citalopram or fluoxetine.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Bupropion is stimulating and can reduce appetite, may worsen anxiety, and should be avoided in drinkers because it lowers the seizure threshold.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: 'Start low and go slow' is reasonable, but if a patient has no side effects and no benefit, you must push the dose—a low-dose SSRI that was never titrated is not a treatment failure.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Counsel patients that antidepressants take four to six weeks to reach full effect, and eight to twelve weeks if started slowly, but the neurovegetative symptoms (sleep, appetite, energy) usually improve first.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Serial PHQ-9 scores let you track progress and, as in the IMPACT collaborative care model, signal when to increase the dose if scores plateau or fail to fall.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: To augment a partially effective antidepressant, add an agent with a different mechanism—e.g., add bupropion (dopamine) to an SSRI, or mirtazapine or bupropion to an SNRI.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Options for augmenting depression treatment include atypical antipsychotics (aripiprazole is FDA-approved), lamotrigine, lithium, and thyroid hormone.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: When using atypical antipsychotics (e.g., quetiapine, olanzapine), monitor with EKG for QTc, plus triglyceride and hemoglobin A1c levels.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: For a first episode of depression, continue the antidepressant for six months to a year after recovery; there is about a 30% chance of relapse if stopped.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: After a second episode, treat for at least two years after recovery; there is a 50-60% chance of a third episode, and after a third episode recurrence is nearly certain, favoring indefinite treatment.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: When starting an SSRI in a patient also taking aspirin, consider adding a proton pump inhibitor to protect the stomach.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: An efficient office workflow is to hand patients a PHQ-2 in the waiting room; if positive, they complete a PHQ-9 before seeing you, making depression screening feasible at every or quarterly visits.

episode_title: #115 Geriatric Depression: Diagnosis, Antidepressants, and More
statement: Referring a suicidal patient to a hospital or urgent psychiatry center does not mean they will be admitted—an expert will evaluate and produce a better risk assessment.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: You can't compare your insides to somebody else's outsides—we compare our internal insecurities to how others project themselves outwardly, which is an unfair comparison.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Consider framing wellness as 'work-life integration' rather than 'work-life balance,' since the latter falsely assumes work only drains you and life only rejuvenates you—when in fact both can do either.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: The 'three good things' or gratitude journal exercise—writing down three good things each night for a week and your role in them—is evidence-based: happiness scores rise and depression scores fall for up to six months afterward.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: If you do the three good things exercise for a week, happiness scores rise and depression scores go down up to six months out, with sustained responses and no risk to trying it.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Employee Assistance Programs are run through HR, separate from your residency program, offering confidential therapy or a place to debrief difficult situations without going through your program director.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Interns should not self-prescribe—if you think you might benefit from medication such as an SSRI, the right approach is to find your own primary care doctor rather than using your prescribing ability on yourself.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: A 2015 program director survey (Angus, UConn) found the top four qualities of a successful intern were organization and time management, effective communication, basic clinical skills, and knowing when to ask for assistance—not medical knowledge.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: For fourth-year rotations to prepare for internal medicine internship, do a medical ICU/intensive care rotation and an emergency medicine rotation so your first exposure to critically ill and undifferentiated patients isn't during internship.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Use SMART goals for self-improvement: specific, measurable, assignable, relevant, and timely—e.g., 'for one admission a day I'm going to read the summary and recommendations of an UpToDate article relevant to that admission' rather than a vague 'read more.'

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: When presenting on rounds, commit to a firm plan rather than hedging with phrases like 'consider' or vague 'narrow antibiotics'—stating a definite plan (including the specific drug you'd switch to) helps you learn more when it's corrected.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: After rounds, prioritize seeing your unstable or uncertain patients first, then call consults, then complete discharges, and finally circle back to less urgent tasks like electrolyte repletion and notes.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Aim to complete discharges early in the day because it coordinates the plan of care and makes discharge safer—6 p.m. discharges never go well.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Always get verbal sign-out in addition to any written sign-out—night float teams are often too busy to write everything down, and you'll frequently learn important context by talking to them directly.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Use your newness early in intern year as an asset—everyone knows you're new, so being transparent about not knowing and asking nurses or pharmacists how things are usually done is a legitimate way to learn.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Ask nurses, pharmacists, and respiratory therapists for their input—e.g., ask the pharmacist about warfarin dosing—to gain information and build goodwill with the team.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Attend noon conference or your program's didactic session—it's one of the few things with any direct correlation to passing boards.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Intern year is about the apprenticeship of learning how to do the job—process, efficiency, and completing tasks (like placing orders and discharging patients) matter as much as clinical knowledge.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: As an intern, teaching medical students is best done by role-modeling your workflow (e.g., showing how you repletes electrolytes) rather than formal chalk talks—your primary focus should be getting your own work done.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: 'Soft mentors' are people you don't formally identify as mentors but from whom you copy effective behaviors (like a meaningful palliative care comment) and reject ineffective ones, building a repertoire of skills.

episode_title: #176 Survive Intern Year: Becoming a PGY1
statement: Personal wellness efforts can only take you so far—large systemic changes and institutional support are needed, as individual resilience won't protect you in the worst working conditions.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: Vaping has been associated with serious pulmonary illness, including cases requiring intubation and corticosteroids, prompting the CDC to urge clinicians to report possible cases to state and local health departments.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: Vaping-associated lung injury appears related to the delivery mechanism rather than the specific substance vaped, and affects patients across a wide age range (16 to 53 years).

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: A systematic review/meta-analysis found procalcitonin (common cutoff 0.4 mcg/L) was not clinically helpful, with sensitivity ~55% and specificity ~75-76% for guiding antibiotic decisions.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: In primary care patients with acute COPD exacerbation, point-of-care CRP testing (cutoff greater than 40) allowed avoiding antibiotics in about 1 in 5 patients with no difference in outcomes.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: The classic Anthonisen criteria for COPD exacerbation include increased dyspnea, increased sputum production, or increased sputum purulence.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: A CDC advisory committee revisited PCV13 (Prevnar) in adults over 65 and found it did not reduce invasive pneumonia, now recommending shared decision-making rather than routine administration.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: The polysaccharide vaccine PPV23 is less immunogenic than the conjugate PCV13, but PCV13 appears less important than previously thought in immunocompetent adults over 65, and PPV23 remains recommended.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: Impostor syndrome is a pattern where a person fears being outed as a fraud despite compelling evidence of competence, and it is thought to be more prevalent in women and underrepresented minorities.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: Impostor syndrome should be viewed as a systemic issue tied to structural factors (like lack of visible role models) rather than only an individual problem to be fixed with reassurance.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: Impostor syndrome and burnout appear closely entwined, forming a vicious cycle where worse impostor syndrome is associated with more burnout.

episode_title: #173 Hotcakes: Imposter syndrome, Vacations & metabolic syndrome, Pneumonia vaccine update, and More!
statement: A small retrospective study found that taking vacations more often was associated with reduced metabolic syndrome risk, with metabolic symptoms decreasing with each additional vacation taken.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: In COVID-19 patients, the threshold to intubate has been lower because of rapidly progressive hypoxemic respiratory failure with less lag time than seen with flu, and because non-invasive support increases aerosolization and spread risk.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: Standard ARDS strategies work in COVID-19: lung-protective ventilation, inhaled prostacyclin, neuromuscular blockers, and early proning.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: COVID-19 ARDS patients often present with marked hypoxemia but less stiff lungs and less hypercapnia, and applying a lot of PEEP has been helpful.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: High flow nasal cannula, non-invasive positive pressure (BiPAP), and most nebulizers aerosolize the virus and increase spread risk to staff.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: Avoid high flow nasal cannula or non-invasive positive pressure unless there are highly individualized reasons, since there is clear evidence this is risky to staff, especially with limited PPE and non-negative-pressure rooms.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: To reduce aerosolization risk, prefer inhalers with spacers over nebulizers for COVID-19 patients, avoiding nebulizers altogether when there is no strong indication.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: There is concern that healthcare workers may have higher mortality than expected for their age, possibly related to a higher inoculum dose from being in the room during aerosolizing procedures, so clinicians should be extremely cautious even if otherwise low-risk.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: For COVID-19 intubation, aim for first-best-success with the fewest people in the room, using the most senior anesthesiologist available.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: A useful four-step ICU hierarchy for COVID-19 care: protect yourself and your team, focus on proven ICU care, consider clinical trial enrollment, and remember compassion for isolated patients.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: Proper donning and doffing of PPE is essential, and clinicians should relearn correct technique rather than cutting corners like holding a mask or pulling up gown sleeves.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: Minimize room entries by calling the nurse before entering to consolidate tasks, and learn small tasks (like changing a pump) so staff do not have to re-enter the room.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: Innovations to conserve PPE and reduce exposure include moving IV pumps into the hallway and placing the ventilator control panel outside the room so settings can be adjusted without entering.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: Reported mortality is reassuringly low for people in their 30s (about 0.2%), while the chronically ill elderly face a truly deadly disease and neonates/children appear less often affected.

episode_title: Hotcakes: Exercise, Aspirin, Fish Oil and Hypertension
statement: When patients are already in the room and exposed (especially during intubating procedures), take an extra minute to provide human contact and comfort given how isolating this disease is.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Palpitations are frequently benign, especially in patients without structural heart disease, and do not warrant extensive evaluation in structurally normal hearts.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Atrial fibrillation is most commonly associated with structural cardiovascular disease, especially conditions that increase left atrial pressure and cause left atrial enlargement.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Obesity is a recognized risk factor for atrial fibrillation, and improving fitness and losing weight lowers AFib burden and improves long-term outcomes.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: The likely threshold for prognostically important atrial fibrillation is at least 6 minutes of sustained AFib and up to 24 hours, though shorter bursts tend to progress to longer episodes over time.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Initial workup for atrial fibrillation should include a limited lab panel with thyroid testing, renal function, liver tests, and a blood count, plus an echocardiogram for essentially all adult AFib patients.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Essentially all adult patients with atrial fibrillation should have an echocardiogram to look for associated structural cardiovascular disease.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Given the substantial overlap between AFib and sleep apnea, patients with risk factors or symptoms of sleep apnea should undergo a sleep study, since treating sleep apnea helps treat AFib.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: AFib causes two major problems: tachycardia (which can cause reversible cardiomyopathy with good rate control) and stroke risk (which persists whether or not the patient feels symptoms).

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Ischemic heart disease is low on the list of causes of atrial fibrillation; AFib and CAD are common conditions that share risk factors and run together, but there is little cause-and-effect relationship between them.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: The AFFIRM trial showed no difference in hard endpoints between rate and rhythm control, so there is no mandate to pursue rhythm control primarily to improve outcomes; the decision should be individualized based on symptom burden.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: The RACE trial compared strict rate control (resting HR less than 80) to lenient rate control (resting HR less than 110) and found no significant difference in hard endpoints; guidelines give a class 2A indication for strict control and 2B for lenient.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: A rhythm control strategy is always acceptable when rate control fails due to unacceptable side effects or inadequate rate control.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: In the CASTLE-AF trial, radiofrequency ablation in patients with HFrEF and paroxysmal AFib not suited for antiarrhythmics led to lower mortality, fewer heart failure admissions, and improved ejection fraction.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: The larger CABANA trial in a general AFib population (only about 9% with cardiomyopathy) showed no superiority of radiofrequency ablation over medical therapy for hard cardiovascular endpoints over about 5 years.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Young, vigorous endurance athletes with high vagal tone and little structural disease who develop AFib benefit from radiofrequency ablation with higher cure rates and should be referred to electrophysiology earlier.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Per the 2019 guidelines, anticoagulation is a class 1 indication in men with a CHADS2-VASc score of 2 or greater and women with a score of 3 or greater; women's automatic 1 point is only counted when a second risk factor is present.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: For men with a CHADS2-VASc score of zero or women with a score of one, it is reasonable to omit anticoagulation (class 2A); for men with a score of one or women with a score of two, anticoagulation is optional but acceptable (class 2B).

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Direct-acting oral anticoagulants are preferred over vitamin K antagonists for most patients with non-valvular atrial fibrillation who are eligible.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Valvular AFib requiring a vitamin K antagonist is now specifically defined as AFib associated with moderate to severe mitral stenosis or a mechanical heart valve; all other AFib is non-valvular and eligible for DOACs.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: DOACs show comparable or less bleeding risk than warfarin and specifically less intracranial hemorrhage, the most feared bleeding complication.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Triple antithrombotic therapy in AFib patients with coronary stents is being shortened, often to about a month or 4 to 6 weeks, followed by dual therapy with a drug like clopidogrel plus an anticoagulant; high bleeding-risk patients may go straight to dual therapy.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Immediate synchronized cardioversion is acceptable for unstable AFib patients such as those with refractory heart failure, cardiogenic shock, refractory hypotension, ongoing ischemia, or Wolff-Parkinson-White with rapid pre-excited AFib.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: For non-emergency cardioversion of AFib lasting 48 hours or more or of unknown duration, patients should either be anticoagulated 3 weeks prior (class 1) or undergo TEE to exclude intracardiac clot after starting anticoagulation (class 2A).

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: The atrium is stunned for hours to days after cardioversion with reduced contractile function even in sinus rhythm, so stroke risk peaks in the days after cardioversion; nearly all patients should be anticoagulated for at least 4 weeks afterward.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Even modest alcohol use can be a strong trigger for AFib in prone patients and should be emphasized, whereas caffeine is not strongly linked across the AFib population and most patients need not avoid coffee.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: AFib arising during critical illness or other reversible conditions is rarely cured after treating the condition; clinicians should resist labeling it purely reversible and generally anticoagulate based on the CHADS2-VASc score.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: Post-cardiac surgery (e.g., CABG) AFib is still often treated as a reversible cause, with short-term anticoagulation and extended ECG monitoring 4 to 6 weeks after surgery; patients without recurrence may get a pass on that episode.

episode_title: #159 Atrial Fibrillation Review and Update
statement: There is no good data supporting aspirin for stroke prophylaxis in AFib; anticoagulants, not antiplatelet drugs, are needed for thromboembolism reduction in atrial fibrillation.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: Remdesivir is an ATP analog that inhibits viral polymerases in vitro (including SARS and MERS), but was ineffective in a randomized controlled trial for Ebola and has no FDA-approved indication.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: The compassionate-use remdesivir regimen in the case series was 200 mg on day one followed by 100 mg daily for nine more days, for a total 10-day course in addition to usual care.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: The remdesivir case series (Grein et al., NEJM) enrolled 61 hospitalized patients with severe COVID-19 and had no control group, so causal inference about the drug's benefit is not possible.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: Case series and descriptive studies are still vulnerable to selection and ascertainment bias, so lack of a comparison group does not make them free of bias.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: In the remdesivir case series, median time from symptom onset to drug receipt was 12 days, meaning patients had to survive that long to be eligible—raising concern for a population predisposed to do better.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: The ISTH interim guidance recommends checking d-dimer, PT, and platelet count on all admitted COVID-19 patients, with d-dimer being the most important, as these can help guide care and predict severity.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: The ISTH recommends prophylactic low molecular weight heparin in all admitted COVID-19 patients without contraindications.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: Low molecular weight heparin is often preferred over unfractionated heparin in COVID-19 to reduce the number of nursing contacts and PPE used caring for these patients.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: In a Wuhan observational study of 449 patients, heparin showed no overall 28-day mortality difference, but among high-risk patients (d-dimer >6x ULN) treatment-dose heparin was associated with lower mortality (33% vs 52%).

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: In the same Wuhan study, patients with a high sepsis-induced coagulopathy score who received heparin had 40% mortality versus 64% in those who did not.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: Confounding by indication is a key concern in observational anticoagulation data—sicker patients may be more likely to receive heparin, biasing the apparent effect.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: Routine prophylactic anticoagulation is generally not recommended for outpatients being treated for COVID-19 at home.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: In a universal testing study of a NYC pregnancy ward, of 215 women only 4 (about 2%) had symptoms, and of the remaining asymptomatic 211, 29 (about 14%) tested positive—so about 88% of infected patients were asymptomatic at presentation.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: Modeling data suggest that a significant portion of SARS-CoV-2 transmission occurs before symptoms, with an estimated 44% of transmission occurring pre-symptomatically.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: The incubation period of COVID-19 has a median of about 5.2 days.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: The CDC recommends the public wear cloth face masks primarily to protect others, not primarily to protect the wearer.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: In a Nature Medicine study of coronavirus patients, surgical masks reduced detectable virus in aerosols from 40% to zero and in droplets from 30% to zero during breathing and coughing.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: Serological antibody testing (IgM/IgG) should not be used as the sole diagnostic for COVID-19 because antibodies take time to develop and do not necessarily indicate active infection.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: When disease prevalence is low (under ~5%), even a small drop in test specificity can produce more false positives than true positives, limiting the utility of antibody seroprevalence estimates.

episode_title: #211 COVID Cakes: Remdesivir, Anticoagulation, Transmission
statement: A VA study of 368 patients found no evidence of benefit from hydroxychloroquine, with higher death rates in the hydroxychloroquine group versus no hydroxychloroquine.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: No legal method has successfully held a physician or institution liable for not providing futile care, even when withholding it resulted in a patient's death; clinicians are only held to the malpractice standard of care.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Avoid the term 'futile' when discussing end-of-life care; instead use 'potentially inappropriate' and 'medically ineffective.'

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Language is itself an intervention; how options like trach and PEG are framed to families can create unrealistic expectations and prolong suffering.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Eliciting patient/family goals at the outset of an ICU stay allows future discussions to be framed around whether interventions will actually meet those goals.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Out-of-hospital DNR orders often do not carry over into the inpatient setting, so clinicians should advocate to fix these systems gaps.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: When discussing religion in end-of-life care, relate to families using your own experience without imposing your beliefs, or ask about their belief system if you have none to share.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: TEG can help differentiate coagulopathy in liver patients who are simultaneously prone to clotting and bleeding, and using it in the ICU may reduce transfusions while maintaining equivalent outcomes.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Andexanet alfa is a reversal agent for the factor Xa inhibitors rivaroxaban and apixaban; the risk of thrombotic events on it sits in the roughly 18% range.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Idarucizumab is used to reverse dabigatran.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: DOAC reversal agents are extremely expensive—each vial can cost tens to hundreds of thousands of dollars—so their cost-benefit should be weighed, especially in patients unlikely to benefit.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: In massive transfusion, trauma patients get a 1:1:1 ratio while ICU practice tends toward 3:1:1; clinicians should try to keep to reasonable transfusion ratios.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: In the CITRIS-ALI study of vitamin C in acute lung injury, there was no difference in the primary inflammatory endpoints (CRP, modified SOFA) but a 28-day mortality difference of 25% versus 40%; this was a secondary endpoint subject to multiple-comparison error, so it is not ready for primetime.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Vitamin C reduces ferric to ferrous iron, and lower-pH environments like sepsis favor conversion to unavailable ferric iron; IV ascorbic acid may restore ferrous iron for synthesis of sympathomimetic neurotransmitters targeted by vasopressors.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: A JAMA Internal Medicine medical-overuse article recommends against using procalcitonin to diagnose lower respiratory tract infection, since clinician judgment performs about equally for antibiotic use and its sensitivity/specificity is around 75%.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Procalcitonin is often unreliable in the medical ICU—it can be elevated without infection (e.g., renal failure) or low with clear infection—so it has less utility in that population.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Cystic fibrosis patients have chronic bronchial colonization that keeps procalcitonin mildly elevated, limiting its usefulness for diagnosing pulmonary exacerbations.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: A January 2018 study showed a procalcitonin-guided strategy had a lower rate of superinfection with ventilator-associated pneumonia (12.5%) compared with standard therapy (26.9%).

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Procalcitonin should be treated as one additional data point alongside clinical acumen, white count, fever, and peripheral perfusion rather than being relied on in isolation.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: Learners retain only about 10% of material within 20 minutes of a lecture; spaced, spontaneous re-exposure improves long-term retention (the forgetting curve).

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: A poster correlated PM 2.5 fine particulate matter with age-adjusted non-smoking-related lung cancer incidence and mortality, with r-squared values of 0.27 for incidence and 0.314 for mortality.

episode_title: #180 CHEST 2019 Recap Part 2 from New Orleans
statement: A South Korea study measuring air pollution over a year correlated it with acute exacerbations of IPF, supporting that even air pollution injury can trigger ILD exacerbations.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: The pooled cohort equation estimates 10-year risk for both coronary events and stroke, and is best calibrated for non-Hispanic whites and blacks living in the US, so it may not predict well for other races/ethnicities.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: The pooled cohort equation input variables are age, sex, race, total and HDL cholesterol, systolic blood pressure, use of blood pressure medications, smoking, and diabetes.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: The 10-year risk assessment is designed for primary prevention in individuals aged 40 to 75 without diabetes with LDL 70 to 189; patients with diabetes or LDL above 190 are recommended for statins regardless of the risk score.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: The four risk bins are: low risk (<5%), borderline (5 to 7.5%), intermediate (7.5 to 19.9%), and high risk (>20%); above 7.5% is a statin benefit group.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: When risk remains uncertain after calculating 10-year risk and considering risk-enhancing factors for intermediate or select borderline patients, a coronary artery calcium score can further refine risk upward or downward to guide statin decisions.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: A heart-healthy diet rich in fruits and vegetables, low in saturated fats and refined carbohydrates, with polyunsaturated fats, lean/plant protein, nuts, phytosterols, and high-fiber foods can reduce LDL by up to 30 to 40 mg/dL.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: Persistently elevated triglycerides above 175 non-fasting on at least three occasions is a risk enhancer, as are elevated CRP above 2, elevated lipoprotein(a), and elevated apoB if measured.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: Lipoprotein(a) is genetically inherited, so it only needs to be measured once to assess risk; it is worth checking in patients with a strong family history or premature coronary disease.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: The LDL from a standard lipid panel uses the Friedewald equation and does not work well at very low LDL extremes, so apoB may better capture increased atherogenic particles.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: A coronary calcium score of zero is one of the strongest negative risk factors, with about a 0.1% per year event rate, and can shift a patient's risk downward enough to defer statin therapy.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: In randomized trials, every 1 mmol/L (39 mg/dL) reduction in LDL yields about a 22% relative risk reduction in cardiovascular events and about a 10% reduction in mortality, with higher-risk patients deriving more net benefit.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: High-intensity statins reduce LDL by about 50%, moderate-intensity by at least 30%, and low-intensity by less than 30%; high-intensity is recommended for secondary prevention under age 75, LDL above 190, 10-year risk over 20%, and diabetes with multiple risk factors.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: Coronary calcium scores above 300 have event rates similar to a stable secondary prevention population, so those patients can be treated like secondary prevention.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: For statin-associated muscle symptoms, re-challenge with the same statin at a lower dose; simvastatin and lovastatin seem to cause more muscle symptoms while pravastatin and rosuvastatin cause somewhat less.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: In REDUCE-IT, icosapent ethyl (4 g/day of purified EPA) reduced major cardiovascular events by 25% in statin-treated patients, and this benefit was independent of baseline or achieved triglyceride levels.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: The REDUCE-IT results with prescription icosapent ethyl should not be extrapolated to over-the-counter dietary fish oil supplements, which are a mixed bag of DHA and EPA and may contain saturated and oxidized fats.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: The 2018 guidelines use LDL thresholds (not targets) such as above 70 to add non-statin therapy, mirroring trial enrollment criteria; the approach is risk-based rather than treating to a specific number.

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD
statement: Total atherosclerotic plaque burden depends not only on the magnitude of LDL elevation but also the duration of exposure, supporting earlier treatment and primordial prevention.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: A normal procalcitonin should not prevent you from starting antibiotics in a patient with pneumonia, as it is not sensitive enough.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The 2019 CAP guidelines recommend against routine anaerobic coverage for aspiration pneumonia, as many events are aspiration pneumonitis and don't necessarily require antibiotics.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The anaerobic antibiotic often added for aspiration (clindamycin) is very C. diff-unfriendly, another reason to avoid it.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The concept of healthcare-associated pneumonia (HCAP) has been abandoned; instead, consider individual risk factors for MRSA and Pseudomonas.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The best predictors of MRSA or Pseudomonas pneumonia are prior culture of these organisms or recent hospitalization with parenteral antibiotic exposure; such patients warrant blood and sputum cultures.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: A negative MRSA nasal swab is a good way to rapidly determine a patient probably does not need MRSA coverage, even before sputum or blood cultures return.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: A positive MRSA swab does not obligate continuing anti-MRSA therapy because its positive predictive value is not great; use clinical judgment based on severity and features like cavitation or necrotizing pneumonia.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: For outpatients with comorbidities, the CAP guidelines permit monotherapy with a respiratory fluoroquinolone, though other effective alternatives exist given fluoroquinolone black box warnings.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: Comorbidities that upgrade outpatient CAP antibiotic choice include chronic heart, lung, liver, or renal disease, diabetes, alcohol use disorder, cancer, or asplenia.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The CAP guidelines recommend corticosteroids only for refractory shock and not routinely for pneumonia.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The 2017 Cochrane review found corticosteroids reduced mortality in severe CAP with a number needed to treat of 18 to prevent one death, but the evidence was low quality due to study design issues.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The updated GINA asthma strategy allows inhaled corticosteroid/formoterol (ICS-LABA) to be used for both maintenance and rescue, even in mild asthma.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The GINA strategy favors formoterol over salmeterol because formoterol has a rapid onset (about 5 minutes, similar to albuterol) whereas salmeterol's onset is longer (1-3 hours).

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The budesonide-formoterol formulation available in the US (4.5/160) differs from that used in the SYGMA trials (6/200) and is not FDA approved for PRN use.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: Reliance on short-acting beta agonists in asthma may lead to increased exacerbation risk, decreased lung function, and increased airway inflammation, supporting inhaled corticosteroid use even in mild disease.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: In SYGMA trials, as-needed budesonide-formoterol was superior to as-needed short-acting beta agonist but did not demonstrate non-inferiority to scheduled budesonide for symptom control.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: As-needed budesonide-formoterol delivered less than 20% of the steroid dose compared to daily budesonide, offering much lower cumulative steroid exposure.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The trade-off of as-needed versus scheduled budesonide was only 3.3% fewer weeks of well-controlled asthma (about 12 days per year), with equivalent severe exacerbation rates and much lower steroid exposure.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The red/processed meat systematic review used cohort (observational) studies, which by GRADE start at low certainty of evidence and can only be downgraded.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The meat guideline concluded there is low to very low certainty evidence that reducing unprocessed red meat by three servings per week is associated with a very small reduction in cardiovascular mortality, stroke, MI, and type 2 diabetes.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The meat guideline recommendations advised adults to continue their current unprocessed and processed meat consumption, but these were weak recommendations based on low-certainty evidence.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: The meat guideline reported population-level outcomes (e.g., 4 fewer cardiovascular deaths and 6 fewer type 2 diabetes cases per 1000) but framed its recommendations toward individuals, without accounting for animal welfare or environmental impact.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: Even small per-person effects of dietary changes can accrue to large population-level benefits, making population-level recommendations worthwhile despite modest individual effect sizes.

episode_title: #185 Hotcakes: CAP, Asthma & Red Meat
statement: When critically appraising any study, start with three foundational questions: what is the basic question being answered, what was the comparison and how were groups defined, and what were the main results.

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
statement: In hyperkalemia, look for reversible causes such as urinary obstruction and hyperglycemia, and preferentially use the kidneys (diuretics) rather than the gut to eliminate potassium.

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
statement: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (SPS) can rarely cause colonic necrosis, so avoid it in patients with a sick gut or bowel obstruction, though the overall risk is low (cited ~0.15%).

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
statement: EKG changes are an insensitive marker of hyperkalemia, often not appearing until potassium is in the 8s and 9s, so treat based on the potassium level rather than relying on the EKG.

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
statement: Calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane but does not lower potassium; it lasts only about 15-30 minutes and should be re-dosed until EKG changes resolve, with additional therapy to shift and remove potassium.

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
statement: Albuterol shifts potassium intracellularly but requires surprisingly high doses and the effect is only temporary.

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
statement: When managing older patients with multimorbidity, consider the 'time to benefit' of a therapy relative to the patient's life expectancy to decide whether it is worthwhile.

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
statement: Framing therapy decisions using number needed to treat, time to benefit, and number needed to harm helps patients understand limited benefit and supports deprescribing rather than medical inertia.

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
statement: To reduce degradation of buprenorphine film when a patient takes a partial dose, instruct them to keep the film in the foil while cutting it.

episode_title: #190 Recap Extravaganza 2019 – The Curbsiders
